questions: Which anticoagulant is used in a lavender-top tube?
EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) is the anticoagulant in lavender/purple-top tubes, used for CBC and most hematology tests. It chelates calcium, preventing clot formation.
What is the primary function of EDTA in blood collection?
EDTA chelates free calcium ions (Ca²⁺), which are essential cofactors in multiple steps of the coagulation cascade; without free calcium, clot formation is blocked (Tietz, 8th ed.).
Which tube is used for complete blood count (CBC)?
The lavender-top tube contains K₂EDTA or K₃EDTA, the anticoagulant of choice for CBC, as it best preserves cellular morphology and prevents platelet clumping.
What is the normal range for haemoglobin in adult males?
WHO and Tietz reference ranges for adult males: Hgb 13.5–17.5 g/dL. Values <13.5 g/dL define anaemia in males; >17.5 g/dL suggest polycythaemia.
A patient has a low MCV. This indicates:
MCV (Mean Corpuscular Volume) reflects average RBC size. Low MCV (<80 fL) indicates microcytic anaemia (iron deficiency, thalassaemia, anaemia of chronic disease). Macrocytic = high MCV (>100 fL).
Which of the following is a common cause of microcytic anemia?

Iron deficiency is the most common cause of microcytic (low MCV), hypochromic anaemia worldwide. B12 and folate deficiencies cause macrocytic anaemia; haemolysis typically causes normocytic anaemia (Rodak's Hematology).
What does RDW measure?
RDW (Red cell Distribution Width) is a coefficient of variation of RBC volume, reflecting anisocytosis — the degree of variation in red cell size. Normal RDW-CV: 11.5–14.5%.
A high RDW is commonly seen in:

Iron deficiency anaemia produces mixed-sized cells (early normal-sized, later microcytic), causing high RDW. Thalassaemia trait characteristically shows normal or minimally elevated RDW despite low MCV — a key differentiating feature (Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
Which of the following is a nucleated red blood cell?

The normoblast (orthochromatic erythroblast) is the late nucleated precursor in erythropoiesis, found in bone marrow. Reticulocytes are anucleate (nucleus extruded) but retain residual RNA. Mature erythrocytes are fully anucleate.
What is the normal range for white blood cell (WBC) count?
Normal adult WBC reference range is 4,000–11,000/mm³ (4.0–11.0 × 10⁹/L). Leukopenia <4,000; leukocytosis >11,000 (Tietz Fundamentals of Clinical Chemistry).
A patient has a 'left shift' on differential. This means:

A 'left shift' refers to the appearance of immature neutrophil precursors (bands, metamyelocytes) in peripheral blood, typically indicating bacterial infection or systemic inflammation — the marrow is releasing cells early.
Which WBC is involved in allergic reactions and parasitic infections?

Eosinophils contain granules rich in major basic protein and eosinophil cationic protein, targeting IgE-mediated allergic responses and helminthic parasites. Eosinophilia (>500/mm³) is a hallmark of both conditions.
Which WBC is the first responder in bacterial infections?

Neutrophils (PMNs) are the predominant first-line phagocytic cells, comprising 50–70% of circulating WBCs. They are rapidly recruited to sites of bacterial infection via chemokines and opsonization within minutes to hours.
What is the normal range for platelets?
Standard reference range for platelets is 150,000–400,000/mm³; among the options given, 100,000–300,000/mm³ best approximates this. Thrombocytopenia <100,000; thrombocytosis >400,000 (Tietz).
A patient with a platelet count of 20,000/mm³ is at risk for:
Severe thrombocytopenia (<50,000/mm³) carries significant bleeding risk; spontaneous bleeding (petechiae, mucosal haemorrhage, intracranial haemorrhage) is particularly likely below 20,000/mm³.
Which tube is used for prothrombin time (PT)?

The light blue-top tube contains 3.2% sodium citrate (9:1 blood-to-anticoagulant ratio), the standard anticoagulant for all coagulation studies (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen), as citrate is fully reversible with calcium recalcification.
What does PT/INR measure?

PT measures the extrinsic (tissue factor) and common pathways of coagulation (Factors VII, X, V, II, I). INR standardises PT results across laboratories using the International Sensitivity Index (ISI).
What is the normal INR for a patient not on warfarin?
Normal INR for healthy individuals not on anticoagulation is approximately 0.8–1.2. Among the options, 1.0–2.0 best contains this range. Therapeutic INR for warfarin is typically 2.0–3.0.
Which anticoagulant is monitored using aPTT?
Unfractionated heparin (UFH) is monitored using aPTT. Heparin potentiates antithrombin III, inhibiting thrombin and Factor Xa (intrinsic pathway), prolonging aPTT. Warfarin is monitored by PT/INR.
What is the normal aPTT range?

Normal aPTT is 25–35 seconds (reagent-dependent). Therapeutic aPTT for heparin therapy is 1.5–2.5× the control (typically 60–80 seconds). Values >35 s (unexplained) indicate intrinsic pathway deficiency or anticoagulant effect.
Which of the following is a clotting factor?

Fibrinogen is Factor I in the coagulation cascade — the soluble plasma glycoprotein converted to insoluble fibrin by thrombin (Factor IIa), forming the structural scaffold of a blood clot. Normal fibrinogen: 200–400 mg/dL.
What is the end product of the coagulation cascade?

The terminal step of the coagulation cascade is the conversion of soluble fibrinogen to insoluble fibrin monomers by thrombin, which are then cross-linked by Factor XIIIa (stabilised fibrin) to form a durable clot.
Which test is used to diagnose DIC?

DIC (Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation) is diagnosed by a panel: prolonged PT/aPTT, decreased fibrinogen (<150 mg/dL), elevated D-dimer/FDPs, thrombocytopenia, and schistocytes on smear (ISTH scoring criteria).
A patient has a high D-dimer. This indicates:

D-dimer is a fibrin degradation product generated when plasmin cleaves cross-linked fibrin. Elevated D-dimer confirms active fibrinolysis, seen in DVT, PE, DIC, and post-surgery. It has high sensitivity but low specificity.
Which of the following is a direct anticoagulant?

In classical pharmacology, heparin is a direct anticoagulant — it acts immediately in circulation by activating antithrombin III. Warfarin is indirect (inhibits hepatic Vitamin K-dependent synthesis). Aspirin is antiplatelet, not anticoagulant.
What is the primary function of fibrinogen?

Fibrinogen (Factor I) is a 340 kDa plasma glycoprotein synthesised by the liver. It is cleaved by thrombin to fibrin monomers, which polymerise and are cross-linked by Factor XIIIa to form a stable haemostatic plug.
Which test is used to monitor heparin therapy?

Unfractionated heparin (UFH) is monitored by aPTT (target: 1.5–2.5× baseline). PT/INR monitors warfarin. Platelet count monitors for HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia). Anti-Xa assay is used for LMWH.
Which of the following cells is a precursor to mature erythrocytes and is normally found only in bone marrow?

The normoblast (late erythroblast) is a nucleated RBC precursor normally confined to bone marrow. Its appearance in peripheral blood (leukoerythroblastosis) indicates severe marrow stress. Reticulocytes are released normally; erythrocytes are fully mature.
Which of the following is a clotting factor deficiency?

Haemophilia A is a hereditary X-linked deficiency of Factor VIII, leading to impaired intrinsic pathway coagulation and recurrent bleeding into joints/muscles. Sickle cell and thalassaemia are haemoglobinopathies; G6PD is an enzyme deficiency.
What is the normal glucose level in fasting blood?
ADA and WHO define normal fasting plasma glucose as 70–99 mg/dL (3.9–5.5 mmol/L). Impaired fasting glucose: 100–125 mg/dL; Diabetes: ≥126 mg/dL on two occasions (ADA Standards of Medical Care).
Which test is used to diagnose diabetes mellitus?

ADA diagnostic criteria for diabetes include: FPG ≥126 mg/dL, 2-hr OGTT ≥200 mg/dL, HbA1c ≥6.5%, or symptomatic hyperglycaemia with random glucose ≥200 mg/dL. HbA1c is preferred for its convenience and non-fasting requirement.
What is the diagnostic threshold for HbA1c in diabetes?

Per ADA 2023 guidelines: HbA1c <5.7% = normal; 5.7–6.4% = prediabetes; ≥6.5% = diabetes. The 6.5% threshold represents the value above which diabetic retinopathy risk increases significantly (ADA Standards of Medical Care).
A patient has elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST). This suggests:

ALT and AST are intracellular hepatic enzymes released into circulation upon hepatocyte injury or necrosis (hepatitis, cirrhosis, drug toxicity). While AST can also rise in myocardial infarction, the combination of ALT + AST elevation primarily indicates hepatocellular damage.
Which enzyme is most specific for liver injury?

ALT (alanine aminotransferase) is predominantly hepatocyte-specific, making it the most specific marker for hepatocellular injury. AST is also found in cardiac muscle, skeletal muscle, and RBCs. ALP and GGT reflect cholestasis (Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
Which enzyme is elevated in bone disease?

ALP (alkaline phosphatase) is elevated in both liver (cholestasis) and bone disease (Paget's, osteosarcoma, hyperparathyroidism, bone metastases) because both osteoblasts and hepatocytes are rich sources. Bone-specific ALP isoenzyme distinguishes the two.
What is the normal range for creatinine in adult males?
Normal serum creatinine in adult males: 0.7–1.3 mg/dL (62–115 µmol/L); in females: 0.5–1.1 mg/dL, reflecting lower muscle mass. Creatinine is the most widely used screening marker for GFR (Tietz).
Elevated BUN and creatinine suggest:

Both BUN (blood urea nitrogen) and creatinine are renally cleared. Proportional elevation of both indicates reduced GFR due to intrinsic renal disease (azotaemia). Isolated BUN elevation with normal creatinine may indicate pre-renal causes or high protein catabolism.
What is the normal BUN/creatinine ratio?

Normal BUN/creatinine ratio is 10:1–20:1 (commonly cited as ~15–20:1). A ratio >20:1 suggests pre-renal azotaemia (dehydration, GI bleed, high protein diet). A ratio <10:1 suggests intrinsic renal disease or low protein intake (Tietz).
Which of the following is a renal function test?

GFR (Glomerular Filtration Rate) is the gold standard measure of kidney function, estimated via CKD-EPI or MDRD equations using serum creatinine, age, sex, and race. ALT/AST are hepatic; amylase is pancreatic.
What is the best estimate of glomerular filtration rate (GFR)?

Creatinine clearance (24-hour urine) and eGFR (CKD-EPI equation) are the standard methods for estimating GFR. Serum creatinine alone underestimates loss of renal function until ~50% nephron loss has occurred (KDIGO guidelines).
A patient has proteinuria. This is most commonly associated with:

Proteinuria reflects disruption of the glomerular filtration barrier (glomerulonephritis, diabetic nephropathy, nephrotic syndrome) or tubular dysfunction (Fanconi syndrome). It is a cardinal marker of kidney disease and predicts CKD progression.
What is the normal urine pH?

Normal urine pH ranges from 4.5–8.0, reflecting the kidney's ability to excrete both acid and base loads. Average first-morning urine pH is ~5.5–6.0. The broad range (4.5–8.0) is the accepted clinical reference (Tietz, Henry's).
Which of the following is a normal finding in urine?

Trace urobilinogen (0.1–1.0 Ehrlich units/dL) is normal in urine — it is the product of intestinal bacterial reduction of bilirubin, partially reabsorbed and excreted by the kidney. Glucose, bilirubin, and ketones are abnormal findings.
Glucosuria is most commonly due to:

Glucosuria occurs when plasma glucose exceeds the renal threshold (~180 mg/dL), overwhelming the tubular glucose transport capacity (SGLT2). The most common cause is hyperglycaemia due to uncontrolled diabetes mellitus (ADA).
Ketones in urine are commonly seen in:

Ketonuria results from increased fatty acid oxidation producing acetone, acetoacetate, and β-hydroxybutyrate, seen in starvation (glucose unavailable), DKA (insulin deficiency prevents glucose uptake), and prolonged low-carbohydrate diets.
What is the normal specific gravity of urine?

Normal urine specific gravity is 1.005–1.030, reflecting the kidney's concentrating ability. SG <1.005 suggests diabetes insipidus or overhydration; SG consistently ~1.010 (isosthenuria) indicates loss of concentrating/diluting capacity in CKD.
Haematuria in urine can be caused by all EXCEPT:

Dehydration does not cause haematuria; it may concentrate urine, producing dark colour, but RBCs are not released. UTI, nephrolithiasis, glomerulonephritis, trauma, and menstrual contamination are all recognised causes of haematuria (Henry's).
What is the normal colour of urine?

Normal urine colour ranges from pale yellow (dilute) to amber (concentrated), due to urochrome pigment (urobilin). Dark brown suggests bilirubinuria or myoglobinuria; red/pink indicates haematuria or haemoglobinuria.
Which of the following is a cast in urine?
Urinary casts are cylindrical structures formed in the renal tubular lumen from Tamm-Horsfall protein. Hyaline casts are the most common cast type. Squamous cells, bacteria, and mucus are not casts.
Hyaline casts are commonly seen in:

Hyaline casts (pure Tamm-Horsfall mucoprotein, no cellular inclusions) are found in concentrated urine from dehydration, fever, strenuous exercise, or diuretic use — they are non-pathological in small numbers. Cellular casts (RBC, WBC, granular) indicate pathology.
Which of the following is a gram-positive bacterium?

Staphylococcus aureus is a Gram-positive coccus with a thick peptidoglycan cell wall retaining crystal violet. E. coli and Pseudomonas are Gram-negative rods; Neisseria gonorrhoeae is a Gram-negative diplococcus.
Which stain is used to identify acid-fast bacteria?

The Ziehl-Neelsen (ZN) stain uses carbol fuchsin with heat, followed by acid-alcohol decolorisation. Acid-fast bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis, M. leprae) resist decolourisation due to their mycolic acid-rich cell wall, appearing red on a blue background.
A patient has a positive blood culture with Staphylococcus aureus. This is:

S. aureus is never considered a contaminant in blood culture — a single positive bottle is clinically significant. It is a virulent pathogen causing bacteraemia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, and sepsis. Coagulase-negative staphylococci are more commonly contaminants.
Which medium is used to culture blood?

Blood agar (5% sheep blood in tryptic soy agar) is the standard non-selective medium used for blood culture subcultures, allowing growth of most aerobic organisms and demonstrating haemolysis patterns (alpha, beta, gamma).
Which medium is selective for gram-negative bacteria?

MacConkey agar contains bile salts and crystal violet, inhibiting Gram-positive organisms. It also contains lactose + neutral red indicator to differentiate lactose fermenters (pink colonies, e.g., E. coli) from non-fermenters (colourless, e.g., Salmonella).
Which medium is selective for Staphylococcus aureus?

Mannitol Salt Agar (MSA) contains 7.5% NaCl (inhibiting most organisms except salt-tolerant staphylococci) and mannitol with phenol red indicator. S. aureus ferments mannitol, producing yellow colonies. S. epidermidis grows but does not ferment mannitol.
A patient has a urinary tract infection. The most common causative organism is:

E. coli accounts for ~80% of community-acquired UTIs due to its virulence factors (type 1 fimbriae, P-fimbriae, haemolysin) enabling uroepithelial adhesion. Diagnosis requires ≥10⁵ CFU/mL on a clean-catch midstream urine culture (IDSA guidelines).
Which test is used to identify E. coli?

E. coli is indole-positive (produces indole from tryptophan via tryptophanase — detected by Kovac's reagent turning red/pink). The indole test is a key biochemical test distinguishing E. coli from other Enterobacteriaceae like Klebsiella (indole-negative).
Which of the following is a fungal infection?

Candidiasis is caused by Candida species (a yeast/dimorphic fungus), most commonly C. albicans. TB is mycobacterial; syphilis is caused by Treponema pallidum (spirochaete); gonorrhoea by Neisseria gonorrhoeae (Gram-negative diplococcus).
Which stain is used to identify fungi in a specimen?

Calcofluor white (fluorescent optical brightener) binds chitin and cellulose in fungal cell walls and fluoresces bright blue-white under UV/fluorescence microscopy, making it the most sensitive stain for detecting fungi and Pneumocystis jirovecii in clinical specimens.
Which anticoagulant chelates calcium ions to prevent coagulation?

EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) is a true chelating agent that tightly binds Ca²⁺ through multiple coordination bonds, removing it from solution. Sodium citrate also binds calcium (reversible complex), but EDTA is the classical chelator. Heparin acts via antithrombin III.
Which of the following is a parasitic infection?

Malaria is caused by the protozoan parasite Plasmodium (P. falciparum, P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae, P. knowlesi), transmitted by the Anopheles mosquito. TB is bacterial; Hepatitis B is viral; Influenza is viral.
Malaria is diagnosed by:

The WHO gold standard for malaria diagnosis is the Giemsa-stained thick and thin peripheral blood smear. Thick smears increase parasite detection sensitivity; thin smears allow species identification and parasite density quantification. RDTs and PCR are adjuncts.
Which of the following is a viral infection?

Hepatitis C is caused by HCV, a single-stranded RNA flavivirus. Syphilis is caused by Treponema pallidum (spirochaete); gonorrhoea by Neisseria gonorrhoeae (bacterium); TB by Mycobacterium tuberculosis (acid-fast bacterium).
Which test is used to detect HIV antibodies?

Fourth-generation ELISA (enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay) detects both HIV-1/2 antibodies AND p24 antigen, enabling earlier detection (window period ~18 days). Positive results are confirmed by Western blot or HIV-1/2 differentiation immunoassay (WHO/CDC algorithm).
Which of the following best describes the principle of the Westergren method for ESR?

The Westergren ESR method measures the distance (in mm) that RBCs sediment in anticoagulated blood in a standardised 200 mm vertical tube over exactly 1 hour. Increased rouleaux formation (due to acute-phase proteins like fibrinogen) accelerates sedimentation.
An RDW value of 18% (reference: 11.5-14.5%) most likely indicates:

RDW-CV of 18% (well above the 14.5% upper limit) indicates marked anisocytosis — significant heterogeneity in RBC volume. This is characteristically seen in iron deficiency anaemia, vitamin B12/folate deficiency, and mixed nutritional deficiencies, not in haemoglobin or platelet disorders.
Which of the following is a tumor marker for prostate cancer?

PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen) is a serine protease produced almost exclusively by prostatic epithelium. Elevated PSA (>4 ng/mL) is associated with prostate cancer, BPH, and prostatitis. CA-125 = ovarian cancer; AFP = hepatocellular/germ cell tumours; CEA = colorectal cancer.
Which tumor marker is elevated in ovarian cancer?

CA-125 (Cancer Antigen 125) is elevated in ~80% of advanced ovarian cancers (epithelial type) and is used for monitoring treatment response and detecting recurrence. It is not specific (also elevated in endometriosis, PID, peritoneal disease).
What is the primary function of complement system?

The complement system (C1–C9) serves three main functions: opsonisation (C3b coating pathogens for phagocytosis), membrane attack complex (MAC, C5b-9) formation causing cell lysis, and inflammation/chemotaxis (C3a, C5a anaphylatoxins) (Janeway's Immunobiology).
Which of the following is a primary immunodeficiency?

DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion) is a primary immunodeficiency caused by thymic aplasia/hypoplasia, resulting in T-cell deficiency. HIV causes secondary (acquired) immunodeficiency; lupus and RA are autoimmune diseases.
What is the most common blood type in the ABO system?

Blood group O is the most common globally (~44% worldwide), though frequency varies by population. Blood group A is most common in some European populations. AB is the least common. O individuals have neither A nor B antigens but carry both anti-A and anti-B antibodies.
Which blood type is the universal donor?

O-negative (O Rh-negative) red cells lack ABO antigens and the Rh(D) antigen, minimising the risk of alloimmune haemolytic transfusion reactions in unmatched emergency transfusions. In practice, full compatibility testing is always preferred when time permits.
Which blood type is the universal recipient?

AB-positive individuals have A and B antigens on their RBCs and no corresponding anti-A or anti-B antibodies in plasma, so they can receive ABO-compatible RBCs of any type. They also carry the Rh(D) antigen, tolerating Rh-positive blood.
A patient with type A blood has which antibodies?

By Landsteiner's law, ABO antibodies form naturally against absent antigens. Blood group A individuals have A antigens on RBCs and naturally occurring anti-B IgM antibodies in plasma. Transfusing B or AB blood to a type A patient causes haemolysis.
A patient with type AB blood can receive blood from:

AB individuals have both A and B antigens and no anti-A or anti-B antibodies, so they can receive RBCs from all ABO groups (A, B, AB, O) without ABO incompatibility. However, Rh compatibility and full crossmatch are still required.
What is the purpose of crossmatching?

Crossmatching tests donor red cells against recipient serum (major crossmatch) and donor serum against recipient cells (minor crossmatch) to detect ABO incompatibility, unexpected alloantibodies, and other clinically significant antibodies that could cause haemolytic transfusion reactions.
What is haemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN) caused by?

HDN can result from both ABO incompatibility (usually mild, first-born affected) and Rh incompatibility (more severe, subsequent pregnancies). Rh HDN occurs when an Rh-negative mother develops anti-D antibodies against an Rh-positive foetus (Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
A mother is Rh-negative and the baby is Rh-positive. What should be given to prevent HDN?

Rh immune globulin (RhoGAM, anti-D IgG) suppresses the maternal immune response to foetal Rh(D)-positive RBCs by coating and clearing them before maternal sensitisation occurs, preventing alloimmunisation and protecting future pregnancies.
When should RhoGAM be administered to a Rh-negative mother?

Standard protocol: 300 µg RhoGAM at 28 weeks gestation (antenatal prophylaxis) and within 72 hours of delivery of an Rh-positive infant (postnatal prophylaxis). Also given after any sensitising event (miscarriage, amniocentesis, trauma) in Rh-negative mothers.
What is the normal pH of arterial blood?

Normal arterial blood pH is 7.35–7.45 (hydrogen ion concentration ~35–45 nmol/L). pH <7.35 = acidaemia; pH >7.45 = alkalaemia. The body maintains this narrow range through respiratory (CO₂) and renal (HCO₃⁻) buffer systems.
Which practice minimizes contamination during blood culture collection?

Chlorhexidine gluconate + isopropyl alcohol (ChloraPrep) is the current gold standard for blood culture skin antisepsis — it reduces contamination rates significantly more than 70% alcohol or povidone-iodine alone (CLSI BP Blood Culture Collection guidelines).
What does PaO₂ measure?

PaO₂ (partial pressure of oxygen in arterial blood) reflects dissolved O₂ in plasma and the adequacy of pulmonary gas exchange. Normal PaO₂ on room air: 80–100 mmHg. PaCO₂ measures CO₂ partial pressure (normal: 35–45 mmHg).
A normal PaO₂ on room air is:

Normal PaO₂ breathing room air (FiO₂ 21%) is 80–100 mmHg. PaO₂ <80 mmHg = mild hypoxaemia; <60 mmHg = significant hypoxaemia (O₂ saturation falls steeply below 60 mmHg on the O₂-Hgb dissociation curve); <40 mmHg = severe hypoxaemia.
What does HCO₃⁻ represent in blood gas analysis?

HCO₃⁻ (bicarbonate) is the primary metabolic buffer of blood, regulated by the kidneys. Normal: 22–26 mEq/L. In acid-base interpretation, HCO₃⁻ abnormalities indicate metabolic disorders; PaCO₂ abnormalities indicate respiratory disorders.
In which condition is RDW typically normal despite anemia?

Thalassaemia trait produces uniformly small (microcytic) RBCs due to balanced globin chain reduction, resulting in homogeneous microcytosis and a normal or minimally elevated RDW. This distinguishes it from iron deficiency anaemia, which shows heterogeneous cell sizes (high RDW).
Hemolysis in a serum sample will most significantly interfere with the assay for:

RBCs contain approximately 23× more potassium than plasma. Haemolysis releases intracellular K⁺ into serum, causing marked pseudohyperkalaemia (falsely elevated serum K⁺). This is a critical pre-analytical error that can lead to inappropriate clinical decisions (Henry's).
Which of the following is a tumor marker for liver cancer?

AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) is the primary tumour marker for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and non-seminomatous germ cell tumours. AFP >400 ng/mL in a cirrhotic patient is diagnostic of HCC per AASLD criteria. CA-19-9 = pancreatic cancer; PSA = prostate cancer.
Which of the following is a quality control measure in the lab?

Running known QC materials (with assigned mean and SD) alongside patient samples detects systematic and random analytical errors. This is the foundation of internal quality control (IQC) per Westgard rules and ISO 15189 accreditation standards.
What is the purpose of a Levey-Jennings chart?

The Levey-Jennings chart plots daily QC results against time, with lines at the mean ±1s, ±2s, and ±3s. It visually reveals shifts, trends, and random errors, enabling application of Westgard rules to assess statistical control of an analytical method.
Which analyte is most stable in a serum sample stored at room temperature for 24 hours?

Creatinine is chemically stable in serum at room temperature for 24–48 hours. Glucose decreases due to cellular glycolysis (~5–7% per hour); potassium increases from cellular leakage; LDH is temperature-sensitive and unstable at room temperature (CLSI EP7).
What is the best way to prevent haemolysis during blood draw?

Haemolysis is prevented by using an appropriate-gauge needle (≥21G), gentle inversion of tubes (not shaking), avoiding excessive suction, not drawing through IV lines, and releasing the tourniquet before drawing. Small needles create high shear stress on RBCs.
A blood sample left uncentrifuged at room temperature for 4 hours will show a falsely decreased:

Cellular glycolysis continues in uncentrifuged whole blood — RBCs and WBCs consume glucose at ~10–12 mg/dL per hour at room temperature. After 4 hours, glucose can fall by ~40–50 mg/dL, causing a clinically significant false decrease. Sodium fluoride/oxalate tubes inhibit this.
What is the first action if a lab worker gets a needlestick injury?

Per CDC/WHO guidelines, the immediate first step after a needlestick injury is thorough washing with soap and running water for at least 15 minutes. This is followed by reporting to occupational health, blood-borne pathogen risk assessment, and post-exposure prophylaxis if indicated.
Which of the following is a Class I biological safety cabinet?

Class I BSC provides inward airflow (protecting the worker) and HEPA-filtered exhaust (protecting the environment), but does NOT protect the sample from contamination. Class II BSCs provide additional product protection; Class III provides maximum containment for BSL-4 agents.
What is the purpose of autoclaving?

Autoclaving achieves sterilisation using saturated steam at 121°C under 15 psi for 15–20 minutes (or 134°C for 3 minutes), denaturing proteins and destroying all microbial life including spores. It is the gold standard moist heat sterilisation method.
Which of the following is a centrifuge safety rule?

Proper tube balance is critical in centrifuge safety — unbalanced rotors cause vibration, mechanical failure, and aerosol generation if tubes crack. Balanced tubes of equal volume and weight must be placed opposite each other. Never open the lid during a run.
What is the normal reticulocyte count?

Normal reticulocyte count is 0.5–2.0% of RBCs (absolute count: 25,000–85,000/µL). In anaemia, the corrected reticulocyte index (CRI) adjusts for the degree of anaemia to assess true marrow erythropoietic activity.
A high reticulocyte count indicates:

Reticulocytosis (>2%) reflects the bone marrow's compensatory response to increased RBC demand — seen in haemolytic anaemia (RBC destruction), haemorrhage, or recovery from iron/B12 deficiency treatment. It confirms intact erythropoietic capacity (Rodak's Hematology).
In a Gram stain, Gram-positive bacteria appear:

Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan cell wall (~20–80 nm) that retains the crystal violet-iodine complex after acetone-alcohol decolourisation, appearing purple. Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane, losing crystal violet and staining pink with safranin counterstain.

questions: Which of the following stains is used to detect reticulocytes in blood smears?

New methylene blue (NMB) is a supravital stain that precipitates residual ribosomal RNA in reticulocytes as dark-blue granules/filaments, allowing their identification and counting. Giemsa and Wright stains are Romanowsky stains for morphology; Gram stain is for bacteria.
What is the most specific marker for hemolysis?

Decreased haptoglobin is the most specific single marker for intravascular haemolysis. Free haemoglobin released from lysed RBCs binds haptoglobin (a scavenger protein), consuming it rapidly. LDH is non-specific (elevated in many tissue injuries); bilirubin rises in haemolysis and liver disease; schistocytes indicate microangiopathic haemolysis specifically (Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
What is the expected RBC morphology in a patient with iron deficiency anemia?

Iron deficiency impairs haem synthesis, reducing haemoglobin content per cell (hypochromia: MCH <27 pg, MCHC <32 g/dL) and cell size (microcytosis: MCV <80 fL). The peripheral smear shows small, pale cells with increased central pallor (>1/3 of cell diameter) (Rodak's Hematology).
Which of the following coagulation factors is not synthesized in the liver?

Factor VIII is unique among coagulation factors in that it is primarily synthesised by vascular endothelial cells and sinusoidal cells, not hepatocytes. All Vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) and Factors I, V, XI, XII, XIII are hepatically synthesised. This is why Factor VIII is relatively preserved in liver failure compared to other factors.
Which test is used to confirm the diagnosis of hereditary spherocytosis?

The osmotic fragility test is the classical confirmatory test for hereditary spherocytosis (HS). Spherocytes, lacking the normal biconcave disc shape and membrane redundancy, lyse in higher concentrations of hypotonic saline than normal RBCs. The EMA (eosin-5'-maleimide) binding flow cytometry test is the modern gold standard, but among these options, osmotic fragility is the classic answer. CD55/CD59 flow cytometry and Ham's test diagnose PNH; Coombs test diagnoses autoimmune haemolytic anaemia.
What is the normal range for mean corpuscular volume (MCV) in adults?

Normal adult MCV is 80–100 fL (femtolitres) per standard Tietz and WHO reference ranges. MCV <80 fL = microcytosis; MCV >100 fL = macrocytosis. (Note: Option D was missing from the original question.)
Which of the following is a critical step in preventing cross-contamination during PCR?

Aerosol formation is the most PCR-specific contamination risk — microscopic droplets from post-amplification products (amplicons) can contaminate pre-amplification reagents and samples, causing false-positive results. This is controlled by physical separation of pre/post-PCR areas, filtered pipette tips, and uracil-DNA glycosylase (UNG) carryover prevention. Gloves and laminar flow are general lab safety practices.
What is the primary use of flow cytometry in a clinical laboratory?

Flow cytometry uses laser-based fluorescence detection to identify and quantify cells by their surface and intracellular antigen expression (immunophenotyping). Clinical uses include leukaemia/lymphoma classification (CD markers), T-cell subsets in HIV (CD4 count), PNH diagnosis (CD55/CD59), and stem cell enumeration.
Which of the following is a method used for protein quantification?

The Bradford assay (Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 dye-binding) is the gold-standard colorimetric method for total protein quantification, measured spectrophotometrically at 595 nm. PCR amplifies DNA; Western blot identifies specific proteins by molecular weight (semi-quantitative); ELISA quantifies specific antigens/antibodies (not total protein).
What is the most commonly used method for microbiological culture and sensitivity testing?

The Kirby-Bauer disk diffusion method (CLSI/EUCAST standardised) is the most widely used routine antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) method in clinical microbiology — antibiotic-impregnated discs are placed on a lawn of bacteria and inhibition zone diameters correlate with susceptibility categories (S/I/R).
What is the purpose of using a centrifuge in clinical laboratories?

Centrifugation applies centrifugal force to separate blood components by density: plasma/serum (top), buffy coat — WBCs/platelets (middle), and RBCs (bottom). This is essential for serum/plasma preparation for chemistry testing, urine sediment concentration, and CSF cell counts.
Which type of chromatography is most commonly used for separating proteins?

Ion-exchange chromatography (IEC) separates proteins based on their net surface charge, using charged resin beads (cation or anion exchangers) and eluting with increasing salt gradients. It is the most widely used technique for protein purification and separation in both research and clinical labs (e.g., HbA1c measurement by cation-exchange HPLC).
Which method is commonly used for the detection of bacterial DNA?

PCR amplifies specific bacterial DNA sequences exponentially (10⁶–10⁹ copies), enabling detection of minute quantities directly from clinical specimens. Real-time PCR (qPCR) additionally quantifies bacterial load. It is especially valuable for fastidious, slow-growing, or non-cultivable organisms (e.g., M. tuberculosis, C. trachomatis).
Which of the following is a key indicator of sample degradation in PCR reactions?

Degraded DNA (from nuclease activity, freeze-thaw cycles, or improper storage) is fragmented below the size of the target amplicon, resulting in complete failure to amplify. Smeared or absent bands on gel electrophoresis, or no Ct value in qPCR, are the laboratory indicators. Primer-only presence or smaller amplicons suggest partial degradation or non-specific amplification.
What type of microscope is most suitable for examining live cells in their natural state?

Phase-contrast microscopy converts differences in refractive index of unstained, transparent living cells into visible contrast differences, allowing examination of live cells without fixation or staining. Bright-field requires staining; fluorescence requires labelling; electron microscopy requires fixation, dehydration, and vacuum conditions — all incompatible with live imaging.
In spectrophotometry, what does the absorbance of a sample depend on?

Per Beer-Lambert Law: A = εcl, where A = absorbance, ε = molar absorptivity (constant for a given substance at a given wavelength), c = concentration of the analyte, and l = path length. Absorbance is directly proportional to concentration — the basis of quantitative spectrophotometric assays in clinical chemistry.
What is the most important factor in the accuracy of a laboratory test?

Instrument calibration ensures that the analytical system's response is correctly correlated to known analyte concentrations. Without proper calibration using certified reference materials, all results are systematically biased. It is the foundation of metrological traceability in ISO 15189 accreditation and CLIA requirements.
Which of the following techniques is used for the detection of antibody-antigen reactions?

Immunoelectrophoresis combines electrophoretic separation of proteins with immunodiffusion against specific antisera, detecting antigen-antibody precipitin arcs. It identifies specific immunoglobulins and is used in diagnosing monoclonal gammopathies. PCR amplifies DNA; flow cytometry characterises cells; PCR-RFLP detects DNA polymorphisms.
What is the advantage of using a laminar flow hood in the laboratory?

A laminar flow hood (horizontal or vertical) delivers HEPA-filtered, unidirectional air over the work surface, creating a sterile environment that protects samples and reagents from airborne contamination. It is essential for cell culture, PCR setup, and microbiology work requiring aseptic technique.
Which of the following is a key diagnostic feature of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) on laboratory testing?

DKA is defined by the triad: hyperglycaemia (glucose usually >250 mg/dL), elevated ketones (β-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate, acetone), and metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, HCO₃⁻ <18 mEq/L). Elevated BUN may occur secondary to dehydration. Elevated ketones are the most specific biochemical feature distinguishing DKA from hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar state. (Note: Option D was missing from the original question.)
What is the most common method for diagnosing urinary tract infections (UTIs)?

Quantitative urine culture (clean-catch midstream) is the gold standard for UTI diagnosis. A colony count ≥10⁵ CFU/mL of a single uropathogen is diagnostic. Urine dipstick (nitrite + leukocyte esterase) is a rapid screen, but culture provides organism identification and antibiotic susceptibility (IDSA guidelines).
Which laboratory test is commonly used to monitor heparin therapy?

Unfractionated heparin (UFH) is monitored by aPTT (target: 1.5–2.5× baseline control, approximately 60–80 seconds). UFH potentiates antithrombin III, inhibiting thrombin and Factors Xa, IXa, XIa (intrinsic pathway). PT/INR monitors warfarin; anti-Xa assay monitors LMWH.
Which test is most useful for diagnosing myocardial infarction?

High-sensitivity cardiac troponin I (cTnI) or troponin T (cTnT) is the biomarker of choice for diagnosing acute MI per ESC/ACC/AHA guidelines. Troponins are highly sensitive and specific for myocardial injury, rising within 1–4 hours and remaining elevated for 7–14 days. CK-MB is less specific; CRP is an inflammatory marker; glucose is non-specific.
Which of the following is a common method used to determine the blood group of a patient?

ABO blood typing (forward typing: RBCs tested against anti-A and anti-B antisera; reverse typing: serum tested against known A and B cells) is the primary method for blood group determination. Crossmatching tests compatibility; Coombs detects antibodies on/in patient's RBCs; blood culture is for infection.
Which of the following is the standard technique for detecting DNA mutations associated with diseases?

PCR and its derivatives (allele-specific PCR, real-time PCR, digital PCR, PCR-sequencing) are the foundation of molecular diagnostics for disease-associated mutations. When combined with Sanger sequencing or next-generation sequencing (NGS), PCR enables detection of point mutations, insertions, deletions, and copy number variants.
Which of the following tests would be most appropriate for determining the presence of tuberculosis?

WHO gold standard for TB diagnosis: Ziehl-Neelsen or auramine-rhodamine-stained sputum smear (screening, rapid) plus culture on Löwenstein-Jensen medium or MGIT liquid medium (definitive confirmation and DST). Chest X-ray is supportive but non-specific. Molecular tests (Xpert MTB/RIF) are now recommended as initial diagnostics where available.
What is the primary use of a flow cytometer in a laboratory setting?

Flow cytometry interrogates individual cells as they pass single-file through laser beams, measuring scatter (size, granularity) and fluorescence (labelled antibodies/dyes). It identifies, counts, and can physically sort cell populations based on specific immunophenotypic and physical characteristics. Used clinically for leukaemia classification, CD4 counts, PNH, and more.
What is the purpose of performing a cytogenetic analysis on a patient sample?

Cytogenetic analysis (karyotyping, FISH, chromosomal microarray) examines the number, structure, and arrangement of chromosomes to detect aneuploidy (trisomy 21, monosomy X), structural rearrangements (translocations, deletions, inversions), and copy number variants associated with genetic diseases and malignancies.
Which of the following is a primary method used to detect genetic mutations in laboratory medicine?

DNA microarrays (gene chips) contain thousands of probes hybridising to complementary DNA sequences from patient samples, enabling simultaneous detection of thousands of SNPs, copy number variations (CNVs), and gene expression changes. They are a primary platform for genome-wide mutation analysis in clinical genetics and oncology.
What is the role of a spectrophotometer in the laboratory?

A spectrophotometer measures the amount of light absorbed by a solution at specific wavelengths, quantifying analytes per Beer-Lambert Law. It is fundamental to clinical chemistry assays (glucose, creatinine, bilirubin, protein, enzymes) and molecular biology (nucleic acid quantification at A260, protein at A280).
What is the primary function of an autoclave in a laboratory?

An autoclave achieves sterilisation using saturated steam at 121°C under 15 psi for 15–20 minutes (or 134°C for 3 minutes), destroying all microorganisms including bacterial spores via protein denaturation. It is the gold standard for sterilising heat/moisture-stable equipment, glassware, and biohazardous waste.
Which of the following blood tests can provide information about a patient's liver function?

ALT and AST are intracellular hepatic enzymes released upon hepatocyte injury or necrosis, making them direct markers of hepatocellular damage. While PT also reflects liver synthetic function (clotting factor production) and is technically a liver function test, the most direct and specific answer among the options is liver enzymes (ALT, AST).
Which of the following is most appropriate for diagnosing bacterial meningitis?

CSF analysis via lumbar puncture is the gold standard for diagnosing bacterial meningitis: turbid appearance, elevated opening pressure, markedly raised WBC (predominantly PMNs), elevated protein (>45 mg/dL), decreased glucose (<45 mg/dL or CSF:serum ratio <0.6), Gram stain, and culture/sensitivity. Blood cultures are adjunctive (IDSA guidelines).
What is the role of a laboratory technician in performing a PCR assay?

The laboratory technician's role in PCR encompasses pre-analytical (sample receipt, DNA extraction, quality assessment), analytical (reagent preparation, reaction setup, thermocycler programming), and post-analytical (gel electrophoresis or qPCR analysis, result documentation) steps. Clinical interpretation and diagnosis remain the physician's responsibility.
Which of the following markers is commonly used in diagnosing prostate cancer?

PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen) is a serine protease kallikrein produced by prostatic epithelial cells. Elevated total PSA (>4 ng/mL) triggers further investigation for prostate cancer. PSA density, PSA velocity, and free/total PSA ratio refine specificity. CA-125 = ovarian; CEA = colorectal; AFP = hepatocellular/germ cell tumours.
Which of the following methods is typically used to test for blood coagulation?

aPTT tests the intrinsic and common coagulation pathways (Factors XII, XI, IX, VIII, X, V, II, I). Together with PT/INR (extrinsic pathway), it constitutes the standard coagulation screening panel. CBC provides platelet count but does not directly test clotting factor function.
Which of the following tests is used to measure iron levels in the blood?

Ferritin is the primary intracellular iron storage protein, and serum ferritin is the best indicator of total body iron stores. Low ferritin (<12 ng/mL) is specific for iron deficiency. The iron studies panel also includes serum iron, TIBC, and transferrin saturation. ABG measures blood gases; Coombs detects antibodies; BUN measures renal function.
Which of the following tests is commonly used to confirm a diagnosis of syphilis?

The standard syphilis testing algorithm uses a non-treponemal screening test (VDRL or RPR), which detects anticardiolipin antibodies — these are sensitive but non-specific (false positives in autoimmune disease, pregnancy). Positive results are confirmed with a treponemal test (FTA-ABS, TPPA). Among the options, VDRL is the only relevant test (CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021).
What is the primary use of the ELISA test in the laboratory?

ELISA (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay) uses enzyme-labelled antibodies to detect and quantify specific antigens or antibodies in patient samples through colorimetric, fluorescent, or chemiluminescent reactions. Clinical applications include HIV antibody detection, hepatitis B/C serology, autoimmune antibody testing, and hormone quantification.
Which of the following tests is used to monitor kidney function by assessing the glomerular filtration rate (GFR)?

Creatinine clearance (calculated from 24-hour urine creatinine, serum creatinine, and urine volume) provides a direct estimate of GFR, as creatinine is freely filtered and minimally secreted/reabsorbed. eGFR (CKD-EPI equation) is now preferred for routine monitoring, but creatinine clearance remains the standard quantitative GFR test (KDIGO guidelines).
Which of the following tests is most commonly used for screening for colorectal cancer in asymptomatic patients?

FOBT (guaiac-based or FIT — fecal immunochemical test) is the most widely used non-invasive screening test for colorectal cancer in asymptomatic average-risk patients, recommended annually from age 45 per ACS/USPSTF guidelines. Colonoscopy is the gold standard diagnostic test but is not a primary screening method due to cost and invasiveness.
Which of the following is the most commonly used method for measuring viral load in HIV patients?

HIV viral load is measured by quantitative real-time PCR (RT-qPCR), which amplifies and quantifies HIV-1 RNA copies in plasma. It is used for treatment monitoring (target: undetectable <50 copies/mL on ART), assessing disease progression, and guiding treatment decisions (WHO, DHHS HIV guidelines). Western blot confirms HIV antibody status; ELISA screens for antibodies.

questions: A common cause of proteinuria:

All three are established causes of proteinuria. Diabetes mellitus causes glomerular injury leading to albuminuria (diabetic nephropathy). Bence-Jones proteins (free immunoglobulin light chains in multiple myeloma) are small enough to pass the glomerulus and appear in urine. Prolonged fasting (>18 hours) can produce orthostatic/functional proteinuria due to altered renal perfusion and protein metabolism.
An urine output < 400 ml / 24 hours is called:

Oliguria is defined as urine output <400 mL/24 hours (or <0.5 mL/kg/hr) in adults, indicating insufficient renal clearance. Anuria is <100 mL/24 hours (near-complete cessation); polyuria is >3,000 mL/24 hours. Oliguria is a key sign of acute kidney injury (AKI) (Tietz Fundamentals of Clinical Chemistry).
The more specific kidney function marker is:

Creatinine clearance (calculated from 24-hour urine and serum creatinine) is the most specific functional marker of GFR, as creatinine is freely filtered and minimally secreted/reabsorbed. BUN is affected by diet, catabolism, and liver function; uric acid reflects purine metabolism, not GFR specifically (KDIGO guidelines).
Semen contains a sugar source for the spermatozoa energy that is:

The primary energy substrate for spermatozoa in seminal plasma is fructose, secreted by the seminal vesicles (normal >13 µmol/ejaculate). Spermatozoa metabolise fructose via glycolysis for ATP production. Glucose, sucrose, lactose, and maltose are not the primary sugar in semen — hence 'None of the above' is correct.
An increased level in Gamma-GT gives an indication of:

Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) is elevated in obstructive jaundice (cholestasis), hepatocellular disease, alcohol abuse, and drug-induced liver injury. It is the most sensitive enzyme for detecting biliary obstruction and alcohol-related liver disease. (Note: Only one option was provided in the original question.)
In anemia, the count of reticulocytes is increased EXCEPT:

Vitamin B12 deficiency (megaloblastic) anemia is a hypoproliferative anemia — the bone marrow cannot synthesise DNA normally, so RBC production is impaired and reticulocyte count is LOW (not increased). In contrast, acute haemorrhagic anaemia, haemolytic anaemia, and treated iron deficiency anaemia all stimulate compensatory reticulocytosis (Rodak's Hematology).
Na Fluoride is used to prepare samples for:

Sodium fluoride (NaF) is a glycolytic inhibitor — it blocks enolase, halting cellular glucose consumption by RBCs and WBCs in whole blood. It is used exclusively for blood glucose and lactate specimens to prevent in-vitro glucose decline (without NaF, glucose falls ~10–12 mg/dL/hour at room temperature). It is not used for electrolytes or blood gases.
The first collected tube of CSF is used for:

Standard CSF collection protocol: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, protein, LDH); Tube 2 = Microbiology (culture and sensitivity — collected second to minimise skin flora contamination); Tube 3 = Haematology (cell count and differential). The first tube is used for chemistry because potential minor contamination from the needle track has less impact on chemical analytes than on cell counts or cultures.
HbA1c in diabetic patient is important for:

HbA1c reflects the average blood glucose concentration over the preceding 2–3 months (the lifespan of a red blood cell), making it the gold standard for long-term glycaemic monitoring in diabetes. Fasting glucose and post-prandial glucose are used for short-term assessment. ADA recommends HbA1c testing at least twice yearly in well-controlled and quarterly in poorly controlled diabetics.
The most common type of anemia is:

The most common type of anemia worldwide is iron deficiency anemia (IDA), which is not listed among options a–d. IDA accounts for approximately 50% of all anaemias globally, affecting ~1.2 billion people (WHO). Megaloblastic, pernicious, and haemolytic anaemias are all far less common. Therefore, 'None of the above' is correct.
The final result 'No Growth' for a blood culture is given after:

Standard automated blood culture systems (BACTEC, BacT/ALERT) incubate continuously for 5 days for routine specimens; 'No Growth' is reported at day 5 (approximately 1 week). Extended incubation (2–4 weeks) is reserved for suspected endocarditis, fungal, or fastidious organisms (e.g., Brucella, Bartonella). Among the given options, 1 week best approximates routine blood culture protocol.
'Bad' cholesterol is carried on:

LDL (Low-Density Lipoprotein) is termed 'bad' cholesterol because it transports cholesterol to peripheral tissues; elevated LDL promotes atheromatous plaque formation and cardiovascular disease. HDL ('good' cholesterol) performs reverse cholesterol transport, removing cholesterol from tissues to the liver for excretion (Tietz).
Gonorrhea can be diagnosed by:

RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin) and VDRL are non-treponemal serological tests used exclusively for syphilis (Treponema pallidum), NOT gonorrhoea. Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) is diagnosed by Gram stain (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci), culture on Thayer-Martin medium, or NAAT/PCR (CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021).
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase enzyme is present in:

G6PD is the key enzyme of the pentose phosphate pathway (hexose monophosphate shunt), generating NADPH to maintain glutathione in its reduced form, protecting RBCs from oxidative damage. G6PD deficiency is clinically significant specifically in erythrocytes because mature RBCs lack mitochondria and depend entirely on this pathway for oxidative protection (Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
An Anti-Streptolysin O test is used to detect:

ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) detects antibodies against streptolysin O, an exotoxin of Group A beta-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS). Elevated ASO titre confirms recent streptococcal infection and is used to support diagnoses of post-streptococcal sequelae: rheumatic fever and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. RF (Rheumatoid Factor) diagnoses rheumatoid arthritis, not ASO.
Which of the following has the highest affinity for oxygen?

Myoglobin has the highest oxygen affinity (P50 ~1–2 mmHg) — its hyperbolic O₂ dissociation curve reflects single haem-binding without cooperative interactions. Haemoglobin has a sigmoidal curve with P50 ~26 mmHg due to allosteric cooperativity (Bohr effect), allowing efficient O₂ release in tissues. Methemoglobin (Fe³⁺) cannot bind oxygen at all.
Acute leukemia is characterized by:

All three statements are correct. ALL (Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia) accounts for ~80% of childhood leukaemias (peak age 2–5 years). AML predominates in adults (>60% of acute leukaemia in adults). The hallmark of acute leukaemia is an accumulation of immature blast cells (>20% blasts in bone marrow by WHO criteria) with arrested maturation (Henry's, Rodak's).
In a major cross matching, the following is applied:

Major crossmatch = Donor RBCs + Recipient (patient) serum — detects antibodies in the patient's serum that may react with donor RBCs, the most clinically critical incompatibility. Minor crossmatch = Donor serum + Recipient RBCs. The major crossmatch is the essential test before any blood transfusion to prevent haemolytic transfusion reactions.
Widal test is used for the diagnosis of:

The Widal test detects agglutinating antibodies (IgM/IgG) against the O (somatic) and H (flagellar) antigens of Salmonella typhi and S. paratyphi, diagnosing enteric (typhoid) fever. Typhus fever is caused by Rickettsia species and is diagnosed by Weil-Felix test or specific serological tests. These are distinct diseases with distinct diagnostic tests.
An acid-fast bacterium is EXCEPT:

Mycoplasma spp. completely LACKS a cell wall and therefore cannot retain carbol fuchsin in the Ziehl-Neelsen stain — it is NOT acid-fast. Mycobacterium spp. are the classical acid-fast bacteria (mycolic acid-rich cell wall). Nocardia spp. is partially (weakly) acid-fast. Mycoplasma is unique as the smallest self-replicating bacterium with no cell wall (resistant to beta-lactam antibiotics for the same reason).
In emergency, what is the best blood group that can be transfused safely?

O Rh-negative ('universal donor') RBCs lack ABO antigens (no anti-A or anti-B reaction) AND the Rh(D) antigen, minimising the risk of acute haemolytic transfusion reactions and Rh alloimmunisation in unmatched emergency transfusions. O Rh-positive would be unsafe for Rh-negative females of childbearing potential. AB positive is the universal plasma donor, not RBC donor.
ANA is found in case of:

ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) are the hallmark autoantibodies of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) — sensitivity >95% for SLE, though specificity is limited (~60%). Specific ANA subtypes (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm) are highly specific for SLE. CML (chronic myeloid leukaemia) is a myeloproliferative neoplasm; IMN is a viral infection — neither is primarily associated with ANA.
Schilling test is applied to diagnose:

The Schilling test diagnoses pernicious anemia and B12 malabsorption by measuring radioactive B12 absorption with and without intrinsic factor (IF). A low absorption corrected by oral IF confirms IF deficiency (pernicious anemia); uncorrected absorption suggests ileal malabsorption. Neither iron deficiency anaemia nor aplastic anaemia is diagnosed by this test. (Note: Option D 'None of the above' was missing from the original question but is the correct answer — the test diagnoses pernicious/B12 malabsorption anaemia.)
Hot air oven is used for sterilization of EXCEPT:

Hot air ovens use dry heat at 160–180°C for 1–2 hours. They are suitable for heat-stable, moisture-sensitive items: metal instruments, glassware, oils, and powders. Catheters (made of rubber, latex, or plastic/PVC) cannot withstand these temperatures — they would melt, warp, or degrade. Catheters are sterilised using ethylene oxide (EtO) or gamma irradiation instead.
Sickle cell anemia (SCA) is caused by the following:

All three statements are accurate. SCA is caused by a single nucleotide substitution (GAG→GTG) at codon 6 of the HBB gene, replacing hydrophilic glutamic acid with hydrophobic valine in the beta-globin chain. This causes HbS polymerisation under deoxygenation. Haemoglobin electrophoresis (alkaline and acid pH) or HPLC confirms HbSS genotype, showing absent HbA and >90% HbS (WHO, Henry's).
Eosinophilia is seen in EXCEPT:

This question contains a structural contradiction. Food allergy (IgE-mediated), atopic dermatitis (allergic skin disease), and parasitic infections (especially tissue-invasive helminths) are all well-established causes of eosinophilia — none qualifies as an 'EXCEPT.' The question is incorrectly constructed; no option listed is a valid exception. The likely intended answer if 'None of the above' were present would be 'None of the above' (meaning all three DO cause eosinophilia). Bacterial infections would be the classic 'does NOT cause eosinophilia' — but this is not offered as an option.
A good hand disinfectant used for viruses is:

Ethanol (70–85%) is the gold standard hand disinfectant with proven broad-spectrum virucidal activity against both enveloped viruses (SARS-CoV-2, influenza, HIV) and many non-enveloped viruses. Soap removes but does not inactivate viruses. Hypochlorite (bleach) is virucidal but too corrosive for repeated skin application. WHO recommends ethanol-based hand rubs for healthcare settings.
An increased (>1.020) specific gravity in urine indicates:

Urine specific gravity >1.020 reflects highly concentrated urine due to elevated ADH secretion or reduced fluid intake, occurring in water restriction (voluntary or prescribed), dehydration (inadequate intake), and diarrhoea (fluid loss with compensatory water conservation). All three cause the kidney to maximally concentrate urine, increasing solute concentration and specific gravity.
Selective media is:

All three are selective media. SS agar (Salmonella-Shigella) selects for Salmonella and Shigella by inhibiting coliforms with bile salts and brilliant green. XLD agar (Xylose-Lysine-Deoxycholate) selects for enteric pathogens. Mannitol Salt Agar selects for staphylococci using 7.5% NaCl. (Note: The question options were incomplete in the original; all listed options are correctly classified as selective media.)
Blood agar media is:

Blood agar (5% sheep blood in tryptic soy agar base) is simultaneously: (1) Basic/general-purpose medium supporting most non-fastidious organisms; (2) Differential medium — haemolysis patterns (alpha/partial, beta/complete, gamma/none) differentiate bacterial species; (3) Enriched medium — blood supplies growth factors (NAD, hemin) for fastidious organisms like Streptococcus pneumoniae (Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
Which of the following is a direct test for HIV?

p24 antigen (detects viral capsid protein) and PCR (detects HIV RNA/DNA) are DIRECT tests — they detect the virus itself. Western blot detects HIV-specific ANTIBODIES (indirect, used for confirmatory serology). Therefore, 'All of the above' is incorrect. The correct answer is p24 antigen and PCR only. This question contains an error — no single listed option correctly captures 'p24 and PCR only.' Marking p24 antigen as the best single direct test answer if forced.
Which of the following tests are used for female infertility?

Both LH (Luteinising Hormone) and FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) are essential in the female infertility workup. FSH assesses ovarian reserve (elevated FSH = diminished reserve); LH detects ovulatory surges and polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS). The full panel also includes oestradiol, AMH, progesterone, prolactin, and thyroid function. (Note: Options c and d were missing from the original question.)
In liver dysfunction investigation, which test is used?

All three autoantibodies are used in autoimmune liver disease investigation: ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) + ASMA (Anti-Smooth Muscle Antibodies) are characteristic of Autoimmune Hepatitis Type 1; AMA (Anti-Mitochondrial Antibodies, specifically anti-M2) are the hallmark (>95% sensitive and specific) of Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC). Together they form the autoimmune liver disease antibody panel.
Ferritin is used for:

Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific marker for body iron stores. Low ferritin (<12 ng/mL) is virtually diagnostic of iron deficiency anaemia (IDA) and is the first parameter to fall in iron depletion. In haemolytic anaemia, ferritin is elevated (from iron released by lysed RBCs). Ferritin plays no diagnostic role in B12 deficiency, which is assessed by serum B12, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine.
Beta hemolysis on blood agar indicates:

Beta-haemolysis is complete lysis of surrounding RBCs, producing a clear zone around colonies (e.g., Streptococcus pyogenes, S. agalactiae). Alpha-haemolysis is partial (greenish zone due to H₂O₂-mediated methaemoglobin formation, e.g., S. pneumoniae, viridans streptococci). Gamma-haemolysis = no lysis. (Note: Options c and d were missing from the original question.)
CA19.9 marker is used for EXCEPT:

CA19-9 is elevated in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (primary marker), cholangiocarcinoma, and colorectal cancer. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is diagnosed using AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) as the primary tumour marker, not CA19-9. CA19-9 may be mildly elevated in hepatic conditions but is NOT a marker for HCC. Therefore, hepatocellular cancer is the correct EXCEPT answer.
Marker CA15.3 is used for:

CA15-3 (Cancer Antigen 15-3) is the primary serum tumour marker for breast cancer, used for monitoring treatment response and detecting recurrence (not for initial screening). CA-125 = ovarian cancer; CA19-9 = pancreatic cancer; PSA = prostate cancer; AFP = liver/germ cell tumours.
Gonorrhea is diagnosed by:

RPR, VDRL, and Wassermann test are ALL serological tests for syphilis (Treponema pallidum), not gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae). Gonorrhoea diagnosis requires: Gram stain (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci in PMNs), culture on Thayer-Martin/chocolate agar, or NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test) — the most sensitive modern method (CDC 2021 STI Guidelines).
The following are liver function tests EXCEPT:

ALP, AST, and GGT are ALL recognised liver function/liver injury tests. ALP reflects cholestasis and biliary disease; AST reflects hepatocellular injury (also found in cardiac and skeletal muscle); GGT is the most sensitive marker for alcohol-related liver disease and biliary obstruction. Since all listed enzymes are genuine liver function tests, 'None of the above' is the correct EXCEPT answer (i.e., there is no exception among the listed options).
Which of the following is used to measure the function of thyroid gland?

Thyroid function is assessed by T3 (triiodothyronine), T4 (thyroxine), and TSH (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone). TSH is the most sensitive single test — it reflects the pituitary's response to circulating thyroid hormone levels. FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) is a gonadotropin assessing reproductive/gonadal function, not thyroid function. Therefore, 'a and b' (T3/T4 + TSH) is correct.
Infection in urinary tract infections (UTI) is most frequently associated with:

The most common UTI pathogens are Gram-negative enteric bacteria — primarily Escherichia coli (~80% of community UTIs), Klebsiella pneumoniae, Proteus mirabilis, and Enterobacter spp. These organisms colonise the perineum from the GI tract and ascend the urethra. They are part of the normal intestinal (colonic) flora, not respiratory or nasal flora (IDSA UTI Guidelines).
Syphilis is diagnosed by:

All three tests detect syphilis (Treponema pallidum). RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin) and VDRL (Venereal Disease Research Laboratory) are modern non-treponemal screening tests detecting anticardiolipin antibodies, quantifiable for treatment monitoring. The Wassermann test is the historical complement fixation test (largely obsolete). All three are used/have been used for syphilis diagnosis.
All of the following are gram-positive rods EXCEPT:

Corynebacterium (e.g., C. diphtheriae) IS a Gram-positive rod — it is the exception to the question's implicit premise. Shigella, Salmonella, and Klebsiella are all Gram-negative rods (Enterobacteriaceae). The question as written contains an error (all of options a, b, c are gram-NEGATIVE, not gram-positive), but the intended answer is Corynebacterium — the only gram-positive rod among the options. The question should read 'gram-negative rods EXCEPT.'
According to gas requirements, bacteria can be:

Bacteria are classified by oxygen requirements into: Obligate (strict) aerobes (require O₂), Obligate anaerobes (killed by O₂), Facultative anaerobes (grow with or without O₂, prefer O₂), Microaerophiles (require low O₂ ~5%), and Aerotolerant anaerobes. Both listed options are valid categories. (Note: Options c and d were missing from the original question, likely including obligate aerobe and microaerophile.)
Which of the following is the pinworm parasite?

Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm/threadworm) is the most common helminthic infection in developed countries, causing perianal pruritus (especially nocturnal). Females migrate to the perianal area to lay eggs, diagnosed by the adhesive tape (Scotch tape) test. Ascaris lumbricoides = roundworm; Ancylostoma duodenale = hookworm.
Pernicious anemia is caused by:

Pernicious anaemia is an autoimmune condition in which antibodies against gastric parietal cells and/or intrinsic factor (IF) destroy IF-producing cells. Without IF, ileal B12 absorption is impossible, causing B12 deficiency and megaloblastic anaemia. All three components (IF deficiency, B12 deficiency, parietal cell/IF antibodies) are integral to the pathophysiology of pernicious anaemia (Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
RF is used for diagnosis of:

RF (Rheumatoid Factor) is an autoantibody (usually IgM) directed against the Fc region of IgG. It is used to diagnose and monitor Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) — present in ~70–80% of RA patients (seropositive RA). Rheumatic fever is diagnosed using Jones criteria with supporting evidence from ASO titre (Anti-Streptolysin O), NOT RF. Confusing RF with rheumatic fever is a common trap.
Occult blood test is used for:

Fecal occult blood test (FOBT/FIT) detects hidden blood in stool from all three: hookworm infection (causes GI blood loss → iron deficiency anaemia); colorectal cancer (tumour bleeding); and as a workup for unexplained iron deficiency or microcytic anaemia (identifying occult GI blood loss as the source). All three are legitimate clinical indications.
Which of the following is part of a cardiac profile?

The cardiac biomarker profile includes: Troponin I/T (most sensitive and specific for myocardial injury — current gold standard); CK-MB (creatine kinase MB isoform, earlier marker); and Myoglobin (rises earliest at 1–3 hours post-MI but non-specific). Together they provide a time-based picture of myocardial injury. LDH (LD-1 isoform) and AST are older markers still used in some panels.
Used for TB diagnosis:

The Tuberculin (Mantoux/PPD) skin test detects delayed-type hypersensitivity to Mycobacterium tuberculosis antigens — a positive result (induration ≥10 mm at 48–72 hours) indicates prior TB exposure or infection. Modern alternatives include IGRAs (Interferon-Gamma Release Assays, e.g., QuantiFERON-TB Gold). (Note: Options were incomplete in the original question; the Tuberculin test is the identifiable correct answer.)
