questions: A hand disinfectant used against viruses is

Ethanol 70–85% is the WHO-recommended gold standard hand disinfectant with proven broad-spectrum virucidal activity against enveloped viruses (SARS-CoV-2, influenza, HIV) and many non-enveloped viruses. Dettol (chloroxylenol) is primarily bactericidal with limited virucidal efficacy. Ethanol-based hand rubs are the first-line recommendation in healthcare settings.
Autoclave is used for sterilization of

Autoclaving (saturated steam at 121°C/15 psi for 15–20 minutes) achieves sterilisation of all heat- and moisture-stable items: culture media, borosilicate glassware, metal instruments, and lab tools. It destroys all microbial life including bacterial endospores by protein denaturation and is the gold standard moist-heat sterilisation method (ISO 17665).
Lab. equipment should be cleaned and disinfected by

Both agents are standard laboratory disinfectants. Sodium hypochlorite (bleach, 0.1–1%) is a broad-spectrum surface disinfectant effective against bacteria, fungi, spores, and viruses. Formaldehyde/formalin is used for gaseous fumigation of biosafety cabinets and rooms. Both are approved for laboratory decontamination per WHO and CDC biosafety guidelines.
The best sample to diagnose meningitis

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) obtained by lumbar puncture is the definitive specimen for diagnosing meningitis. CSF analysis provides cell count and differential, protein, glucose, Gram stain, and culture/sensitivity — the gold standard for bacterial, viral, fungal, and tuberculous meningitis (IDSA guidelines). Blood culture is adjunctive; sputum is irrelevant to meningitis.
Acid fast bacteria

Mycobacterium spp. are the quintessential acid-fast bacteria — their mycolic acid-rich waxy cell wall retains carbol fuchsin after acid-alcohol decolourisation in the Ziehl-Neelsen stain, appearing red. Nocardia is only partially/weakly acid-fast. Mycoplasma has NO cell wall and cannot be acid-fast. 'All of the above' is incorrect because Mycoplasma is not acid-fast.
Mycobacterium leprae is

Mycobacterium leprae is an obligate aerobe that preferentially infects cooler, well-oxygenated peripheral tissues (skin, peripheral nerves, nasal mucosa). It grows optimally at 27–30°C, explaining its predilection for superficial structures. It cannot be cultured on artificial media and is grown experimentally in armadillo footpads.
Widal test used to diagnose

The Widal test detects agglutinating antibodies against O (somatic) and H (flagellar) antigens of Salmonella typhi and S. paratyphi, supporting the diagnosis of enteric (typhoid) fever. Typhus fever is caused by Rickettsia spp. and is diagnosed by the Weil-Felix test or specific Rickettsia serology — not the Widal test.
To diagnose gonorrhea

RPR and VDRL are non-treponemal serological tests used exclusively for syphilis (Treponema pallidum). Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) is diagnosed by Gram stain (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci in PMNs), culture on Thayer-Martin medium, or NAAT/PCR — the most sensitive modern method (CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines).
The following statements are true about viruses except

Viruses are NOT metabolically active — they are obligate intracellular parasites that completely lack metabolic machinery (no ribosomes, no ATP-generating systems). They rely entirely on host cell metabolism for replication. The other statements are true: viruses contain only DNA or RNA (never both); their size ranges approximately 20–300 nm; and certain viruses (Parvovirus B19, HIV) cause anaemia.
Schilling test is used to diagnose

The Schilling test uses radiolabelled Vitamin B12 to assess B12 absorption. Low urinary excretion corrected by co-administration of intrinsic factor (IF) confirms IF deficiency = pernicious anaemia. It does not diagnose IDA (assessed by ferritin, serum iron, TIBC) or aplastic anaemia (diagnosed by bone marrow biopsy).
Gamma haemolysis on blood agar indicates

Gamma (γ) haemolysis = no lysis of surrounding RBCs, no change around colonies (e.g., Enterococcus faecalis, S. epidermidis). Beta (β) haemolysis = complete, clear zone of RBC lysis (S. pyogenes). Alpha (α) haemolysis = partial lysis with a greenish zone due to methaemoglobin formation (S. pneumoniae, viridans streptococci).
Direct diagnosis assay for virus identification is

Immunofluorescence (IF) directly detects viral antigens within infected cells or specimens using fluorescently-labelled virus-specific antibodies — it detects the virus itself. ELISA and Western blot detect patient antibodies against viruses (indirect serological methods reflecting the host immune response, not the virus directly). Direct IF is used clinically for RSV, influenza, herpes, and rabies diagnosis.
ANA is found in case of

ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) are the hallmark autoantibodies of SLE — sensitivity >95%, making it the best SLE screening test. Specific subtypes (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm) have high specificity for SLE. CML is a BCR-ABL1 myeloproliferative neoplasm; IMN is an EBV viral infection — neither is primarily associated with ANA.
SCA is caused by all the following except

In Sickle Cell Anaemia, the HBB gene mutation (GAG→GTG) at codon 6 replaces glutamic acid with valine — NOT lysine. Lysine substitution at the same position (GAG→AAG) causes HbC disease, a distinct haemoglobinopathy. All other statements are true: it is a point mutation at codon 6, glutamate is replaced by valine, and Hb electrophoresis/HPLC confirms the HbSS genotype.
Bence-Jones protein found in case of

Bence-Jones proteins are free immunoglobulin light chains (kappa or lambda) produced in excess by malignant plasma cells in Multiple Myeloma. They are small enough to pass the glomerular filtration barrier and appear in urine, detected by urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP) or immunofixation. 'Chronic/acute myeloid myeloma' are not recognised medical entities.
Codocyte are seen in case of

Codocytes (target cells / Mexican hat cells) are RBCs with excess membrane relative to cell volume, occurring in IDA, thalassaemia, haemoglobin C/SC disease, liver disease, and post-splenectomy. Vitamin B12 deficiency produces macroovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils, not codocytes.
Eosinophilia found in

Both food allergy (IgE-mediated, triggering eosinophil recruitment) and atopic dermatitis (type 2 hypersensitivity with IL-4/IL-5/IL-13 driving eosinophilia) are well-established causes of eosinophilia. The NAACP mnemonic covers causes: Neoplasm, Asthma/Allergy, Addison's disease, Collagen vascular disease, Parasites.
Acute leukemia is characterized by

All three statements accurately characterise acute leukaemia. ALL accounts for ~80% of childhood leukaemias (peak 2–5 years). AML predominates in adults (>60% of adult acute leukaemia). The hallmark of acute leukaemia is arrested maturation with ≥20% blasts in bone marrow (WHO 2022 criteria), overwhelming normal haematopoiesis.
Useful cholesterol is

HDL ('good/useful' cholesterol) performs reverse cholesterol transport — removing excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues and arterial walls to the liver for excretion. High HDL (>60 mg/dL) is cardioprotective. LDL ('bad' cholesterol) deposits cholesterol in arterial walls promoting atherosclerosis. Chylomicrons transport dietary lipids from intestine to tissues.
Secretion of gonadal hormone is controlled by

FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone), secreted by anterior pituitary gonadotrophs, stimulates gonadal function: follicular development and oestrogen production in females; spermatogenesis in males. LH also controls gonadal hormones. TSH controls thyroid hormone secretion — it is a thyrotropin, not a gonadotropin.
Which of the following organism is capsulated?

None of the listed organisms are the classical capsulated pathogens. Shigella spp. are non-capsulated. Classical heavily encapsulated bacteria include Streptococcus pneumoniae, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae type b, and Neisseria meningitidis — none appear here. While some E. coli strains have K-antigen capsules, E. coli is not defined primarily by its capsule in clinical microbiology teaching.
HbA1c in patient with DM is important for

HbA1c reflects glycation of haemoglobin over the RBC lifespan (~2–3 months), providing an integrated measure of mean blood glucose over the preceding 8–12 weeks. It is the gold standard for long-term glycaemic monitoring in diabetes. ADA recommends testing every 3 months in poorly controlled and every 6 months in well-controlled diabetics.
First tube in CSF is for

Standard CSF tube collection: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, protein) — minor contamination has minimal impact on chemical analytes; Tube 2 = Microbiology (culture, sensitivity) — later collection reduces skin flora contamination; Tube 3 = Haematology (cell count, differential). This order minimises the impact of traumatic tap on microbiology and haematology results.
The normal value of bilirubin is

Normal total serum bilirubin is 0.2–1.2 mg/dL (Tietz reference range). 'Less than 1 mg/dl' most closely approximates this. 'Less than 2 mg/dl' is too broad — jaundice becomes clinically apparent at ~2–3 mg/dL. '0.2–2 mg/dL' extends above the true upper limit of normal.
Common cause of proteinuria is

Diabetes mellitus is the leading cause of significant, persistent proteinuria worldwide (diabetic nephropathy), accounting for ~40% of all end-stage renal disease cases. Hyperglycaemia causes glomerular hyperfiltration, basement membrane thickening, and progressive albuminuria. Bence-Jones proteinuria is a less common overflow proteinuria from multiple myeloma.
The best test of kidney function

Creatinine clearance (24-hour urine or estimated by CKD-EPI/MDRD equation) is the best practical GFR estimate. Creatinine is freely filtered and minimally secreted/reabsorbed, making it a reliable filtration marker. BUN is affected by diet, catabolism, and hydration; uric acid reflects purine metabolism, not filtration function (KDIGO CKD Guidelines).
The most common type of anemia

The most common type of anaemia worldwide is Iron Deficiency Anaemia (IDA), affecting ~1.2 billion people globally (~50% of all anaemia cases, WHO). IDA is not listed among options a–c. 'None of the above' is therefore correct.
ASO is done to diagnose

ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) detects antibodies against streptolysin O of Group A beta-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS). Elevated ASO supports the diagnosis of post-streptococcal sequelae: rheumatic fever and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. Rheumatoid arthritis is diagnosed by Rheumatoid Factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies.
The following is one of the tapeworm

Neither Ascaris lumbricoides (large intestinal roundworm — Nematode) nor Ancylostoma duodenale (hookworm — Nematode) is a tapeworm. Tapeworms (Cestodes) include Taenia solium, Taenia saginata, Diphyllobothrium latum, and Echinococcus. Both listed organisms are nematodes.
The final result in blood culture given no growth after

This reflects extended blood culture protocols for fastidious/slow-growing organisms. While standard automated systems (BACTEC/BacT/ALERT) report 'No Growth' at 5 days for routine cultures, 3-week incubation applies when Brucella spp., Bartonella spp., HACEK organisms, or fungi are suspected (e.g., culture-negative endocarditis workup). Per CLSI M47 Blood Culture guidelines, extended incubation is protocol-dependent.
In emergency, what is the best blood group that can be transfused safely?

O Rh-negative RBCs are the universal donor — lacking ABO antigens (no anti-A or anti-B reaction) AND the Rh(D) antigen (preventing alloimmunisation in Rh-negative females of childbearing age). O Rh-positive would be unsafe for Rh-negative patients. Full compatibility testing is always preferred when time permits.
Semen contains sugar as a source of energy

The primary energy substrate in seminal plasma for spermatozoa is fructose, secreted by the seminal vesicles (normal: >13 µmol/ejaculate per WHO 2021 criteria). Spermatozoa metabolise fructose via glycolysis for ATP-driven motility. Glucose, sucrose, and lactose are not significant seminal components — 'None of the above' is correct.
Urine output <400 ml/24hrs is called

Oliguria is defined as urine output <400 mL/24 hours (or <0.5 mL/kg/hr) — a key diagnostic criterion for Acute Kidney Injury (AKI). Anuria: <100 mL/24 hrs; Polyuria: >3,000 mL/24 hrs (as in diabetes insipidus or uncontrolled DM) (Tietz, KDIGO AKI Guidelines).
Haematopoiesis is defined as formation of

Haematopoiesis is the process of formation, differentiation, and maturation of ALL cellular blood elements from pluripotent haematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) in bone marrow — encompassing erythropoiesis (RBCs), leukopoiesis (granulocytes, monocytes, lymphocytes), and thrombopoiesis (platelets from megakaryocytes). All three lineages arise from a common CD34+ HSC precursor.
In anemia, the count of reticulocytes is decreased in

Vitamin B12 deficiency (megaloblastic) anaemia is a hypoproliferative anaemia — impaired DNA synthesis prevents normal RBC precursor proliferation, resulting in ineffective erythropoiesis and a LOW reticulocyte count. In contrast, acute haemorrhagic and haemolytic anaemias stimulate compensatory bone marrow reticulocytosis (elevated count). Low reticulocyte index = bone marrow failure or nutritional deficiency.
Sodium Fluoride is added to prepare samples for

Sodium fluoride (NaF) inhibits enolase, halting in-vitro cellular glycolysis in whole blood. Without NaF, glucose falls ~10–12 mg/dL per hour at room temperature. NaF/potassium oxalate grey-top tubes are standard for glucose and lactate testing. NaF is not used for coagulation studies (require citrate) or electrolytes.
Reservoir for visceral leishmania

For zoonotic visceral leishmaniasis (L. infantum/chagasi) in the Mediterranean basin, Middle East, and Latin America, the domestic dog is the primary reservoir host. Sandflies (Phlebotomus/Lutzomyia spp.) transmit the parasite between reservoir dogs and humans. In the Indian subcontinent, L. donovani causes anthroponotic VL where humans are the reservoir.
H. ducreyi causes

Haemophilus ducreyi causes chancroid (soft chancre) — a painful, non-indurated genital ulcer with suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy. It is distinguished from the painless, indurated hard chancre of primary syphilis (Treponema pallidum). Diagnosis: culture on GC agar with special supplements or NAAT/PCR.
CRP test is

C-Reactive Protein (CRP) is a non-specific acute phase reactant synthesised by the liver in response to IL-6 during ANY inflammatory state — bacterial or viral infection, tissue necrosis, autoimmune disease, malignancy, or trauma. It is not specific to any particular disease or pathogen. Its clinical value is its sensitivity as a general inflammatory marker.
Most common cause of UTI

~80% of community-acquired UTIs are caused by E. coli — a Gram-negative rod from the normal intestinal (colonic) flora that ascends from the perineum to colonise the urethra and bladder. Other intestinal flora include Klebsiella pneumoniae, Proteus mirabilis, and Enterococcus faecalis (IDSA UTI Guidelines).
VDRL is

VDRL stands for Venereal Disease Research Laboratory — the US Public Health Service laboratory where this non-treponemal flocculation test was developed (1946). It detects anticardiolipin (reagin) antibodies produced in response to Treponema pallidum infection. It is used for syphilis screening and treatment monitoring (titre falls with successful therapy).
Normal Hb in adult is (globin chains)

Normal adult haemoglobin (HbA) consists of 2 alpha (α) + 2 beta (β) globin chains (α2β2), accounting for ~97% of adult haemoglobin. None of the listed options are correct: 2β+2γ = HbF (foetal haemoglobin); 3β+2β is nonsensical; 1α+1β is a non-functional dimer. 'None of the above' is correct — the correct structure (α2β2) is absent from the options.
Increased >1.020 specific gravity in urine indicates

Urine specific gravity >1.020 reflects maximal renal concentrating response to reduced fluid availability, triggered in all three: water restriction, dehydration, and diarrhoea (GI fluid loss) all stimulate ADH secretion, increasing water reabsorption in the collecting duct and producing concentrated, high-SG urine. Normal SG: 1.005–1.030.
Glucose-6-phosphate enzyme is present in

G6PD is a ubiquitous cytoplasmic enzyme of the pentose phosphate pathway present in ALL cells, as NADPH production is essential for oxidative protection in every cell type. Clinically, G6PD deficiency manifests primarily in erythrocytes because mature RBCs lack mitochondria and are entirely dependent on this pathway for antioxidant defence (NADPH → reduced glutathione), making them uniquely vulnerable to oxidative haemolysis.
An increased level in Gamma-GT gives an indication of

GGT is elevated by all listed conditions. Obstructive jaundice and intrahepatic cholestasis elevate GGT via bile acid reflux inducing microsomal enzyme synthesis. Alcohol is one of the most potent GGT inducers (cytochrome P450 2E1 induction). Haematoma (tissue necrosis) releases intracellular GGT. GGT is the most sensitive but least specific liver enzyme.
AST test is used for diagnosis of

AST (Aspartate Aminotransferase) is found in high concentrations in both hepatocytes and cardiac myocytes, making it a marker for liver injury (hepatitis, cirrhosis, drug toxicity) and myocardial injury (acute MI). It is less liver-specific than ALT. The kidney has low AST content and is not a primary source of elevated serum AST.
Belong to liver function tests

All three are standard LFT panel components. AST reflects hepatocellular injury; ALP reflects cholestasis, biliary obstruction, and bone disease; bilirubin (total, direct, indirect) reflects hepatic conjugation capacity and biliary excretion. The full LFT panel also includes ALT, GGT, albumin, and total protein.
Pernicious anemia is deficiency of

Pernicious anaemia is caused by Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency due to autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells that produce intrinsic factor (IF), which is required for ileal B12 absorption. B12 is not among the listed options. B5 = pantothenic acid; B1 = thiamine (beriberi/Wernicke's); B9 = folate (megaloblastic anaemia but NOT pernicious anaemia). 'None of the above' is correct.
Pseudomonas is

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a strict/obligate aerobe — it requires oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor and cannot sustain growth in truly anaerobic conditions. This explains its predilection for the respiratory tract, burns, and wounds with adequate oxygen tension. It is oxidase-positive and grows on minimal media, reflecting its aerobic metabolism and metabolic versatility.

It cannot distinguish between these causes. Its clinical value lies in its sensitivity as a general inflammatory marker, not in specificity for any particular condition.

Ethanol 70–85% is the WHO-recommended gold-standard hand disinfectant with proven broad-spectrum virucidal activity against enveloped viruses (SARS-CoV-2, influenza, HIV) and many non-enveloped viruses. Medical soap mechanically removes but does not inactivate viruses. Hypochlorite (bleach) is virucidal but far too corrosive for repeated skin application — it is a surface disinfectant, not a hand rub (WHO Hand Hygiene Guidelines 2009).
The final result 'No Growth' for a blood culture is given after:

Definitive answer: Standard automated blood culture systems (BACTEC FX, BacT/ALERT) continuously monitor and report 'No Growth' at 5 days (~1 week) for routine specimens — this is the CLSI M47 and international standard. Extended incubation (up to 4 weeks) is a special protocol reserved ONLY when Brucella spp., Bartonella spp., or other fastidious organisms are specifically suspected (e.g., culture-negative endocarditis workup). Unless specified otherwise, '1 week' is the correct standard answer.
Common cause of Proteinuria is:

Definitive answer: Diabetes mellitus is the leading cause of clinically significant, persistent proteinuria worldwide (diabetic nephropathy), responsible for ~40% of end-stage renal disease globally (WHO, ADA). Hyperglycaemia causes glomerular hyperfiltration and basement membrane thickening → progressive albuminuria. Fasting >18 hours may cause mild transient functional proteinuria via altered renal haemodynamics, but it is not a 'common' or clinically important cause — DM is overwhelmingly the most significant answer here.
Lab instruments sterilization:

Definitive answer: For laboratory instrument disinfection/sterilisation, both formaldehyde (formalin, used for gaseous fumigation of enclosed spaces, biosafety cabinets, and heat-sensitive instruments) and hypochlorite (sodium bleach 0.1–1%, used for bench surfaces and non-critical equipment) are standard WHO/CDC-approved agents. Alcohol (70% ethanol/isopropanol) is used for skin and small surfaces but is not a standard instrument sterilant. 'A & B' reflects the broadest correct combination for lab instrument decontamination protocols.
First tube in CSF is for:

Standard CSF collection tube order: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, protein, LDH, lactate) — minor contamination from needle insertion has minimal impact on chemical analytes; Tube 2 = Microbiology (Gram stain, culture, sensitivity) — later collection reduces skin flora contamination risk; Tube 3 = Haematology/Cytology (cell count, differential) — most accurate RBC/WBC count obtained from the last tube, distinguishing traumatic tap from true haemorrhage.
Hot air oven is used for sterilization of:

Hot air ovens use dry heat at 160–180°C for 1–2 hours (or 170°C for 1 hour). They sterilise heat-stable, moisture-sensitive items: borosilicate glassware, metal instruments, oils, powders, and swabs. Items that cannot tolerate these temperatures (rubber, plastics, catheters, culture media) are sterilised by autoclave or EtO gas. Glass is the classic answer for hot air oven sterilisation. Widal test is used to diagnose:

A fourfold or greater rise in titre between acute and convalescent sera is diagnostically significant. It supports the diagnosis of enteric (typhoid) fever, not typhus fever (caused by Rickettsia spp., diagnosed by Weil-Felix test). (Only one option was provided in the original question.)

All three tests are used for syphilis (Treponema pallidum) diagnosis. RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin) and VDRL (Venereal Disease Research Laboratory) are modern non-treponemal flocculation screening tests detecting anticardiolipin antibodies — quantifiable for treatment monitoring. The Wassermann test is the historical complement fixation test (largely obsolete). All three detect the same antibody class and have been/are used for syphilis diagnosis.
The following statements are true about viruses:

All three statements are correct about viruses. (1) Viruses contain only one type of nucleic acid — either DNA (herpesviruses, adenoviruses) OR RNA (influenza, HIV, SARS-CoV-2), never both simultaneously. (2) Antibiotics target bacterial structures (cell wall, ribosomes, DNA gyrase) that viruses lack — viruses are inherently unaffected by antibiotics; antivirals are required. (3) Viruses are metabolically inert outside host cells — they have no ribosomes, ATP-generating systems, or metabolic enzymes; host machinery is hijacked for replication. All three = 'All.'
Schilling test is used to diagnose:

The Schilling test uses radiolabelled Vitamin B12 (57Co-B12): Stage I measures oral B12 absorption alone; Stage II adds intrinsic factor (IF). Low urinary 57Co-B12 excretion in Stage I corrected by IF in Stage II confirms IF deficiency = pernicious anaemia (autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells). IDA is diagnosed by serum ferritin, iron studies, and TIBC — not the Schilling test.
Alpha haemolysis on blood agar indicates:

Alpha (α) haemolysis = partial lysis of surrounding RBCs, producing a greenish-brown discolouration around colonies due to hydrogen peroxide-mediated oxidation of haemoglobin to methaemoglobin (e.g., Streptococcus pneumoniae, viridans streptococci). Beta (β) haemolysis = complete, clear transparent lysis zone (S. pyogenes, S. agalactiae). Gamma (γ) haemolysis = no lysis, no change around colonies.
Direct diagnosis assay for virus identification is, except:

The EXCEPT answer is ELISA. Immunofluorescence (IF) directly detects viral antigens inside infected cells using labelled antibodies — it identifies the virus itself. PCR (including RT-PCR) directly amplifies and detects viral nucleic acid (DNA/RNA) from specimens. ELISA, by contrast, is an INDIRECT serological test — it detects the patient's antibodies produced in response to viral infection (i.e., it detects the host immune response, not the virus directly). Therefore ELISA is the exception among direct diagnostic methods.
ANA is found in case of:

ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) are the hallmark autoantibodies of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) — sensitivity >95%, making it the best SLE screening test. Specific subtypes (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm) are highly specific for SLE. CML (chronic myeloid leukaemia) is a BCR-ABL1 myeloproliferative neoplasm not associated with ANA. IMN (infectious mononucleosis, EBV) can rarely produce transient non-specific ANA but this is not a defining or diagnostically relevant finding.
Patient with anti-B antibodies in his serum will have blood group:

By Landsteiner's Law, individuals naturally produce ABO antibodies against the ABO antigens they do NOT express on their RBCs. Anti-B antibodies are present in Group A individuals (who have A antigens but not B antigens → produce anti-B). Group B individuals have anti-A; Group AB have neither anti-A nor anti-B; Group O have both anti-A and anti-B. Anti-B alone = Blood Group A.
Which is true about sickle cell anemia:

All three statements are accurate about SCA. (1) A single nucleotide substitution (GAG→GTG) occurs at codon 6 of the HBB gene. (2) This replaces the hydrophilic glutamic acid with hydrophobic valine, causing HbS polymerisation under deoxygenation. (3) Hb electrophoresis (alkaline/acid pH) or HPLC confirms the HbSS genotype, showing absent HbA and >90% HbS. All three = 'All above' (Rodak's Hematology, Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
Bence-Jones protein found in case of:

Bence-Jones proteins are free immunoglobulin light chains (kappa or lambda) produced in excess by malignant plasma cells in Multiple Myeloma. Their small molecular weight allows filtration through the glomerulus into urine, detected by urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP) or immunofixation. 'Chronic/acute myeloid myeloma' are not recognised medical entities — Multiple Myeloma is a plasma cell dyscrasia (WHO Classification of Haematological Malignancies).
Eosinophilia found in:

Both food allergy (IgE-mediated hypersensitivity triggering eosinophil recruitment via IL-5 and eotaxin) and atopic dermatitis (type 2 hypersensitivity with Th2-driven IL-4/IL-5/IL-13 overproduction) are well-established causes of peripheral and tissue eosinophilia. Option C was missing from the original exam paper, but 'All above' is correct for the listed options. Classic causes of eosinophilia: NAACP — Neoplasm, Asthma/Allergy, Addison's disease, Collagen vascular disease, Parasites.
Acute leukemia is characterized by:

All three statements accurately characterise acute leukaemia. ALL accounts for ~80% of childhood leukaemias (peak age 2–5 years, B-cell ALL most common). AML predominates in adults (>60% of adult acute leukaemia, median age 68 years). The pathognomonic feature of acute leukaemia is arrested maturation with accumulation of ≥20% immature blast cells in bone marrow (WHO 2022 criteria), overwhelming normal haematopoiesis.
Useful cholesterol is:

HDL (High-Density Lipoprotein) is the 'good/useful' cholesterol — it performs reverse cholesterol transport, scavenging excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues and arterial walls and returning it to the liver for excretion or recycling (via ABCA1/SR-B1 receptors). High HDL (>60 mg/dL) is an independent cardioprotective factor. LDL deposits cholesterol in arterial walls, driving atherogenesis. Chylomicrons transport dietary (exogenous) lipids from the intestine to peripheral tissues.
Which of the following organism is capsulated:

Klebsiella pneumoniae possesses an exceptionally large, prominent polysaccharide capsule — one of the most heavily capsulated bacteria in clinical microbiology. The capsule is its primary virulence factor, conferring resistance to phagocytosis, complement-mediated killing, and antibiotic penetration. Colonies appear mucoid ('string test' positive) on agar. Shigella spp. are non-capsulated. Salmonella spp. have only the Vi antigen in S. typhi. Klebsiella is the definitive answer here. (Only three options were listed in the original question.)
Major cross matching is:

Major crossmatch = Donor RBCs + Recipient (patient) serum — this is the most critical compatibility test, detecting antibodies in the patient's serum (including ABO isoantibodies and unexpected alloantibodies) that could react against donor RBCs and cause an acute haemolytic transfusion reaction. Minor crossmatch = Donor serum + Recipient RBCs (detects donor antibodies against recipient antigens — less clinically critical). The major crossmatch must be performed before every transfusion.
First tube in CSF is for: (duplicate question)

Confirmed answer: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, total protein, LDH). The rationale is that chemical analytes are least affected by trace contamination from the needle insertion track. Tube 2 = Microbiology (culture); Tube 3 = Haematology (cell count). This sequence minimises the impact of any traumatic tap artefact on the diagnostically critical cell count and reduces skin flora contamination of the microbiological specimen. (Duplicate of Q10 in this exam — same answer.)
The best test to kidney function:

Creatinine clearance (from 24-hour urine collection or estimated using the CKD-EPI/MDRD equation incorporating serum creatinine, age, sex, and body size) is the best practical estimate of GFR — the gold standard measure of kidney filtration function. Creatinine is freely filtered and minimally secreted/reabsorbed. BUN is confounded by dietary protein, catabolism, GI bleeding, and hydration status; uric acid reflects purine metabolism, not filtration (KDIGO CKD Guidelines 2012/2024).
The most common type of anemia:

Iron Deficiency Anaemia (IDA) is the most common type of anaemia worldwide, affecting approximately 1.2 billion people (~50% of all anaemia cases globally, WHO Global Nutrition Report). It results from inadequate dietary iron intake, malabsorption, or chronic blood loss, leading to microcytic, hypochromic anaemia. Here, unusually, IDA is the only listed option — and it is unambiguously correct. (Previous exam versions listed only wrong options, requiring 'None of the above'; this version correctly lists IDA.)
ASO is done to diagnose of:

ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) detects antibodies against streptolysin O, a cytolytic exotoxin of Group A beta-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS/S. pyogenes). An elevated ASO titre (>200 IU/mL adults; >300 IU/mL children) supports recent GAS pharyngeal infection and is a criterion supporting the diagnosis of acute rheumatic fever and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. Rheumatoid Arthritis is diagnosed by Rheumatoid Factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies — not ASO.
Fluoride is added to prepare samples for:

Sodium fluoride (NaF) irreversibly inhibits the glycolytic enzyme enolase, halting in-vitro glucose consumption by erythrocytes and leukocytes in whole blood. Without NaF, blood glucose falls ~10–12 mg/dL per hour at room temperature, producing falsely low results. NaF/potassium oxalate grey-top tubes are the dedicated collection tube for blood glucose and lactate. NaF is not used for coagulation tests (require sodium citrate) or electrolyte measurement.
Which of the following has high affinity to bind with oxygen:

Myoglobin has the highest oxygen affinity among the options — its hyperbolic O₂ dissociation curve (P50 ~1–2 mmHg) reflects single haem-iron binding without cooperative allosteric effects. It holds O₂ tightly, facilitating O₂ storage and diffusion in muscle. Haemoglobin's sigmoidal curve (P50 ~26 mmHg) reflects cooperative binding across four subunits (Bohr effect), enabling efficient O₂ loading in lungs and unloading in tissues. Methaemoglobin (Fe³⁺) cannot bind O₂ at all.
Quality assurance include:

Quality Assurance (QA) in clinical laboratories is a comprehensive, system-wide approach encompassing ALL listed elements. Per ISO 15189 and CLIA regulations: Personal orientation and training (ensuring competency of staff), Laboratory documentation (SOPs, result records, audit trails, chain of custody), and Knowledge/maintenance of instrumentation (calibration, preventive maintenance, troubleshooting) are all mandatory QA components. QA covers pre-analytical, analytical, and post-analytical phases of the total testing process.

7.name: اختبار مزاولة المهنة - بكالوريوس - مختبرات - صنعاء - 07/09/2023
questions: Tumor marker CA15.3 used for:

CA15-3 (Cancer Antigen 15-3) is the primary serum tumour marker for monitoring breast cancer — used to assess treatment response and detect recurrence, NOT for initial screening. CA-125 = ovarian cancer; CEA = colorectal cancer; CA19-9 = pancreatic cancer; AFP = liver/germ cell tumours. Each marker is organ-specific and should not be used interchangeably.
Infection on the urinary tract infections (UTI) is most frequently associated with:

~80% of community-acquired UTIs are caused by Escherichia coli — a Gram-negative bacillus that is part of the normal intestinal (colonic) flora. It ascends from the perineum to colonise the urethra and bladder via fimbriae-mediated adhesion. Other intestinal Gram-negative flora causing UTIs include Klebsiella pneumoniae, Proteus mirabilis, and Enterobacter spp. (IDSA UTI Guidelines 2011/2019).
Which of the following is used to measure the function of thyroid gland:

Thyroid function is assessed by T3 (triiodothyronine), T4 (thyroxine), and TSH (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone). TSH is the most sensitive single screening test — it reflects pituitary feedback to circulating thyroid hormone levels. FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) is a gonadotropin controlling reproductive function, not thyroid function. 'A & B' (T3/T4 + TSH) is the correct combination. (Note: Option D was missing from the original question but implied as 'A & B'.)
Schilling test is done to diagnose of:

The Schilling test uses radiolabelled Vitamin B12 (57Co-B12) to assess intestinal B12 absorption. Stage I: oral 57Co-B12 alone; Stage II: oral 57Co-B12 + intrinsic factor (IF). Low urinary excretion in Stage I corrected by IF in Stage II confirms IF deficiency = pernicious anaemia (autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells). IDA is diagnosed by serum ferritin and iron studies; aplastic anaemia requires bone marrow biopsy.
The reservoir host for visceral leishmaniasis is:

For zoonotic visceral leishmaniasis (L. infantum/chagasi) endemic in the Mediterranean basin, Middle East, and Latin America, the domestic dog (Canis lupus familiaris) is the primary reservoir host. Phlebotomus/Lutzomyia sandflies transmit the parasite between reservoir dogs and humans. In the Indian subcontinent, L. donovani is anthroponotic (humans serve as the reservoir), but the standard teaching answer is dog.
The first collected tubes of CSF fluid is used for:

Standard CSF tube collection order: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, total protein, LDH, lactate) — minor contamination from the needle track has minimal impact on chemical analytes; Tube 2 = Microbiology (Gram stain, culture, sensitivity) — later collection reduces skin flora contamination; Tube 3 = Haematology/Cytology (cell count, differential) — last tube gives the most accurate WBC/RBC count, distinguishing traumatic tap from true subarachnoid haemorrhage.
The following are liver function test except:

ALP, AST, GGT, and Total albumin are ALL recognised components of the liver function test (LFT) panel. ALP reflects cholestasis/biliary obstruction and bone disease; AST reflects hepatocellular injury; GGT is the most sensitive marker for biliary disease and alcohol-induced liver injury; albumin assesses hepatic synthetic function (chronic liver disease depletes albumin). Since all four listed tests are genuine LFTs, 'None of the above' is the correct EXCEPT answer — there is no exception among the options given.
Acid fast bacterium is except:

Mycoplasma spp. completely lack a cell wall — they have no peptidoglycan and no mycolic acids, making it impossible to retain carbol fuchsin in the Ziehl-Neelsen stain. They are definitively NOT acid-fast. Mycobacterium spp. are the classical acid-fast bacteria (thick mycolic acid cell wall). Nocardia spp. are partially/weakly acid-fast (modified ZN stain). Therefore Mycoplasma alone is the correct 'except' answer — NOT 'B & C', because Nocardia IS acid-fast.
Na Fluoride is used to prepare sample for:

Sodium fluoride (NaF) irreversibly inhibits enolase, a key glycolytic enzyme, halting in-vitro glucose consumption by erythrocytes and leukocytes in whole blood. Without NaF, plasma glucose falls ~10–12 mg/dL per hour at room temperature. NaF/potassium oxalate grey-top tubes are dedicated for blood glucose and lactate testing. NaF is not used for electrolytes (plasma separator or serum tubes) or coagulation tests (require sodium citrate blue-top tubes).
Specific gravity of urine > 1.020 indicates:

Urine specific gravity >1.020 reflects maximal renal concentrating capacity triggered by elevated ADH secretion in response to reduced circulating fluid volume. All three conditions cause this: water restriction (reduced intake), dehydration (insensible or pathological fluid loss), and diarrhoea (GI fluid loss with compensatory renal water conservation). Normal SG: 1.005–1.030; maximally concentrated urine can reach 1.030–1.035.
An increased level in Gamma-GT gives an indication:

GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase) is elevated in all three conditions. Obstructive jaundice (extrahepatic biliary obstruction) and cholestasis (intra- or extrahepatic bile stasis) both induce hepatic GGT via bile acid reflux and microsomal enzyme induction. Hepatoma (hepatocellular carcinoma) elevates GGT from tumour cell necrosis and biliary involvement. GGT is the most sensitive liver enzyme for detecting biliary disease and alcohol abuse.
ASO test is done to diagnose of:

ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) detects antibodies against streptolysin O, an exotoxin of Group A beta-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS/S. pyogenes). Elevated ASO titre (>200 IU/mL adults) confirms recent GAS infection and supports diagnosis of post-streptococcal sequelae: acute rheumatic fever (a major Jones criteria supporting test) AND post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. Rheumatoid Arthritis is diagnosed by Rheumatoid Factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies — not ASO. 'B & C' is the correct answer.
G6PD enzyme found in:

G6PD is a ubiquitous enzyme present in all nucleated cells; however, among blood cells, its clinical significance is greatest in erythrocytes and thrombocytes (platelets). Clinically, G6PD deficiency primarily manifests in erythrocytes (which lack mitochondria and depend entirely on the pentose phosphate pathway for NADPH-based oxidative protection), but G6PD activity has also been documented in thrombocytes. 'B & C' (erythrocytes + thrombocytes) is the best available answer among the listed options. (Note: G6PD is technically present in leukocytes too, but they are less affected because they have mitochondria — making 'B & C' the intended exam answer.)
The final result (no growth) of blood culture give after:

Standard automated blood culture systems (BACTEC FX, BacT/ALERT 3D) continuously monitor and release 'No Growth' reports at 5 days (~1 week) for routine specimens — this is the CLSI M47 and international standard protocol. Extended incubation (up to 3–4 weeks) is a special protocol reserved exclusively for suspected fastidious organisms (Brucella spp., Bartonella spp., HACEK group) or culture-negative endocarditis. The default standard answer is 1 week.
Eosinophilia is found in:

All three conditions are well-established causes of eosinophilia. Food allergy (IgE-mediated, IL-5-driven eosinophil recruitment), atopic dermatitis (type 2 Th2 hypersensitivity with IL-4/IL-5/IL-13 overproduction), and drug hypersensitivity reactions (eosinophilia is a hallmark of DRESS syndrome — Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) all cause peripheral blood and tissue eosinophilia. Classic mnemonic: NAACP — Neoplasm, Asthma/Allergy, Addison's, Collagen vascular disease, Parasites.
Common cause of proteinuria is:

All three are recognised causes of proteinuria with distinct mechanisms. Diabetes mellitus causes glomerular hyperfiltration and basement membrane damage → persistent albuminuria (diabetic nephropathy — the most common cause globally). Bence-Jones proteins (free immunoglobulin light chains in multiple myeloma) are small enough to pass the glomerulus → overflow proteinuria. Prolonged fasting (>18 h) causes transient functional proteinuria via altered renal haemodynamics. 'All of the above' is therefore correct.
The most common type of anemia:

Iron Deficiency Anaemia (IDA) is the most common type of anaemia worldwide, affecting ~1.2 billion people and accounting for ~50% of all anaemia cases globally (WHO Global Anaemia Report 2021). It results from inadequate dietary iron, malabsorption (e.g., coeliac disease), or chronic blood loss (menorrhagia, GI bleeding), producing microcytic, hypochromic anaemia with low MCV, MCH, ferritin, and elevated TIBC.
Increase reticulocytes count in:

This question requires selecting ALL conditions associated with elevated reticulocyte count. Acute post-haemorrhagic anaemia and haemolytic anaemia both cause reactive reticulocytosis (bone marrow compensatory response). Iron deficiency anaemia shows elevated reticulocytes only AFTER iron supplementation begins (reticulocyte crisis). Vitamin B12 deficiency causes DECREASED reticulocytes (hypoproliferative, megaloblastic marrow). The best answer among the options is 'None of the above' only if the exam intended a single 'most correct' option — but if multiple answers are accepted, A and C are correct. The most classically correct single answer is haemolytic anemia (C), but since B12 deficiency is the one that is definitively NOT associated with reticulocytosis, the question may be poorly structured. Haemolytic anaemia is the textbook answer for 'highest reticulocytosis.'
Ferritin used for:

Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific marker of body iron stores. Low ferritin (<12 ng/mL) is virtually diagnostic of iron deficiency and is the first iron parameter to fall during depletion, preceding changes in serum iron, TIBC, and MCV. In haemolytic anaemia, ferritin is elevated (iron released from lysed RBCs). Ferritin has no diagnostic role in B12 deficiency, which is assessed by serum B12, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine (Tietz Fundamentals of Clinical Chemistry).
Urine output < 400 ml/24 hours is:

Oliguria is defined as urine output <400 mL/24 hours (or <0.5 mL/kg/hr in adults), indicating inadequate renal clearance of metabolic waste. It is a key diagnostic criterion for Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) stages 1–3 per KDIGO guidelines. Anuria = <100 mL/24 hrs (near-complete cessation); Polyuria = >3,000 mL/24 hrs (excess urine production as in diabetes insipidus or uncontrolled DM).
The more specific kidney function test:

Creatinine clearance (24-hour urine collection, or estimated by CKD-EPI/MDRD equation) provides the most specific and accurate estimate of GFR — the gold standard of kidney filtration function. Creatinine is freely filtered and minimally secreted/reabsorbed, making it a reliable GFR surrogate. BUN (urea nitrogen) is confounded by dietary protein, catabolism, GI bleeding, and hydration; uric acid reflects purine metabolism, not filtration capacity (KDIGO CKD Guidelines 2024).
Beta hemolysis on blood agar indicates for except:

Beta (β) haemolysis indicates COMPLETE lysis of surrounding RBCs, producing a clear transparent zone around colonies (e.g., Streptococcus pyogenes, S. agalactiae, Staphylococcus aureus). It does NOT mean partial or no haemolysis. Partial haemolysis = alpha (α) haemolysis (greenish zone, as in S. pneumoniae). No haemolysis = gamma (γ) haemolysis. Therefore 'partial haemolysis' is the EXCEPT — it is false for beta haemolysis.
The good hand disinfectant used against viruses:

Ethanol 70–85% is the WHO-recommended gold-standard hand disinfectant with proven broad-spectrum virucidal activity against enveloped viruses (SARS-CoV-2, influenza, HIV) and many non-enveloped viruses. Medical soap physically removes but does not reliably inactivate viruses. Diluted hypochlorite (bleach) is virucidal but corrosive to skin with repeated use — it is a surface disinfectant, not a hand rub. WHO Hand Hygiene Guidelines recommend alcohol-based hand rubs as first line for healthcare workers.
Sickling anemia is caused by the following:

All three statements accurately describe sickle cell anaemia (SCA). (1) A single nucleotide substitution (GAG→GTG) at codon 6 of the HBB (beta-globin) gene. (2) This replaces hydrophilic glutamic acid with hydrophobic valine, causing HbS polymerisation under deoxygenation and RBC sickling. (3) Hb electrophoresis (alkaline/acid pH) or HPLC confirms the HbSS genotype: absent HbA, >90% HbS, with HbF comprising the remainder. All three = 'All of the above' (Rodak's Hematology, Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
Bence-Jones protein found in case of:

Bence-Jones proteins are free monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains (kappa or lambda) secreted in excess by malignant plasma cells in Multiple Myeloma. Their low molecular weight (~22–44 kDa) allows passage through the glomerular filtration barrier into urine, where they are detected by urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP) or immunofixation electrophoresis. 'Acute/chronic myeloid myeloma' are not recognised medical entities — multiple myeloma is a plasma cell dyscrasia (WHO Classification of Haematological Malignancies).
One of the following has highest affinity to oxygen:

Myoglobin has the highest oxygen affinity (P50 ~1–2 mmHg) — its hyperbolic O₂ dissociation curve reflects a single haem-iron moiety with no cooperative allosteric interactions. It avidly binds and holds O₂, facilitating O₂ storage and diffusion in muscle tissue. Haemoglobin's sigmoidal curve (P50 ~26 mmHg) reflects cooperative binding across four subunits, allowing efficient O₂ loading in lungs and unloading in metabolically active tissues (Bohr effect). Methaemoglobin (Fe³⁺) cannot bind O₂ at all.
Laboratory equipment could be disinfected with:

All three are standard high-level disinfectants/sterilants for laboratory equipment. Formaldehyde/formalin (10%): used for gaseous fumigation of enclosed spaces and heat-sensitive instruments. Sodium hypochlorite (bleach 0.1–1%): broad-spectrum surface disinfectant for bench tops and non-critical equipment. Glutaraldehyde (2%): a high-level chemical sterilant effective against all microorganisms including endospores, used for heat-sensitive instruments (endoscopes, certain lab tools). All three are WHO/CDC-approved laboratory decontamination agents (WHO Laboratory Biosafety Manual, 4th ed. 2020).
An enteroviruses mainly infects:

Enteroviruses (Picornaviridae family, including poliovirus, coxsackievirus, echovirus, EV-D68) primarily infect the gastrointestinal tract (initial replication in the gut), central nervous system (aseptic meningitis, encephalitis, poliomyelitis), cardiac muscle (myocarditis, pericarditis), skin (hand-foot-and-mouth disease), and respiratory mucosa. They are NOT primarily hepatotropic, nephrotrophic, or pulmonary pathogens. 'None of the above' (kidney, liver, lung) is correct — the GI tract and CNS are the primary target organs.
A harmful cholesterol is carried except:

The EXCEPT answer is HDL. HDL (High-Density Lipoprotein) is the 'good/useful' cholesterol — it performs reverse cholesterol transport, scavenging excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues and arterial walls back to the liver for excretion, thereby protecting against atherosclerosis. LDL ('bad' cholesterol) deposits cholesterol in arterial intima, promoting atheromatous plaque formation and cardiovascular disease. Chylomicrons transport large amounts of dietary (exogenous) triglycerides and cholesterol from the intestine to peripheral tissues — elevated chylomicron remnants contribute to cardiovascular risk.
According to its gas requirements Mycobacterium leprae is:

Mycobacterium leprae is an obligate aerobe that preferentially colonises cooler, well-oxygenated peripheral tissues: skin, superficial peripheral nerves, nasal mucosa, and anterior chamber of the eye. It grows optimally at 27–30°C (below core body temperature), explaining its predilection for these peripheral structures. It cannot be cultured on artificial laboratory media and is grown experimentally in mouse footpads and nine-banded armadillos.
Treponema palladium is the causative agent of:

Treponema pallidum subsp. pallidum is the causative agent of syphilis — a sexually transmitted bacterial infection with primary (painless indurated chancre), secondary (disseminated rash, mucous patches), latent, and tertiary (gummas, neurosyphilis, cardiovascular syphilis) stages. Gonorrhoea = Neisseria gonorrhoeae; TB = Mycobacterium tuberculosis; AIDS = HIV. (Note: Correct spelling is 'pallidum', not 'palladium'.)
Gonorrhea is diagnosed by:

RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin — note: written as 'PRP' in original, likely a typo), VDRL, and Wassermann test are ALL serological tests for syphilis (Treponema pallidum), not gonorrhoea. Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) is correctly diagnosed by: Gram stain of urethral/cervical discharge (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci within PMNs), culture on Thayer-Martin/chocolate agar, or NAAT/PCR — the most sensitive modern method recommended by CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines.
The reservoir host of toxoplasma:

Domestic and wild cats (Felidae) are the definitive host of Toxoplasma gondii — the only hosts in which the parasite completes its sexual reproductive cycle, shedding environmentally-resistant oocysts in faeces. Humans and other warm-blooded animals (including rats, pigs, dogs) are intermediate hosts where only asexual tissue cysts (bradyzoites in muscle/brain) develop. Transmission to humans occurs via cat faeces (gardening, litter boxes) or consumption of undercooked meat containing tissue cysts.
Hot air oven is used for sterilization of except:

Hot air ovens use dry heat at 160–180°C for 1–2 hours — suitable for heat-stable, moisture-insensitive items: borosilicate glassware, metal instruments/utensils, oils, and powders. Catheters (rubber, latex, or PVC/silicone) and rubber gloves cannot withstand these temperatures and would melt, warp, degrade, or lose integrity. These items require low-temperature sterilisation methods: ethylene oxide (EtO) gas, gamma irradiation, or hydrogen peroxide plasma. 'B & D' is therefore the correct EXCEPT answer.
Which of the following is gram negative bacilli capsulated bacteria:

Klebsiella pneumoniae is the definitive answer: it is a Gram-negative bacillus (rod) with an exceptionally prominent polysaccharide capsule — one of the most heavily encapsulated pathogens in clinical microbiology. The capsule produces mucoid colonies and a positive 'string test.' It is the primary virulence factor conferring resistance to phagocytosis and complement killing. Salmonella spp. are non-capsulated (S. typhi has the Vi antigen, not a true capsule). Neisseria meningitidis is a Gram-negative COCCUS (diplococcus), not a bacillus, though it is capsulated.
HbA1c in patient with diabetes mellitus is important for:

HbA1c reflects the cumulative glycation of haemoglobin over the lifespan of red blood cells (~120 days), providing an integrated measure of mean blood glucose over the preceding 2–3 months. It is the gold standard for long-term glycaemic monitoring in diabetes. The ADA recommends HbA1c testing every 3 months for poorly controlled patients and every 6 months for well-controlled patients. Target HbA1c for most non-pregnant adults with DM: <7.0% (ADA Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes 2024).
Semen contains a sugar source for the spermatozoa energy that is:

The primary energy substrate in seminal plasma for spermatozoa is fructose, secreted exclusively by the seminal vesicles. Spermatozoa metabolise fructose via glycolysis to generate ATP for flagellar motility. Normal seminal fructose: >13 µmol/ejaculate (WHO 2021 semen analysis criteria). Absence of seminal fructose (fructose-negative semen) indicates bilateral seminal vesicle aplasia or complete ejaculatory duct obstruction, causing obstructive azoospermia.
H.ducrey agent causes:

Haemophilus ducreyi is the causative agent of chancroid (soft chancre) — a painful, non-indurated, undermined genital ulcer with tender suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy (bubo formation). It must be distinguished from the painless, indurated hard chancre of primary syphilis (Treponema pallidum). Diagnosis: PCR is most sensitive; culture on enriched GC-based media with special supplements (CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines). Note: Correct spelling is 'ducreyi.'
In emergency case what is the best blood group that transfused Safely:

O Rh-negative ('universal donor') red cells lack all ABO antigens (no risk of ABO-incompatible haemolytic reaction) AND the Rh(D) antigen (preventing Rh alloimmunisation in Rh-negative recipients, especially females of childbearing age). O Rh-positive red cells would be unsafe for Rh-negative patients. AB Rh-positive is the universal plasma/FFP donor. Full pretransfusion compatibility testing is always preferred when time permits (AABB Technical Manual 21st ed.).
The ANA test is used for detection of:

ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) are the hallmark autoantibodies of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) — sensitivity >95% for SLE, making it the best initial screening test. Specific subtypes (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm) are highly specific for SLE and correlate with disease activity. AML (acute myeloid leukaemia) is a haematological malignancy not associated with ANA. IMN (infectious mononucleosis, EBV) may produce transient non-specific ANA but this is not a defining or diagnostically relevant association.
In a major cross matching the following is applied:

Major crossmatch = Donor RBCs + Recipient (patient) serum — the most clinically critical compatibility test, detecting antibodies in the patient's serum (ABO isoantibodies and unexpected alloantibodies) that may react against donor RBCs, causing an acute haemolytic transfusion reaction. Minor crossmatch = Donor serum + Recipient RBCs. The major crossmatch is mandatory before every red cell transfusion (AABB Technical Manual).
A direct diagnostic assay for virus identification is except:

Both ELISA and Western blot are INDIRECT (serological) assays — they detect patient-generated antibodies produced in response to viral infection, not the virus itself. IF (Immunofluorescence) directly detects viral antigens within infected cells using fluorescently-labelled virus-specific antibodies. PCR (RT-PCR) directly amplifies and detects viral nucleic acid (DNA/RNA) from clinical specimens. Therefore ELISA AND Western blot are both exceptions to 'direct diagnostic assay.' If only one answer is expected, ELISA is the primary exam answer (Western blot is also indirect but may be considered confirmatory serology).
Acute leukemia is characterized by:

All three statements correctly characterise acute leukaemia. ALL (Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia) accounts for ~80% of childhood leukaemias (peak age 2–5 years). AML (Acute Myeloid Leukaemia) predominates in adults (>60% of adult acute leukaemia, median age 68 years). The pathognomonic hallmark of ALL acute leukaemias is arrested maturation with ≥20% blast cells in bone marrow (WHO 2022 criteria), overwhelming normal haematopoiesis. 'All of the above' = E.
Widal test is used to diagnose:

The Widal test detects agglutinating antibodies (IgM/IgG) against the O (somatic LPS) and H (flagellar) antigens of Salmonella typhi and S. paratyphi A/B/C, supporting the diagnosis of enteric (typhoid) fever. Typhus fever (caused by Rickettsia spp.) is diagnosed by the Weil-Felix test or specific Rickettsia serology. Undulant fever (brucellosis, caused by Brucella spp.) is diagnosed by Brucella agglutination tests (Rose Bengal, SAT) or blood culture. Widal = typhoid only.
The Normal value of bilirubin is:

Normal total serum bilirubin per Tietz and WHO reference ranges: 0.2–1.2 mg/dL. 'Less than 1 mg/dl' most accurately approximates this upper limit of normal. '1–2 mg/dl' is above normal (jaundice becomes clinically visible at ~2–3 mg/dL). 'Less than 2 mg/dl' is too permissive — it includes values that are already above the normal upper limit of 1.2 mg/dL. Note: direct (conjugated) bilirubin normal <0.3 mg/dL; indirect (unconjugated) <0.8 mg/dL.
According to gas requirement Salmonella is:

Salmonella spp. (S. typhi, S. typhimurium, S. enteritidis) are Gram-negative facultative anaerobic bacilli (rods) — they can grow in both the presence and absence of oxygen, preferring aerobic conditions but capable of anaerobic growth. This allows them to survive in the anaerobic gut environment, penetrate the intestinal mucosa, and disseminate systemically. They are members of Enterobacteriaceae, all of which are Gram-negative facultative anaerobes.
Patient with anti-B antibody in his serum will have blood group:

By Landsteiner's Law, ABO antibodies are naturally produced against ABO antigens that an individual does NOT express on their own RBCs. Anti-B antibodies are present in Group A individuals (who express only A antigens → produce anti-B). Group B individuals produce anti-A; Group AB individuals produce neither anti-A nor anti-B; Group O individuals produce both anti-A and anti-B. Anti-B alone in serum = Blood Group A.
