questions: AFB is, except:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Mycoplasma spp. is the only true exception: it completely lacks a cell wall (no peptidoglycan, no mycolic acids) and therefore cannot retain carbol fuchsin in the Ziehl-Neelsen stain — it is definitively NOT acid-fast. Mycobacterium spp. are the classical acid-fast bacteria. Nocardia spp. are partially/weakly acid-fast (modified ZN stain). 'B+C' is wrong because Nocardia IS acid-fast. Mycoplasma alone (B) is the correct EXCEPT.
The final result of blood culture give after:

⚠️ CONTESTED QUESTION — exam answer key marks 3 weeks. Scientific analysis: Standard automated blood culture systems (BACTEC FX, BacT/ALERT) report 'No Growth' at 5 days (~1 week) for routine specimens per CLSI M47. However, this exam's official answer is 3 weeks, reflecting extended-incubation protocol for fastidious organisms (Brucella spp., HACEK, Bartonella spp.) common in the Yemeni clinical context. Accept 3 weeks per this institution's official answer key.
ASO test is done to diagnose of:

❌ Student answer A+B is SCIENTIFICALLY INCORRECT. ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) detects antibodies against streptolysin O of Group A Streptococcus (GAS) and supports the diagnosis of post-GAS sequelae: acute rheumatic fever (B) AND post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (C). Rheumatoid Arthritis (A) is an autoimmune joint disease diagnosed by Rheumatoid Factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies — ASO has NO role in RA diagnosis. The scientifically correct answer is D (B+C). This is one of the most common exam traps in this subject.
Specific gravity of urine > 1.020 indicates:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Urine SG >1.020 reflects maximal renal concentrating response to reduced intravascular volume and elevated ADH secretion. All three conditions cause this: water restriction (reduced oral intake), dehydration (insensible/pathological fluid loss), and diarrhoea (GI fluid loss triggering compensatory renal water conservation). Normal SG range: 1.005–1.030.
Infection on the urinary tract infections (UTI) is most frequently associated with:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. ~80% of community-acquired UTIs are caused by Escherichia coli, a Gram-negative bacillus from normal intestinal (colonic) flora that ascends from the perineum to colonise the urethra and bladder via fimbriae. Other intestinal Gram-negative pathogens include Klebsiella pneumoniae, Proteus mirabilis, and Enterobacter spp. (IDSA UTI Guidelines).
Eosinophilia is found in:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. All three cause eosinophilia: food allergy (IgE-mediated, IL-5-driven eosinophil recruitment), atopic dermatitis (type 2 Th2 hypersensitivity with IL-4/IL-5/IL-13 overproduction), and drug hypersensitivity reactions including DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms). Classic mnemonic: NAACP — Neoplasm, Asthma/Allergy, Addison's, Collagen vascular disease, Parasites.
First collected tubes of CSF fluid is used for:

⚠️ No student checkmark on this question. Correct answer is B (Chemistry). Standard CSF collection: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, protein, LDH) — minor needle-track contamination minimally affects chemical analytes; Tube 2 = Microbiology (Gram stain, culture) — later collection reduces skin flora contamination; Tube 3 = Haematology (cell count, differential) — last tube gives most accurate WBC/RBC count distinguishing traumatic tap from true haemorrhage.
The most common type of anemia:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Iron Deficiency Anaemia (IDA) is the most common anaemia worldwide, affecting ~1.2 billion people and comprising ~50% of all anaemia cases globally (WHO 2021). Results from inadequate dietary iron, malabsorption, or chronic blood loss, producing microcytic, hypochromic anaemia with low MCV, MCH, serum iron, ferritin, and elevated TIBC/transferrin.
Common cause of proteinuria is:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Diabetes mellitus is the leading cause of clinically significant, persistent proteinuria worldwide (diabetic nephropathy), responsible for ~40% of end-stage renal disease globally. Hyperglycaemia causes glomerular hyperfiltration and basement membrane thickening → progressive albuminuria. Fasting and Bence-Jones protein are less common/secondary causes.
G6PD enzyme found in:

✅ Student answer CORRECT per exam key. G6PD is biochemically present in all cells, but among blood cell populations, its clinical significance is most notable in erythrocytes (which depend entirely on the pentose phosphate pathway for NADPH-based oxidative protection since they lack mitochondria) and thrombocytes. Leukocytes have mitochondria and are less clinically affected by G6PD deficiency. 'B+C' (erythrocytes + thrombocytes) is the intended exam answer.
Decrease reticulocytes count in:

⚠️ Student marked B (Iron deficiency anemia) — PARTIALLY CORRECT but the more classic textbook answer is C (Vitamin B12 deficiency). In megaloblastic (B12/folate deficiency) anaemia, impaired DNA synthesis causes hypoproliferative marrow with definitively low reticulocyte count (low reticulocyte index). In IDA, reticulocytes are low-normal but not classically 'decreased' — they rise dramatically with iron therapy (reticulocyte crisis). Acute haemorrhagic and haemolytic anaemias cause reactive reticulocytosis (elevated). If this exam key marks B, accept it; the classical teaching answer is C.
Ferritin used for:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific marker of body iron stores — it is the first parameter to fall during iron depletion, before serum iron, TIBC, or MCV change. Low ferritin (<12 ng/mL) is virtually diagnostic of IDA. In haemolytic anaemia ferritin is elevated (iron released from lysed RBCs). Ferritin has no diagnostic role in B12 deficiency, which requires serum B12, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine levels.
Urine output < 400 ml/24 hours is:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Oliguria = urine output <400 mL/24 hours (or <0.5 mL/kg/hr), a key diagnostic criterion for Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) per KDIGO guidelines. Anuria = <100 mL/24 hrs (near-complete cessation); Polyuria = >3,000 mL/24 hrs (as in diabetes insipidus, uncontrolled DM, or excess IV fluids).
Beta hemolysis on blood agar indicates for except:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Beta (β) haemolysis = COMPLETE, clear transparent lysis of surrounding RBCs (e.g., S. pyogenes, S. agalactiae, S. aureus). It does NOT mean partial lysis (= alpha haemolysis) or no lysis (= gamma haemolysis). Therefore both 'partial haemolysis' (A) and 'no haemolysis' (C) are EXCEPT answers — 'A and C' is the correct combined exception.
The good hand disinfectant used against viruses is:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Ethanol 70–85% is the WHO-recommended gold-standard hand disinfectant with proven broad-spectrum virucidal activity against enveloped viruses (SARS-CoV-2, influenza, HIV) and many non-enveloped viruses. Medical soap physically removes but does not reliably inactivate viruses. Hypochlorite (bleach) is virucidal but too corrosive for repeated skin application — it is a surface, not a hand, disinfectant.
Sickling anemia is caused by the following, except:

❌ Student answer D (A+C) is SCIENTIFICALLY INCORRECT. All three options (A, B, C) are TRUE and accurate statements about sickle cell anaemia: A = point mutation at codon 6 of HBB gene (GAG→GTG) is correct; B = Hb electrophoresis/HPLC is the gold-standard diagnostic method; C = glutamate replaced by valine is the molecular consequence of the mutation. Since ALL listed options are true, NONE should be 'excepted.' The correct answer is E ('All of the above' in the sense that no exceptions exist), but as a well-designed EXCEPT question the answer is that no valid EXCEPT exists here. This question is poorly constructed — flag it as problematic.
Bence-Jones protein found in case of:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Bence-Jones proteins are free monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains (kappa or lambda) produced in excess by malignant plasma cells in Multiple Myeloma. Their low molecular weight allows passage through the glomerular filtration barrier into urine, detected by urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP) or immunofixation. 'Chronic/acute myeloid myeloma' are not recognised medical entities.
One of the following has highest affinity to oxygen:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Myoglobin has the highest O₂ affinity (P50 ~1–2 mmHg) — its hyperbolic dissociation curve reflects a single haem moiety with no cooperative allosteric interactions, allowing it to hold O₂ tightly for muscle O₂ storage. Haemoglobin's sigmoidal curve (P50 ~26 mmHg) is designed for cooperative O₂ loading/unloading. Methaemoglobin (Fe³⁺) cannot bind O₂ at all.
CA19.9 marker used for except:

❌ Student answer E (None of the above) is INCORRECT. The correct EXCEPT is B (hepatocellular cancer). CA19-9 is a recognised tumour marker for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (primary use), cholangiocarcinoma, and colorectal cancer — options A and C are TRUE uses. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is diagnosed using AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) as its primary marker, not CA19-9. Therefore B (hepatocellular cancer) is the correct exception. 'None of the above' (E) would mean there are no exceptions, which is factually wrong.
Laboratory equipment could be disinfected with:

⚠️ Student marked A (Hypochlorite only) but the scientifically complete answer is D (All of the above). All three are WHO/CDC-approved laboratory disinfectants: Sodium hypochlorite (0.1–1%) = broad-spectrum surface disinfectant; Formaldehyde/formalin = gaseous fumigation of biosafety cabinets and enclosed spaces; Glutaraldehyde (2%) = high-level chemical sterilant for heat-sensitive instruments (endoscopes, certain lab tools). Selecting only hypochlorite is incomplete — D is the superior answer.
An enterovirus mainly infects:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Enteroviruses (poliovirus, coxsackievirus, echovirus, EV-D68 — family Picornaviridae) replicate initially in the intestinal mucosa (including the colon/GI tract), which is their primary portal of entry and initial replication site before systemic spread. They subsequently target the CNS (aseptic meningitis, poliomyelitis), cardiac muscle (myocarditis), and skin. 'Colon' as part of the intestinal tract is the best available answer among the listed options.
A harmful cholesterol is carried except:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. The EXCEPT is HDL. HDL ('good' cholesterol) performs reverse cholesterol transport, removing excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues and arterial walls back to the liver — it is ANTI-atherogenic. LDL ('bad' cholesterol) deposits cholesterol in arterial intima, driving atheromatous plaque formation. Chylomicrons transport large amounts of dietary triglycerides and cholesterol to peripheral tissues — elevated chylomicron remnants contribute to cardiovascular risk.
According to its gas requirements Mycobacterium leprae is:

⚠️ No student checkmark. Correct answer: B (obligate aerobic). Mycobacterium leprae is an obligate aerobe that preferentially colonises cooler, well-oxygenated peripheral structures: skin, superficial peripheral nerves, nasal mucosa, and anterior chamber of the eye. It grows optimally at 27–30°C (below core body temperature). It cannot be cultured on artificial media; grown experimentally in armadillos and mouse footpads.
Marker CA15.3 used for:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. CA15-3 (Cancer Antigen 15-3) is the primary serum tumour marker for monitoring breast cancer — used to assess treatment response and detect recurrence, NOT for initial screening. CA-125 = ovarian; CEA = colorectal; CA19-9 = pancreatic; AFP = liver/germ cell tumours.
Treponema pallidum is the causative agent of:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Treponema pallidum subsp. pallidum causes syphilis — a sexually transmitted spirochaetal infection with primary (painless indurated chancre), secondary (disseminated rash), latent, and tertiary stages (gummas, neurosyphilis, cardiovascular syphilis). Gonorrhoea = Neisseria gonorrhoeae; TB = Mycobacterium tuberculosis; AIDS = HIV.
Gonorrhea is diagnosed by:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. RPR, VDRL, and Wassermann test are ALL serological tests for syphilis (Treponema pallidum), not gonorrhoea. Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) is correctly diagnosed by: Gram stain of urethral/cervical discharge (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci within PMNs), culture on Thayer-Martin/chocolate agar, or NAAT/PCR — the most sensitive modern method (CDC 2021 STI Guidelines).
The reservoir host of toxoplasma:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Domestic and wild cats (Felidae) are the DEFINITIVE host of Toxoplasma gondii — the only hosts in which the parasite completes its sexual reproductive cycle, producing and shedding environmentally-resistant oocysts in faeces. Humans, rodents, pigs, and dogs are INTERMEDIATE hosts where only asexual tissue cysts (bradyzoites) develop. Human transmission: cat faeces (gardening/litter boxes) or ingestion of undercooked meat containing tissue cysts.
ASO test is done to diagnose of (duplicate Q3):

⚠️ No student checkmark (duplicate of Q3). Correct answer is D (B+C = Rheumatic fever + Glomerulonephritis). ASO detects antibodies against streptolysin O of Group A Streptococcus, supporting post-GAS sequelae: acute rheumatic fever AND post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. ASO does NOT diagnose Rheumatoid Arthritis (option A) — that requires RF and anti-CCP. Note: This is the same question as Q3 where the student incorrectly marked A+B. The correct answer is B+C.
The reservoir host for visceral leishmaniasis is:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. For zoonotic visceral leishmaniasis (L. infantum/chagasi) endemic in the Mediterranean, Middle East, and Latin America, the domestic dog is the primary reservoir host. Phlebotomus/Lutzomyia sandflies transmit the parasite between infected dogs and humans. In the Indian subcontinent L. donovani is anthroponotic (humans = reservoir), but 'dog' is the standard teaching answer.
The following are liver function test, except:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. ALP, AST, GGT, and Total albumin are ALL legitimate components of the liver function test (LFT) panel. ALP = cholestasis/biliary obstruction and bone disease; AST = hepatocellular injury; GGT = most sensitive marker for biliary disease and alcohol-induced liver injury; Total albumin = hepatic synthetic function (depleted in chronic liver disease). Since all listed options ARE liver function tests, 'None of the above' (E) is the correct EXCEPT answer.
Na Fluoride is used to prepare sample except:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Sodium fluoride (NaF) is used ONLY for blood glucose and lactate testing (inhibits enolase, preventing in-vitro glycolysis). It is NOT used for electrolyte measurement (plasma separator/serum tubes) or coagulation tests (require sodium citrate blue-top tubes). Therefore B (electrolytes) and C (coagulation) are both exceptions = 'B+C' is the correct answer.
An increased level in Gamma-GT gives an indication for:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase) is elevated in all three conditions. Obstructive jaundice (extrahepatic biliary obstruction) and cholestasis (intra/extrahepatic bile stasis) both induce hepatic GGT via bile acid reflux and microsomal enzyme induction. Hepatoma (HCC) elevates GGT from tumour cell necrosis and biliary involvement. GGT is the most sensitive but least specific liver enzyme.
Hot air oven is used for sterilization of except:

❌ Student answer D (A+C = glassware + metal utensils) is SCIENTIFICALLY INCORRECT. Glassware (A) and metal utensils (C) are both APPROPRIATE and standard items for hot air oven sterilisation at 160–180°C. They are NOT exceptions. Catheters (B) are the true exception — they are made of rubber, latex, silicone, or PVC, which melt or degrade at these temperatures. Catheters require EtO gas or gamma irradiation. The correct answer should be B (catheters only). The exam's official mark of A+C appears to be an error.
A direct diagnostic assay for virus identification is, except:

⚠️ Student marked D (Western blot) — PARTIALLY CORRECT but ELISA is also indirect. Scientific analysis: IF (immunofluorescence) directly detects viral antigens in infected cells; PCR directly detects viral nucleic acid. Both ELISA and Western blot are INDIRECT serological tests — they detect patient-generated antibodies against the virus (not the virus itself). If only one EXCEPT is required, ELISA is the more classic exam answer; Western blot is also indirect but is used as a confirmatory serology test. Flag: both A (ELISA) and D (Western blot) are indirect methods.
The following statements are true about viruses, except:

❌ Student answer E (None of the above) is INCORRECT. The correct EXCEPT is A. Statement A — 'Contain DNA and RNA' — is FALSE: viruses contain only ONE type of nucleic acid (either DNA OR RNA, never both simultaneously). This is a fundamental characteristic of viruses. Statement B (size 10–300 nm) is approximately true. Statement C (metabolically inactive) is true — viruses have no metabolic machinery of their own. Therefore A is the exception, not 'None of the above.'
Acute leukemia is characterized by:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. All three statements accurately characterise acute leukaemia: ALL accounts for ~80% of childhood leukaemias (peak 2–5 years); AML predominates in adults (>60% of adult acute leukaemia, median age 68 years); the defining hallmark is arrested maturation with ≥20% blast cells in bone marrow (WHO 2022 criteria), overwhelming normal haematopoiesis.
Which of the following is gram negative bacilli capsulated bacteria:

⚠️ Student marked D (B and C) — PARTIALLY CORRECT but scientifically imprecise. Klebsiella pneumoniae (C) is a Gram-negative BACILLUS (rod) with a large polysaccharide capsule — unambiguously correct. Neisseria meningitidis (B) is capsulated but is a Gram-negative COCCUS (diplococcus), NOT a bacillus. The question specifically asks for 'gram-negative bacilli (rods) capsulated' — so B is excluded by morphology. The precise answer should be C (Klebsiella) only. D (B+C) is the exam's official answer but is morphologically imprecise regarding Neisseria.
HbA1c in patient with Diabetes mellitus is important for:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. HbA1c reflects glycation of haemoglobin over the RBC lifespan (~120 days), providing an integrated measure of mean blood glucose over the preceding 2–3 months. It is the gold standard for long-term glycaemic monitoring. ADA target for most non-pregnant adults with DM: <7.0%. Testing frequency: every 3 months (poorly controlled) or every 6 months (well-controlled) (ADA Standards of Medical Care 2024).
Semen contains a sugar source for the spermatozoa energy that is:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. The primary energy substrate in seminal plasma for spermatozoa is FRUCTOSE, secreted by the seminal vesicles (normal: >13 µmol/ejaculate per WHO 2021 criteria). Fructose is not listed among options A–D (lactose, maltose, dextrose, sucrose), so 'None of the above' (E) is correct. Absence of seminal fructose indicates bilateral seminal vesicle aplasia or ejaculatory duct obstruction.
H. ducreyi agent causes:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Haemophilus ducreyi causes chancroid (soft chancre) — a painful, non-indurated, necrotic genital ulcer with tender suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy (bubo). Distinguished from primary syphilis which produces a painless, indurated hard chancre. Diagnosis: PCR (most sensitive) or culture on enriched GC-based media with special supplements (CDC 2021 STI Guidelines).
The following is hookworm:

⚠️ No student checkmark. Correct answer: B (Ancylostoma duodenale). Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus are the two human hookworms — attaching to intestinal mucosa, causing chronic blood loss and iron deficiency anaemia. Ascaris lumbricoides = large roundworm (nematode, not a hookworm). Enterobius vermicularis = pinworm. Hookworm = Ancylostoma duodenale (B).
In emergency safely: what is the best blood group that is transfused:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. O Rh-negative RBCs are the 'universal donor' — lacking all ABO antigens (no risk of ABO-incompatible haemolytic reaction) AND the Rh(D) antigen (preventing Rh alloimmunisation in Rh-negative recipients, especially females of childbearing age). O Rh-positive is unsafe for Rh-negative patients. AB Rh-positive is the universal plasma/FFP donor. Full pretransfusion compatibility testing is always preferred when time permits.
The ANA test is used for detection of:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) are the hallmark autoantibodies of SLE — sensitivity >95%, making ANA the best initial screening test. Specific subtypes (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm) are highly specific for SLE. AML is a haematological malignancy with no ANA association. IMN (infectious mononucleosis, EBV) may produce transient non-specific ANA but this is not diagnostically relevant.
Schilling test is done to diagnose of:

✅ Student answer CORRECT (with nuance). The Schilling test measures intestinal Vitamin B12 absorption — low urinary 57Co-B12 corrected by intrinsic factor (IF) confirms IF deficiency = pernicious anaemia, which results in B12 deficiency. When 'pernicious anaemia' is not listed and 'B12 deficiency' is offered, B12 deficiency is the accepted answer, as pernicious anaemia is the primary cause of B12 malabsorption being tested. IDA requires ferritin/iron studies; aplastic anaemia requires bone marrow biopsy.
In a major cross matching, the following is applied:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Major crossmatch = Donor RBCs + Recipient (patient) serum — the most critical compatibility test, detecting antibodies in the patient's serum (ABO isoantibodies and unexpected alloantibodies) that may react against donor RBCs, causing an acute haemolytic transfusion reaction. Minor crossmatch = Donor serum + Recipient RBCs. The major crossmatch is mandatory before every red cell transfusion (AABB Technical Manual 21st ed.).
Fecal occult blood is used for:

⚠️ No student checkmark. Correct answer: D (All of the above). Fecal occult blood test (FOBT/FIT) detects hidden blood in stool from all three: hookworm infection (causes GI blood loss → iron deficiency anaemia detected by FOBT); iron deficiency anaemia workup (identifying occult GI blood loss as the cause); and rectal/colorectal carcinoma screening (tumour bleeding, recommended by USPSTF from age 45). All three are legitimate clinical indications for FOBT.
Which of the following is used to measure the function of thyroid gland:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Thyroid function is assessed by T3 (triiodothyronine), T4 (thyroxine), and TSH (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone). TSH is the most sensitive single screening test — it reflects pituitary feedback to circulating thyroid hormone levels. FSH is a gonadotropin controlling reproductive/gonadal function, not thyroid function. 'A+B' (T3/T4 + TSH) is the complete and correct combination.
Stem cell defined:

✅ Student answer CORRECT. Haematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) are classically defined by two core properties: (1) Self-renewal — the ability to undergo symmetric division producing daughter HSCs identical to the parent, maintaining the stem cell pool; and (2) Multipotency/Differentiation — the ability to give rise to all mature blood cell lineages. Proliferation (clonal expansion) enables haematopoietic output. All listed properties define stem cell biology. 'All of the above' is correct (Rodak's Hematology, Janeway's Immunobiology).

questions: An increased level in Gamma-GT gives an indication of:

GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase) is elevated in ALL three conditions. Obstructive jaundice (extrahepatic biliary obstruction) and intrahepatic cholestasis both elevate GGT via bile acid reflux and hepatic microsomal enzyme induction. Hepatoma (hepatocellular carcinoma) elevates GGT from tumour cell necrosis, biliary involvement, and paraneoplastic induction. GGT is the most sensitive liver enzyme for detecting biliary disease, alcohol abuse, and drug-induced hepatic injury — but the least specific. 'All of the above' (E) is correct.
The more specific kidney function marker is:

Creatinine clearance (from 24-hour urine collection or estimated by CKD-EPI/MDRD equation) is the most specific and accurate practical estimate of GFR — the gold standard of kidney filtration function. Creatinine is freely filtered by the glomerulus and minimally secreted/reabsorbed, making it a reliable marker specific to filtration. BUN (urea nitrogen) is confounded by dietary protein, catabolism, GI bleeding, and hydration status. Uric acid reflects purine metabolism, not filtration capacity (KDIGO CKD Guidelines 2024).
A common cause of proteinuria:

All three are recognised and distinct mechanisms of proteinuria. Diabetes mellitus causes glomerular hyperfiltration and basement membrane thickening → persistent albuminuria (diabetic nephropathy — the leading cause globally, ~40% of ESRD). Bence-Jones proteins (free immunoglobulin light chains in multiple myeloma) are small enough to pass the glomerulus → overflow proteinuria detected by urine immunofixation. Prolonged fasting (>18 h) causes transient functional proteinuria via altered renal haemodynamics and reduced proximal tubular reabsorption. 'All of the above' (D) is the correct comprehensive answer.
Na Fluoride is used to prepare samples for:

Sodium fluoride (NaF) is a specific glycolytic inhibitor (blocks enolase) used ONLY for blood glucose and lactate specimens. It prevents in-vitro cellular glucose consumption (~10–12 mg/dL/hour decline without NaF at room temperature). NaF is NOT used for: Blood count (EDTA lavender-top tube required); Electrolytes (plasma separator/serum tubes); Blood gas (heparinised arterial syringe). None of the listed options (blood count, electrolytes, blood gas) are correct uses of NaF — 'None of the above' (E) is the correct answer.
In diabetic patient the test belonging to profile is:

All three are components of the diabetic monitoring profile. HbA1c (glycated haemoglobin) reflects 2–3 month average glucose — gold standard for long-term glycaemic control. PPBS (Post-Prandial Blood Sugar, 2-hour post-meal glucose) assesses post-meal glycaemic excursions and is used for short-term monitoring. Fructosamine (glycated serum proteins) reflects 2–3 week average glucose — useful when HbA1c is unreliable (haemolytic anaemia, haemoglobinopathies, or pregnancy). Together they provide comprehensive short-, medium-, and long-term glycaemic assessment (ADA Standards 2024).
Procalcitonin marker inflammation used for:

Procalcitonin (PCT) is the most specific biomarker for BACTERIAL sepsis and systemic bacterial infection. It is produced by non-thyroidal cells (liver, monocytes, adipocytes) in response to bacterial endotoxins (LPS) and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6). PCT is characteristically low or suppressed in viral infections (interferons paradoxically suppress PCT synthesis) and only modestly elevated in fungal infections without bacterial co-infection. PCT-guided antibiotic stewardship protocols use it to distinguish bacterial from viral pneumonia and sepsis, guiding antibiotic initiation and cessation (IDSA/SCCM Sepsis Guidelines).
All the following is gram negative rod EXCEPT:

ALL four listed organisms are Gram-negative rods (bacilli): Salmonella spp. (Enterobacteriaceae, straight rod); Shigella spp. (Enterobacteriaceae, short rod); Klebsiella spp. (Enterobacteriaceae, plump rod with capsule); Campylobacter spp. (curved/comma-shaped or S-shaped rod, Epsilonproteobacteria). Since there is no exception among the listed options — all are genuinely Gram-negative rods — the correct EXCEPT answer is 'None of the above' (E).
The final result no growth of blood culture give after:

Standard answer: CLSI M47 and international automated blood culture protocols (BACTEC FX, BacT/ALERT 3D) report 'No Growth' at 5 days (~1 week) for routine specimens with continuous monitoring. This is the globally standardised default. Extended incubation (2–4 weeks) is a special protocol reserved exclusively for suspected fastidious organisms (Brucella spp., Bartonella spp., HACEK organisms) in culture-negative endocarditis workup. For a general exam question without specified context, '1 week' is the correct standard answer. (Note: Some Yemeni institutional exam keys have marked 3 weeks due to local Brucella prevalence — accept institution-specific guidance if provided.)
The first collected tube of CSF is used for:

Standard CSF collection tube order: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, total protein, LDH, lactate) — minor contamination from the needle track has negligible impact on chemical analytes; Tube 2 = Microbiology (Gram stain, culture, sensitivity) — later collection reduces skin flora contamination, improving culture specificity; Tube 3 = Haematology/Cytology (cell count, differential) — last tube gives the most accurate WBC/RBC count, distinguishing traumatic tap (RBCs decrease across tubes) from true subarachnoid haemorrhage (RBCs constant across all tubes).
HbA1c in diabetic patient is important for:

HbA1c (glycated haemoglobin) reflects the cumulative non-enzymatic glycation of haemoglobin over the lifespan of red blood cells (~120 days), providing an integrated measure of mean blood glucose over the preceding 2–3 months. It is the gold standard for long-term glycaemic monitoring in diabetes. ADA target: <7.0% for most non-pregnant adults. Testing frequency: every 3 months in poorly controlled patients; every 6 months when well-controlled. Short-term glycaemic assessment uses fasting glucose or PPBS; medium-term (2–3 weeks) uses fructosamine (ADA Standards of Medical Care 2024).
Gonorrhea can be diagnosed by:

RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin) and VDRL (Venereal Disease Research Laboratory) are non-treponemal serological tests used exclusively for syphilis (Treponema pallidum). They detect anticardiolipin (reagin) antibodies produced in syphilitic infection and are NOT relevant to gonorrhoea diagnosis. Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) is correctly diagnosed by: Gram stain of urethral/cervical discharge (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci within PMNs); culture on Thayer-Martin or chocolate agar; or NAAT/PCR — the most sensitive method recommended by CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines.
An Anti-Streptolysin O test is used to detect:

ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) detects antibodies against streptolysin O, a cytolytic exotoxin of Group A beta-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS/S. pyogenes). Elevated ASO titre (>200 IU/mL adults; >300 IU/mL children) confirms recent GAS pharyngeal infection and is used to support the diagnosis of post-GAS immunological sequelae: (B) acute rheumatic fever and (C) post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. Rheumatoid Arthritis (A) is an autoimmune joint disease diagnosed by Rheumatoid Factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies — ASO plays NO role in RA diagnosis. 'B and C' (D) is correct.
Acute leukemia is characterized by:

All three statements accurately characterise acute leukaemia. ALL (Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia) accounts for ~80% of childhood leukaemias (peak age 2–5 years, predominantly B-cell precursor type). AML (Acute Myeloid Leukaemia) predominates in adults (>60% of adult acute leukaemia, median age 68 years). The pathognomonic hallmark is arrested differentiation with accumulation of ≥20% immature blast cells in bone marrow (WHO 2022 classification), suppressing normal haematopoiesis and causing the clinical triad of anaemia, infection susceptibility, and bleeding.
In a major cross matching, the following is applied:

Major crossmatch = Donor RBCs + Recipient (patient) serum. This is the most clinically critical pretransfusion compatibility test, detecting antibodies in the patient's serum — including ABO isoantibodies and unexpected alloantibodies (anti-Kell, anti-Duffy, anti-Kidd, etc.) — that may react against donor RBCs and trigger an acute haemolytic transfusion reaction. Minor crossmatch = Donor serum + Recipient RBCs (detects donor antibodies against recipient antigens — less clinically critical since donor plasma is diluted). The major crossmatch is mandatory before every red cell transfusion (AABB Technical Manual 21st ed.).
Widal test is used for the diagnosis of:

The Widal test detects agglutinating antibodies (IgM/IgG) against the O (somatic LPS) and H (flagellar) antigens of Salmonella typhi and S. paratyphi A/B/C, supporting the diagnosis of enteric (typhoid) fever. Typhus fever (caused by Rickettsia spp.) is diagnosed by the Weil-Felix test or specific Rickettsia serology. Undulant fever (brucellosis, Brucella spp.) is diagnosed by Rose Bengal slide test, standard agglutination test (SAT), or blood culture. The Widal test is strictly for typhoid/enteric fever only.
An acid fast bacterium is EXCEPT:

Mycoplasma spp. is the definitive EXCEPT: it completely lacks a cell wall — no peptidoglycan, no mycolic acids — making it impossible to retain carbol fuchsin in the Ziehl-Neelsen stain. It is definitively NOT acid-fast. Mycobacterium spp. are the classical acid-fast bacteria (thick mycolic acid-rich cell wall retains dye). Nocardia spp. is partially/weakly acid-fast (modified Kinyoun ZN stain). 'A and C' is incorrect because Nocardia IS acid-fast. Only Mycoplasma (C) is the correct single EXCEPT answer.
In emergency, what is the best blood group that can be transfused safely:

O Rh-negative RBCs ('universal donor') lack ALL ABO antigens (eliminating ABO-incompatible haemolytic reaction risk) AND the Rh(D) antigen (preventing Rh alloimmunisation in Rh-negative recipients, critically important for females of childbearing potential). O Rh-positive would be unsafe for Rh-negative patients, particularly women. AB Rh-positive is the universal plasma/FFP donor. Full pretransfusion compatibility testing is always preferred when time permits. In true emergency (no time for testing), O Rh-negative packed RBCs are the only safe option (AABB Technical Manual 21st ed.).
ANA is found in case of:

ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) are the hallmark autoantibodies of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) — sensitivity >95%, making ANA by indirect immunofluorescence (IIF) the best initial SLE screening test. Specific ANA subtypes (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm) are highly specific for SLE and correlate with disease activity and renal involvement. CML (chronic myeloid leukaemia) is a BCR-ABL1-driven myeloproliferative neoplasm — not an autoimmune disease and not associated with ANA. IMN (infectious mononucleosis, EBV) may transiently produce non-specific ANA but this is not diagnostically relevant or clinically important.
Hot air oven is used for sterilization of EXCEPT:

Hot air ovens use dry heat at 160–180°C for 1–2 hours — appropriate for heat-stable, moisture-sensitive items: metal surgical instruments, borosilicate glassware, oils, powders, and sealed ampules. Catheters (made of rubber, latex, silicone, or PVC) CANNOT withstand these temperatures — they melt, deform, or lose their physical integrity. Catheters require low-temperature sterilisation: ethylene oxide (EtO) gas, hydrogen peroxide gas plasma, or gamma irradiation. Catheters (B) is the correct EXCEPT. 'A and B' is wrong because metal surgical instruments ARE appropriate for hot air ovens.
Schilling test is applied to diagnose:

The Schilling test diagnoses pernicious anaemia and Vitamin B12 malabsorption — neither of which is listed among the options. The test uses radiolabelled 57Co-B12: Stage I alone (low urinary excretion = malabsorption); Stage II + intrinsic factor (IF) correction = confirms IF deficiency = pernicious anaemia. IDA is diagnosed by serum ferritin, iron studies, and TIBC. Aplastic anaemia requires bone marrow biopsy and aspirate. Since the actual diagnosis (pernicious anaemia/B12 malabsorption) is absent from options A–C, 'None of the above' (D) is the correct answer.
Blood agar media is:

Blood agar (5% sheep blood in tryptic soy agar base) simultaneously serves all three functions. (1) Basic/general-purpose medium: supports growth of most non-fastidious organisms without selectivity. (2) Differential medium: haemolysis patterns (alpha/partial green, beta/complete clear, gamma/none) differentiate bacterial species, guiding presumptive identification. (3) Enriched medium: blood components (NAD, hemin, growth factors) support the growth of fastidious organisms (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae on CO₂-enriched plates, Listeria spp.). All three classifications apply — 'All of the above' (E) is correct.
Selective media EXCEPT:

The EXCEPT is chocolate agar (D). SS agar (Salmonella-Shigella), XLD agar (Xylose-Lysine-Deoxycholate), and Mannitol Salt Agar (7.5% NaCl) are all selective media — they contain inhibitory agents that suppress certain organisms while allowing target pathogens to grow. Chocolate agar (heat-lysed sheep blood releasing NAD/hemin) is an ENRICHED, NON-selective medium designed to support the growth of fastidious organisms (Haemophilus influenzae, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Neisseria meningitidis). It does not selectively inhibit any group of organisms and therefore is NOT a selective medium.
A good hand disinfectant used for viruses is:

Ethanol 70–85% is the WHO-recommended gold-standard hand disinfectant with proven broad-spectrum virucidal activity against enveloped viruses (SARS-CoV-2, influenza, HIV, hepatitis B) and many non-enveloped viruses (adenovirus, norovirus at higher concentrations). Medical soap mechanically removes but does not reliably inactivate viruses. Diluted hypochlorite (bleach) is an effective surface virucide but is corrosive to skin with repeated use — it is a surface disinfectant, NOT a hand rub. WHO Hand Hygiene Guidelines recommend alcohol-based hand rubs as first-line for healthcare workers.
Eosinophilia is seen in EXCEPT:

This question is structurally flawed — ALL three listed conditions (food allergy, atopic dermatitis, parasitic infection) are well-established and classic causes of eosinophilia, not exceptions. Food allergy (IgE-mediated, IL-5-driven eosinophil recruitment); atopic dermatitis (type 2 Th2 hypersensitivity with IL-4/IL-5/IL-13 overproduction); and parasitic infections, especially tissue-invasive helminths (the most potent cause of eosinophilia worldwide). None qualifies as an EXCEPT. The question lacks a 'None of the above' option and has no scientifically valid answer as written. Bacterial infections (not listed) would be the classic condition that does NOT cause eosinophilia. Flag as a flawed question.
What is the following direct test of HIV:

Direct HIV diagnostic assays detect the virus itself rather than the patient's immune response. P24 antigen (viral capsid protein) is detected directly in plasma by ELISA-based assays — rises within 14–18 days of infection. RNA PCR (HIV-1 RNA viral load by RT-qPCR) directly detects and quantifies viral RNA — rises within 10–12 days (earliest detectable marker). Western blot detects patient-generated anti-HIV antibodies (INDIRECT serological method — detects host immune response, not the virus). 'A and B' (C) is the correct answer. Note: Fourth-generation combination HIV tests detect both p24 antigen AND antibodies simultaneously.
Which of the following belongs to thyroid gland function:

Thyroid function is primarily assessed by T3 (triiodothyronine), T4 (thyroxine), and TSH (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone). TSH is the most sensitive single test — it reflects the anterior pituitary's feedback response to circulating thyroid hormone levels (elevated in hypothyroidism; suppressed in hyperthyroidism). Calcitonin is produced by thyroid parafollicular (C) cells but regulates calcium metabolism, not thyroid function per se — it is a tumour marker for medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC), not a thyroid function test. 'A+B' (D) is the correct answer.
The following are liver function test EXCEPT:

ALP, AST, GGT, and Total albumin are ALL recognised components of the liver function test (LFT) panel. ALP = cholestasis and biliary obstruction; AST = hepatocellular injury (also cardiac); GGT = biliary disease and alcohol-induced liver injury (most sensitive liver enzyme); Total albumin = hepatic synthetic function (depleted in chronic liver disease, indicating reduced hepatic protein synthesis). Since ALL listed options A–C AND option E (total albumin) are genuine LFTs, 'None of the above' (D) is the correct EXCEPT answer — there is no exception among the options given.
Marker CA15.3 used for:

CA15-3 (Cancer Antigen 15-3, MUC-1 mucin antigen) is the primary serum tumour marker for breast cancer — used to monitor treatment response and detect recurrence, NOT for initial screening (low sensitivity at early stage). The organ-specific tumour marker panel: CA15-3 = breast; CA-125 = ovarian; CA19-9 = pancreatic; CEA = colorectal; AFP = hepatocellular/germ cell; PSA = prostate. These should not be used interchangeably.
CA19.9 marker used for EXCEPT:

The EXCEPT is B (hepatocellular cancer). CA19-9 (sialylated Lewis blood group antigen) is a recognised tumour marker for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (primary and most sensitive clinical use), cholangiocarcinoma, and colorectal cancer. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) uses AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) as its primary tumour marker — AFP >400 ng/mL in a cirrhotic patient is diagnostic per AASLD criteria. While CA19-9 may be mildly elevated in various hepatic diseases, it is NOT a primary marker for HCC. Therefore 'Hepatocellular cancer' (B) is correctly excepted.
Ferritin used for:

Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific marker of total body iron stores. Low ferritin (<12 ng/mL) is virtually pathognomonic of iron deficiency and is the FIRST iron parameter to fall during progressive iron depletion, preceding changes in serum iron, transferrin saturation, TIBC, and MCV. In haemolytic anaemia, ferritin is elevated (iron released from lysed RBCs enters the storage pool). Ferritin plays no diagnostic role in B12 deficiency, which requires serum cobalamin, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine assays (Tietz Fundamentals of Clinical Chemistry, 8th ed.).
What is the following tests used for female infertility:

All three are essential components of the female infertility workup. FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone): elevated FSH indicates diminished ovarian reserve (poor ovarian response); assesses folliculogenesis. LH (Luteinising Hormone): detects ovulatory LH surge; elevated LH with high androgen/oestrogen ratio suggests PCOS; low LH suggests hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism. Progesterone: mid-luteal phase (Day 21) progesterone >3 ng/mL confirms ovulation. The complete panel also includes oestradiol (E2), AMH (anti-Müllerian hormone), prolactin, TSH, and antral follicle count (AFC) by ultrasound.
In liver dysfunction investigation what is the test used:

All three autoantibodies constitute the autoimmune liver disease diagnostic panel. ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) + ASMA (Anti-Smooth Muscle Antibodies, targeting F-actin) are the hallmark antibodies of Autoimmune Hepatitis Type 1 (AIH-1, >80% sensitivity). AMA (Anti-Mitochondrial Antibodies, specifically anti-M2 targeting pyruvate dehydrogenase complex) are the hallmark of Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) — sensitivity >95%, specificity >98%. Together they allow differentiation of AIH subtypes from PBC and from viral/drug-induced hepatitis (EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines).
Infection on the urinary tract infections (UTI) is most frequently associated with:

~80% of community-acquired UTIs are caused by Escherichia coli — a Gram-negative bacillus constituting normal intestinal (colonic) flora. It ascends from the perineum to colonise the urethra and bladder via type 1 and P-fimbriae adhesion to uroepithelium. Other intestinal Gram-negative flora causing UTIs: Klebsiella pneumoniae (~10%), Proteus mirabilis, Enterobacter spp., and Pseudomonas aeruginosa in healthcare-associated UTIs. Gram-positive Staphylococcus saprophyticus accounts for ~10–15% in young sexually active women (IDSA UTI Guidelines 2019).
According to gas requirements the most pathogenic bacteria for human is:

Facultative anaerobes are the most pathogenic bacteria for humans because their metabolic versatility allows them to survive and replicate in virtually ALL body compartments — both oxygenated tissues (blood, lungs, kidneys) and anaerobic environments (gut lumen, abscesses, necrotic tissue). The vast majority of clinically important pathogens are facultative anaerobes: E. coli, Salmonella, Shigella, Klebsiella, Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, Listeria, and Enterococcus. This dual metabolic capability is a major virulence attribute enabling systemic dissemination and multi-organ infection.
What is the following parasite pinworm:

Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm/threadworm/seatworm) is the causative agent of enterobiasis — the most common helminthic infection in developed countries. The adult female migrates nocturnally from the colon to the perianal area to deposit eggs, causing intense perianal pruritus (especially at night). Diagnosed by the adhesive tape (Scotch tape) test applied to the perianal area early morning. Ascaris lumbricoides = large intestinal roundworm; Ancylostoma duodenale = hookworm. Both are nematodes but NOT pinworms.
Pernicious anemia diagnosis by:

All three are diagnostic features of pernicious anaemia (PA). PA is an autoimmune condition where: (1) Antibodies against gastric parietal cells (anti-parietal cell Ab, ~90% sensitivity but low specificity) destroy intrinsic factor (IF)-producing cells; (2) Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (Type I blocking, ~60% sensitivity but >95% specific for PA) prevent IF from binding B12; (3) The resulting IF deficiency causes ileal B12 malabsorption → B12 deficiency → megaloblastic anaemia. Schilling test confirms the pathophysiology. All three elements are integral to PA diagnosis (Henry's Clinical Diagnosis, 24th ed.).
Which is the following for cardiac profile:

The cardiac biomarker profile includes all three. Troponin I/T (cTnI/cTnT): most sensitive and specific for myocardial injury — rises at 1–4 hours, peaks at 12–24 hours, remains elevated 7–14 days; gold standard for acute MI diagnosis (ESC/ACC/AHA guidelines). CK-MB (creatine kinase MB isoform): rises at 3–6 hours, peaks at 12–24 hours, normalises in 48–72 hours — useful for reinfarction detection. Myoglobin: rises earliest (1–3 hours post-MI) but is non-specific (also elevated in skeletal muscle injury); high negative predictive value when normal. LDH and AST are older markers now rarely used in acute settings.
Occult blood count test used for EXCEPT:

Fecal Occult Blood Test (FOBT/FIT) is a valid clinical tool for ALL three listed conditions: Hookworm infection (Ancylostoma duodenale/Necator americanus — attaches to intestinal mucosa causing chronic GI blood loss detectable by FOBT); Cancer (colorectal cancer screening — recommended by USPSTF/ACS from age 45, tumour surface bleeding detected by FOBT); Iron deficiency anaemia workup (identifying occult GI blood loss as the underlying aetiology). Since all three ARE valid indications for FOBT, there is no exception among the listed options — 'None of the above' (E) is the correct EXCEPT answer.
Used for TB diagnosis:

All three are established diagnostic methods for tuberculosis. Tuberculin skin test (TST/Mantoux/PPD): intradermal injection of purified protein derivative; induration ≥10 mm at 48–72 hours indicates TB exposure/infection (delayed-type hypersensitivity). Interferon-Gamma Release Assays (IGRAs: QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus, T-SPOT.TB): in-vitro blood tests measuring IFN-γ response to TB-specific antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10) — more specific than TST (not affected by BCG vaccination). PCR (Xpert MTB/RIF, NAAT): directly detects M. tuberculosis DNA and rifampicin resistance in sputum — WHO-recommended initial diagnostic test where available. All three are in current WHO/CDC TB diagnostic guidelines.
Rheumatoid arthritis diagnosis by:

Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) is diagnosed by: RF (Rheumatoid Factor) — an IgM autoantibody targeting the Fc region of IgG, present in ~70–80% of RA patients (seropositive RA); AND anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide) antibodies — high specificity (~95%) for RA, detecting earlier and more specifically than RF. ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) diagnoses post-streptococcal sequelae (rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis) — NOT RA. Anti-dsDNA (anti-double-stranded DNA) is specific for SLE, NOT RA. Since anti-CCP is not listed, RF (B) alone is the best answer. 'B and C' is wrong because anti-dsDNA is for SLE.
Diagnosis of syphilis:

All three methods are used for syphilis (Treponema pallidum) diagnosis. RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin): a non-treponemal flocculation test detecting anticardiolipin antibodies — used for screening and treatment monitoring (titre quantification). Latex agglutination tests: treponemal confirmatory tests (e.g., TPLA — Treponema pallidum latex agglutination) detect specific anti-treponemal antibodies. Immunochromatographic rapid tests (point-of-care): treponemal or combination (antigen + antibody) rapid diagnostic tests used at the bedside. Current CDC algorithm: non-treponemal screen (RPR/VDRL) → confirmed by treponemal test. All three have roles in the syphilis diagnostic pathway.
In diabetic patient the test belonging to profile is (duplicate Q5):

All three are components of the comprehensive diabetic monitoring profile: HbA1c reflects 2–3 month average glucose (long-term); PPBS (Post-Prandial Blood Sugar, 2-hour post-meal) assesses short-term postprandial glycaemic control; Fructosamine reflects 2–3 week average glucose (medium-term, useful when HbA1c is unreliable due to haemolytic anaemia, haemoglobinopathies, or pregnancy). Together they provide complete glycaemic timeline assessment (ADA Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes 2024).
Bence-Jones protein found in case of:

Bence-Jones proteins are free monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains (kappa or lambda) produced in excess by malignant plasma cells in Multiple Myeloma. Due to their low molecular weight (~22–44 kDa for monomers), they pass the glomerular filtration barrier into urine, where they are detected by urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP) or immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE). They may cause cast nephropathy ('myeloma kidney'). 'Acute myeloma' and 'chronic myeloid myeloma' are not recognised medical entities. Multiple myeloma is the only correct answer (WHO Classification of Haematological Malignancies 2022).
Decrease in reticulocyte count in EXCEPT:

The EXCEPT is C (Haemolytic anaemia). Haemolytic anaemia causes INCREASED (not decreased) reticulocyte count — the bone marrow dramatically increases RBC production to compensate for accelerated destruction, producing marked reticulocytosis (reticulocyte index >2–3%). In contrast, Vitamin B12 deficiency (megaloblastic, hypoproliferative), iron deficiency anaemia (insufficient substrate for haemoglobin synthesis), and the initial phase of acute post-haemorrhagic anaemia (before marrow compensation begins) all feature relatively low reticulocyte counts. Haemolytic anaemia is the textbook example of reactive reticulocytosis (Rodak's Hematology).
PCR principle depends on:

PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) is based exclusively on the exponential in-vitro amplification of specific nucleic acid (DNA) sequences using thermostable DNA polymerase (Taq polymerase), sequence-specific primers, deoxyribonucleotide triphosphates (dNTPs), and thermal cycling (denaturation at 94°C, annealing at 55–65°C, extension at 72°C). It is NOT based on antigen-antibody reactions (immunoassay principle) or DNA sequencing (though sequencing can follow PCR as a downstream application). Nucleic acid amplification (A) is the sole correct principle (Molecular Diagnostics, Jorgensen & Pfaller).
Anti-gliadin IgA diagnostic test:

Anti-gliadin IgA antibodies target gliadin — a component of gluten (the protein composite found in wheat, barley, and rye). They are serum markers of immune-mediated reactivity to dietary gluten, used in the diagnosis of celiac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy) and non-celiac gluten sensitivity. Current preferred serological tests for celiac disease: anti-tTG IgA (anti-tissue transglutaminase, most sensitive/specific), anti-EMA IgA (anti-endomysial), and anti-deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) IgG (in IgA-deficient patients). Confirmation requires duodenal biopsy (Marsh classification). Lactose and fructose are entirely unrelated to gluten/gliadin.
The factors of coagulation state EXCEPT:

All four listed proteins are authentic coagulation factors: Factor V (labile factor/accelerin — cofactor of Factor Xa in prothrombinase complex, common pathway); Factor VII (stable factor/proconvertin — initiates the extrinsic pathway by forming the TF-VIIa complex); Factor VIII (anti-haemophilic factor A — intrinsic tenase cofactor; deficiency causes Haemophilia A); Factor IX (Christmas factor/anti-haemophilic factor B — intrinsic pathway serine protease; deficiency causes Haemophilia B). Since all four ARE genuine coagulation factors, there is no exception among options A–D — 'None of the above' (E) is the correct EXCEPT answer.
RF used to diagnose:

RF (Rheumatoid Factor) is an autoantibody (predominantly IgM) directed against the Fc region of IgG. It is the principal serological marker for Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) — positive in ~70–80% of RA patients (seropositive RA). Anti-CCP antibodies are more specific. RF does NOT diagnose Rheumatic Fever — that requires ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) titre and clinical Jones criteria. The confusion between 'RF (Rheumatoid Factor)' and 'RF (Rheumatic Fever)' is a classic and common exam trap — RF the test = Rheumatoid Arthritis only.
Sickle cell anemia is caused by the following:

All three statements are scientifically accurate descriptions of sickle cell anaemia (SCA). (A) A single nucleotide substitution (GAG→GTG) at codon 6 of the HBB gene on chromosome 11. (B) This replaces hydrophilic glutamic acid (glutamate) with hydrophobic valine in the beta-globin chain — creating HbS, which polymerises under deoxygenation causing RBC sickling, vaso-occlusion, haemolysis, and organ damage. (C) Haemoglobin electrophoresis (alkaline pH: HbS migrates between HbA and HbA₂) and HPLC are the gold-standard diagnostic methods, demonstrating absent HbA and >90% HbS (HbSS genotype). All three = 'All the above' (D) (Rodak's Hematology, Henry's Clinical Diagnosis).
