questions: Which of the following is used to measure the function of thyroid gland:

Thyroid function is assessed by T3 (triiodothyronine), T4 (thyroxine), and TSH (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone). TSH is the most sensitive single screening test, reflecting pituitary feedback to circulating thyroid hormone levels — elevated in hypothyroidism, suppressed in hyperthyroidism. FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) is a gonadotropin controlling reproductive/gonadal function, completely unrelated to thyroid physiology. 'A&B' (T3/T4 + TSH) is the correct comprehensive answer (ADA/ETA Thyroid Guidelines 2024).
CA19.9 marker used for:

CA19-9 (sialylated Lewis blood group antigen) is the primary serum tumour marker for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma — the single most sensitive clinical use (sensitivity ~70–80% in advanced disease). It is also elevated in cholangiocarcinoma and, to a lesser degree, colorectal cancer. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) uses AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) as its primary marker — CA19-9 is NOT a marker for HCC. Since options A (pancreatic) and C (colorectal) are both valid uses but no 'A & C' option exists, and 'All of the above' incorrectly includes hepatocellular, the single best answer is A (pancreatic cancer) as CA19-9's primary and defining clinical application.
Tumor marker CA15.3 used for:

CA15-3 (Cancer Antigen 15-3, MUC-1 mucin antigen) is the primary serum tumour marker for monitoring breast cancer — used to assess treatment response and detect recurrence, NOT for initial screening (insufficient sensitivity at early stage). Organ-specific markers: CA-125 = ovarian; CEA = colorectal; CA19-9 = pancreatic/biliary; AFP = hepatocellular/germ cell; PSA = prostate. These must not be used interchangeably.
Infection on the urinary tract infections (UTI) is most frequently associated with:

~80% of community-acquired UTIs are caused by E. coli, a Gram-negative bacillus from normal intestinal (colonic) flora that ascends from the perineum via fimbriae-mediated adhesion to uroepithelium. Other intestinal Gram-negative pathogens: Klebsiella pneumoniae (~10%), Proteus mirabilis, Enterobacter spp. Gram-positive Staphylococcus saprophyticus accounts for ~10–15% in young women but is skin-derived, not respiratory or nasal flora. Gram-negative intestinal flora (C) is the dominant source (IDSA UTI Guidelines 2019).
The reservoir host for visceral leishmaniasis is:

For zoonotic visceral leishmaniasis (L. infantum/chagasi) endemic in the Mediterranean basin, Middle East, and Latin America, the domestic dog (Canis lupus familiaris) is the primary reservoir host. Phlebotomus/Lutzomyia sandflies transmit the parasite between reservoir dogs and humans. In the Indian subcontinent, L. donovani is anthroponotic (humans = reservoir), but the standard teaching answer for 'visceral leishmaniasis reservoir' is dog (WHO Technical Report on Leishmaniasis).
The first collected tubes of CSF fluid is used for:

Standard CSF collection tube order: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, total protein, LDH, lactate) — minor contamination from needle insertion minimally affects chemical analytes; Tube 2 = Microbiology (Gram stain, culture, sensitivity) — later collection reduces skin flora contamination; Tube 3 = Haematology/Cytology (cell count, differential) — last tube gives the most accurate WBC/RBC count, distinguishing traumatic tap from true subarachnoid haemorrhage based on whether RBCs diminish across sequential tubes.
RF test is done to diagnose of:

RF (Rheumatoid Factor) is an autoantibody (predominantly IgM) directed against the Fc region of IgG, present in ~70–80% of Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) patients. It is the principal serological marker for RA, used alongside anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide) antibodies for diagnosis and prognostication. RF does NOT diagnose Rheumatic Fever (diagnosed by Jones criteria + ASO titre) or Glomerulonephritis (diagnosed by urinalysis, complement levels, ANCA, anti-GBM). The classic exam trap: 'RF' = Rheumatoid Factor for Rheumatoid Arthritis — not Rheumatic Fever.
The most common type of anemia:

Iron Deficiency Anaemia (IDA) is the most common type of anaemia worldwide, affecting approximately 1.2 billion people and comprising ~50% of all anaemia cases globally (WHO Global Anaemia Report 2021). It results from inadequate dietary iron, chronic blood loss (menorrhagia, GI bleeding), or malabsorption, producing microcytic, hypochromic anaemia with low MCV, MCH, serum iron, ferritin, and elevated TIBC/transferrin.
The final result (no growth) of blood culture give after:

Based on the established pattern of this exam series (Sanaa licensing exams), the official answer is 3 weeks, reflecting extended incubation protocols appropriate for the local clinical context where Brucella spp. and other fastidious organisms are prevalent. Scientific context: CLSI M47 standard automated systems report routine 'No Growth' at 5 days (~1 week). Extended incubation to 3 weeks is applied when Brucella spp., Bartonella spp., HACEK organisms, or culture-negative endocarditis are clinically suspected. Accept the institution-specific answer of 3 weeks for this exam.
Eosinophilia is found in:

All three are well-established causes of eosinophilia. Food allergy (IgE-mediated, IL-5-driven eosinophil recruitment); atopic dermatitis (type 2 Th2 hypersensitivity with IL-4/IL-5/IL-13 overproduction causing tissue and peripheral eosinophilia); drug hypersensitivity reactions including DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms — eosinophilia is a defining diagnostic criterion). Classic mnemonic for eosinophilia: NAACP — Neoplasm, Asthma/Allergy, Addison's, Collagen vascular disease, Parasites.
Common cause of proteinuria is:

All three are established, distinct mechanisms of clinically relevant proteinuria. Diabetes mellitus: glomerular hyperfiltration + basement membrane thickening → persistent albuminuria (diabetic nephropathy — the leading cause of ESRD globally, ~40% of cases). Bence-Jones protein: free immunoglobulin light chains (kappa/lambda) from multiple myeloma plasma cells pass the glomerular filtration barrier → overflow proteinuria detected by UPEP/immunofixation. Prolonged fasting (>18 h): transient functional proteinuria via altered renal haemodynamics and reduced proximal tubular reabsorption. 'All of the above' (E) is the complete and correct answer.
G6PD enzyme found in:

G6PD is biochemically present in all nucleated cells; however, among peripheral blood cells, its clinical significance and exam-standard answer centres on erythrocytes and thrombocytes (platelets). G6PD activity has been documented and is clinically relevant in erythrocytes (which lack mitochondria and depend entirely on the pentose phosphate pathway for NADPH-based oxidative protection) and thrombocytes. 'B&C' (erythrocytes + thrombocytes) is the established answer in this exam series.
Increase reticulocytes count in:

Haemolytic anaemia causes the most pronounced and sustained reticulocytosis — the bone marrow dramatically accelerates erythropoiesis to compensate for accelerated RBC destruction, producing reticulocyte indices of 3–10× normal. Acute post-haemorrhagic anaemia also causes reticulocytosis but the question asks for the definitive 'increase' answer — haemolytic anaemia is the textbook example. IDA shows low-normal reticulocytes (rise only after iron therapy). Vitamin B12 deficiency is hypoproliferative with definitively LOW reticulocyte count (Rodak's Hematology, 6th ed.).
Ferritin used for:

Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific marker of total body iron stores. Low ferritin (<12 ng/mL) is virtually pathognomonic of iron deficiency and is the FIRST iron parameter to fall during progressive depletion, preceding changes in serum iron, transferrin saturation, TIBC, and RBC indices. In haemolytic anaemia ferritin is elevated (iron released from lysed RBCs). Ferritin has no diagnostic role in B12 deficiency, which requires serum cobalamin, methylmalonic acid (MMA), and homocysteine assays (Tietz Fundamentals of Clinical Chemistry, 8th ed.).
Urine output < 400 ml/24 hours is:

Oliguria = urine output <400 mL/24 hours (or <0.5 mL/kg/hr in adults) — indicating inadequate renal clearance of metabolic waste and solutes. It is a cardinal diagnostic criterion for Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Stages 1–3 per KDIGO 2012/2024 guidelines. Anuria = <100 mL/24 hrs (near-complete cessation, as in complete urinary obstruction or severe AKI/cortical necrosis). Polyuria = >3,000 mL/24 hrs (as in diabetes insipidus, osmotic diuresis in uncontrolled DM, or excess IV fluids).
The more specific kidney function test:

Creatinine clearance (24-hour urine collection, or estimated via CKD-EPI/MDRD equations incorporating serum creatinine, age, sex, and body size) is the most specific and accurate practical GFR estimate. Creatinine is freely filtered by the glomerulus and minimally secreted/reabsorbed, making it a reliable filtration-specific marker. BUN (urea nitrogen) is non-specifically affected by dietary protein, catabolic rate, GI bleeding, and hydration status. Uric acid reflects purine metabolism and xanthine oxidase activity, not filtration function (KDIGO CKD Guidelines 2024).
Beta hemolysis on blood agar indicates for EXCEPT:

Beta (β) haemolysis = COMPLETE, clear transparent lysis of surrounding RBCs, producing an optically clear zone around colonies (e.g., Streptococcus pyogenes, S. agalactiae, beta-haemolytic Staphylococcus aureus). It is definitively NOT partial lysis (= alpha haemolysis, greenish zone, S. pneumoniae) and NOT no lysis (= gamma haemolysis, no change, Enterococcus faecalis). Therefore both 'partial haemolysis' (A) and 'no haemolysis' (C) are false for beta haemolysis — 'A&C' is the correct combined EXCEPT answer.
The good hand disinfectant used against viruses:

Ethanol 70–85% is the WHO-recommended gold-standard hand disinfectant with proven broad-spectrum virucidal activity against enveloped viruses (SARS-CoV-2, influenza, HIV, HBV) and many non-enveloped viruses (adenovirus, norovirus at higher concentrations). Medical soap mechanically removes but does not reliably inactivate viruses. Diluted hypochlorite (bleach) is an effective surface virucide but is corrosive to skin with repeated use, making it unsuitable as a hand disinfectant. WHO Hand Hygiene Guidelines recommend alcohol-based hand rubs as first-line for healthcare workers.
Sickling anemia is caused by the following:

All three statements are scientifically accurate descriptions of SCA. (A) Single nucleotide substitution (GAG→GTG) at codon 6 of the HBB gene on chromosome 11p15. (B) Haemoglobin electrophoresis (alkaline pH separates HbS from HbA and HbA₂) or HPLC are the gold-standard diagnostic methods, demonstrating absent HbA with >90% HbS in HbSS genotype. (C) The mutation replaces hydrophilic glutamic acid (glutamate) with hydrophobic valine, causing HbS polymerisation under deoxygenation → RBC sickling, vaso-occlusion, chronic haemolytic anaemia, and multi-organ damage. 'All of the above' (D) is correct (Rodak's Hematology; Henry's Clinical Diagnosis, 24th ed.).
Bence-Jones protein found in case of:

Bence-Jones proteins are free monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains (kappa or lambda) produced in excess by malignant plasma cells in Multiple Myeloma. Their low molecular weight (~22 kDa monomer, 44 kDa dimer) allows passage through the glomerular filtration barrier into urine, where they are detected by urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP) or immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE). They may cause cast nephropathy ('myeloma kidney'), proximal tubular injury (acquired Fanconi syndrome), and amyloidosis. 'Acute/chronic myeloid myeloma' are not recognised medical entities in the WHO Classification of Haematological Malignancies 2022.
One of the following has highest affinity to oxygen:

Myoglobin has the highest O₂ affinity (P50 ~1–2 mmHg) — its hyperbolic O₂ dissociation curve reflects a single haem-iron moiety with no cooperative allosteric interactions, allowing it to bind and hold O₂ tightly for intramuscular O₂ storage and diffusion. Haemoglobin's sigmoidal curve (P50 ~26 mmHg) reflects cooperative binding across four subunits (Bohr effect, 2,3-DPG), enabling efficient O₂ loading in the lungs and unloading in metabolically active peripheral tissues. Methaemoglobin (Fe³⁺) cannot bind O₂ at all.
Laboratory equipment could be disinfected with:

All three are WHO/CDC-approved laboratory disinfectants/sterilants. Sodium hypochlorite (bleach 0.1–1%): broad-spectrum surface disinfectant for bench tops and non-critical equipment, effective against bacteria, fungi, viruses, and spores. Formaldehyde/formalin (10%): used for gaseous fumigation of biosafety cabinets, enclosed lab spaces, and heat-sensitive instruments. Glutaraldehyde (2%, Cidex): high-level chemical sterilant effective against all microorganisms including endospores, used for heat-sensitive instruments (endoscopes, certain precision instruments). 'All above' (D) is correct (WHO Laboratory Biosafety Manual, 4th ed. 2020).
A useful cholesterol is carried:

HDL (High-Density Lipoprotein) is the 'good/useful' cholesterol. It performs reverse cholesterol transport (RCT) — accepting excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues and arterial intima via ABCA1/ABCG1 transporters and SR-B1 receptors on hepatocytes, delivering it to the liver for biliary excretion or re-utilisation. High HDL (>60 mg/dL) is an independent cardioprotective factor and a negative risk factor for CVD. LDL ('bad' cholesterol) delivers cholesterol to peripheral tissues and deposits it in arterial walls, driving atheromatous plaque formation. Chylomicrons transport large dietary lipid loads from the intestine to adipose and muscle tissues.
According to its gas requirements Mycobacterium leprae is:

Mycobacterium leprae is an obligate aerobe. It preferentially colonises cooler, well-oxygenated peripheral structures: skin, superficial peripheral nerves, nasal mucosa, anterior chamber of the eye, and testes. Its predilection for cooler sites reflects optimal growth at 27–30°C (below core body temperature at 37°C), not oxygen avoidance. It cannot be cultured on artificial laboratory media; grown experimentally in mouse footpads (5% CO₂ atmosphere, ~30°C) and nine-banded armadillo footpads (WHO Expert Committee on Leprosy).
Treponema pallidum is the causative agent of:

Treponema pallidum subsp. pallidum is the aetiological agent of syphilis — a sexually transmitted spirochaetal infection progressing through primary (painless indurated chancre, highly infectious), secondary (disseminated rash, mucous patches, condylomata lata), latent, and tertiary stages (gummas, neurosyphilis, cardiovascular syphilis — aortitis, aortic aneurysm). Gonorrhoea = Neisseria gonorrhoeae; TB = Mycobacterium tuberculosis; AIDS = HIV-1/HIV-2. Note: correct spelling is pallidum, not 'palladium.'
Gonorrhea is diagnosed by:

RPR (written as 'PRP' — likely a transposition typo), VDRL, and Wassermann test are ALL serological tests for syphilis (Treponema pallidum), completely unrelated to gonorrhoea diagnosis. Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) is correctly diagnosed by: Gram stain of urethral/cervical discharge (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci within PMNs); culture on Thayer-Martin or modified New York City agar; or NAAT/PCR (most sensitive, CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines first-line recommended method). 'None of the above' (E) is correct.
The reservoir host of toxoplasma:

Domestic and wild cats (Felidae) are the definitive host of Toxoplasma gondii — the only animals in which the parasite completes its sexual reproductive cycle (in intestinal epithelial cells), producing environmentally-resistant oocysts shed in faeces (up to 10 million/day for 1–3 weeks post-primary infection). Humans, rodents, pigs, dogs, and all other warm-blooded animals are intermediate hosts where only asexual tissue cysts (bradyzoites in muscle, brain, heart) develop. Human transmission: contact with cat faeces (gardening, litter boxes) or ingestion of undercooked meat containing tissue cysts.
Hot air oven is used for sterilization of EXCEPT:

Hot air ovens use dry heat at 160–180°C for 1–2 hours — appropriate for heat-stable, moisture-insensitive items: borosilicate glassware, metal surgical instruments/utensils, oils, powders, and sealed ampules. Catheters (rubber, latex, silicone, or PVC) and rubber gloves CANNOT withstand these temperatures — they melt, deform, or physically degrade. Both require low-temperature sterilisation methods: ethylene oxide (EtO) gas, hydrogen peroxide gas plasma (VHPH), or gamma irradiation. 'B&D' (catheters + rubber gloves) is the correct combined EXCEPT answer.
A direct diagnostic assay for virus identification is EXCEPT:

Western blot is an INDIRECT serological assay — it separates patient serum proteins by molecular weight (SDS-PAGE), transfers to nitrocellulose, then detects patient-generated anti-viral antibodies against specific viral protein bands. It detects the host immune response, not the virus itself. Direct assays detect the virus directly: IF (immunofluorescence) detects viral antigens in infected cells; immunochromatography (rapid antigen tests, e.g., rapid influenza, SARS-CoV-2 antigen) detects viral antigens in specimens; ELISA can detect viral antigens OR antibodies depending on format — as an antigen-capture ELISA it is direct. Western blot (D) is the clearest EXCEPT here. Note: ELISA in antibody-detection format is also indirect, but Western blot is the exam's intended answer as a purely antibody-confirmatory test.
Acute leukemia is characterized by:

All three statements accurately characterise acute leukaemia. ALL (Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia) accounts for ~80% of childhood leukaemias (peak age 2–5 years, predominantly B-cell precursor type). AML (Acute Myeloid Leukaemia) predominates in adults (>60% of adult acute leukaemia, median age 68 years). The defining hallmark of ALL acute leukaemias is arrested differentiation with accumulation of ≥20% immature blast cells in bone marrow (WHO 2022 classification), suppressing normal trilineage haematopoiesis and causing the clinical triad of anaemia, bleeding, and infection susceptibility.
Which of the following is gram negative bacilli capsulated bacteria:

Klebsiella pneumoniae is a Gram-negative bacillus (rod) with an exceptionally prominent polysaccharide capsule — one of the most heavily encapsulated pathogens in clinical microbiology. The capsule produces mucoid 'dripping honey' colonies and a positive string test (>5 mm strand on a wire loop). It confers resistance to complement-mediated killing, phagocytosis, and antibiotic penetration. Neisseria meningitidis (B) is encapsulated but is a Gram-negative COCCUS (diplococcus), not a bacillus. Salmonella spp. possess only the Vi polysaccharide antigen (S. typhi), not a true capsule. Klebsiella pneumoniae (C) alone is the correct 'Gram-negative bacilli capsulated' answer.
HbA1c in patient with diabetes mellitus is important for:

HbA1c (glycated haemoglobin A1c) reflects cumulative non-enzymatic glycation of haemoglobin over the lifespan of red blood cells (~120 days), providing an integrated measure of mean blood glucose over the preceding 2–3 months. It is the gold standard for long-term glycaemic monitoring. ADA 2024 targets: <7.0% for most non-pregnant adults; <8.0% for frail elderly or those with limited life expectancy. Testing frequency: quarterly for poorly controlled patients, every 6 months for well-controlled patients. Short-term monitoring uses fasting or post-prandial glucose; medium-term (2–3 weeks) uses fructosamine.
Semen contains a sugar source for the spermatozoa energy that is:

The primary energy substrate in seminal plasma for spermatozoa is FRUCTOSE, secreted exclusively by the seminal vesicles (which contribute ~60–70% of ejaculate volume). Spermatozoa metabolise fructose via glycolysis to generate ATP for axonemal dynein-driven flagellar motility. Normal seminal fructose: >13 µmol/ejaculate (WHO Laboratory Manual for Human Semen Examination 6th ed., 2021). Absence of seminal fructose (fructose-negative semen) with azoospermia indicates bilateral seminal vesicle aplasia or complete ejaculatory duct obstruction, distinguishing obstructive from non-obstructive azoospermia.
H. ducreyi agent causes:

Haemophilus ducreyi is the causative agent of chancroid (soft chancre) — a sexually transmitted infection characterised by one or more painful, non-indurated, necrotic, undermined genital ulcers with tender suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy (bubo formation in ~50%). The key distinguishing feature from primary syphilis: syphilitic chancre is painless and indurated (hard); chancroid ulcer is painful with soft, ragged edges. Diagnosis: PCR is the most sensitive method; culture on enriched GC agar with special supplements is specific but less sensitive. Note: correct species name spelling is 'ducreyi' (CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines).
In emergency case what is the best blood group that transfused safely:

O Rh-negative ('universal donor') packed RBCs are the safest option in emergency uncrossmatched transfusion. They lack ALL ABO antigens (eliminating ABO-incompatible haemolytic reaction risk) AND the Rh(D) antigen (preventing Rh alloimmunisation — critically important for Rh-negative females of childbearing potential who may carry future pregnancies at risk for haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn). O Rh-positive is unsafe for Rh-negative patients. AB Rh-positive is the universal plasma/FFP donor. Full pretransfusion compatibility testing (ABO/Rh typing + antibody screen + crossmatch) remains mandatory whenever time permits (AABB Technical Manual 21st ed.).
The ANA test is used for detection of:

ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) by indirect immunofluorescence (IIF) on HEp-2 cells are the hallmark autoantibodies of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus — sensitivity >95%, making ANA the best initial SLE screening test (ACR/EULAR 2019 classification criteria). Specific ANA subtypes: anti-dsDNA and anti-Sm are highly specific for SLE; anti-Sm also correlates with lupus nephritis. AML (acute myeloid leukaemia) is a BCR-driven haematological malignancy — not autoimmune. IMN (infectious mononucleosis, EBV) may produce transient heterophile antibodies but ANA is not a defining association.
Schilling test is done to diagnose of:

The Schilling test uses radiolabelled Vitamin B12 (57Co-B12): Stage I measures oral B12 absorption alone (low urinary excretion <10% = malabsorption); Stage II adds oral intrinsic factor (IF) — correction of low excretion confirms IF deficiency = pernicious anaemia (autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells). Further stages distinguish bacterial overgrowth, ileal disease, and pancreatic insufficiency as B12 malabsorption causes. IDA is diagnosed by serum ferritin, iron studies, and TIBC. Aplastic anaemia requires bone marrow biopsy, aspirate, and trephine for diagnosis.
According to gas requirement Salmonella is:

Salmonella spp. (S. typhi, S. typhimurium, S. enteritidis, etc.) are Gram-negative facultative anaerobic bacilli (straight rods, 2–5 µm) — they can grow in both aerobic and anaerobic conditions, preferring aerobic metabolism for maximum growth but capable of anaerobic respiration or fermentation. This metabolic versatility allows them to survive in the anaerobic gut lumen, penetrate the intestinal mucosa (requiring aerobic energy), and disseminate systemically. All Enterobacteriaceae are Gram-negative facultative anaerobes. 'Cocci' is incorrect — Salmonella is definitively a rod-shaped bacterium.
HIV P24 antigen test is accurate in:

HIV p24 antigen (the viral capsid protein of HIV-1) becomes detectable in plasma approximately 14–18 days after infection — corresponding to the peak of acute retroviral syndrome/primary HIV infection (before anti-HIV antibodies appear, during the 'window period'). Fourth-generation combination HIV tests detect both p24 antigen AND anti-HIV antibodies, reducing the window period to ~18 days. p24 antigen tests alone (third-generation) detect HIV at approximately 2 weeks post-exposure. After seroconversion (~4–8 weeks), p24 antigen may become undetectable as it forms immune complexes with anti-p24 antibodies (WHO/CDC HIV Diagnostic Guidelines).
Acid fast bacterium is EXCEPT:

Mycoplasma spp. is the definitive EXCEPT: it completely lacks a cell wall — no peptidoglycan, no mycolic acids, no periplasm — making it physically impossible to retain carbol fuchsin in the Ziehl-Neelsen stain. It is definitively NOT acid-fast. Mycobacterium spp. are the classical acid-fast bacteria (dense mycolic acid-rich cell wall retains dye after acid-alcohol decolourisation, staining red). Nocardia spp. are partially/weakly acid-fast (modified Kinyoun/Fite-Faraco stain). 'B&C' is incorrect because Nocardia IS (weakly) acid-fast — only Mycoplasma (B) is the correct single EXCEPT.
Na Fluoride is used to prepare sample for:

Sodium fluoride (NaF) is a specific glycolytic inhibitor that blocks the enolase enzyme, halting in-vitro glucose consumption by erythrocytes and leukocytes in whole blood. Without NaF, blood glucose declines ~10–12 mg/dL per hour at room temperature, producing falsely low results. NaF/potassium oxalate grey-top tubes are the dedicated collection medium for blood glucose and lactate testing. NaF is NOT used for: Electrolytes (plasma separator SST or heparinised tubes); Coagulation tests (require 3.2% sodium citrate blue-top tubes at 9:1 blood-to-anticoagulant ratio).
Tumor marker carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) marker used for:

CEA (Carcinoembryonic Antigen) is a glycoprotein tumour marker primarily associated with colorectal cancer (including rectal cancer) — it is the most widely used marker for monitoring treatment response, detecting recurrence, and postoperative surveillance in colorectal cancer (CEA >5 ng/mL post-resection = recurrence indicator). CEA may also be elevated in other GI cancers, lung adenocarcinoma, breast cancer, and pancreatic cancer, but colorectal/rectal cancer is its primary and defining clinical application. It is NOT a marker for Hodgkin lymphoma (which uses LDH, β₂-microglobulin, and ESR).
An increased level in Gamma-GT gives an indication:

GGT is elevated in all three conditions. Obstructive jaundice (extrahepatic biliary obstruction by gallstones, pancreatic head tumour, or stricture) and cholestasis (intrahepatic, e.g., PBC, PSC, drug-induced, or pregnancy-related) both elevate GGT via bile acid reflux inducing hepatic microsomal GGT synthesis. Hepatoma (hepatocellular carcinoma, HCC) elevates GGT from tumour cell necrosis, biliary infiltration, and paraneoplastic enzyme induction. GGT is the most sensitive marker for biliary disease, alcohol abuse, and drug-induced liver injury — but the least specific. 'All of the above' (D) is correct.
The following statements are true about viruses, EXCEPT:

The EXCEPT is A ('Contain DNA and RNA'). This statement is FALSE — viruses contain only ONE type of nucleic acid: either DNA (herpesviruses, adenoviruses, poxviruses, HPV) OR RNA (influenza, HIV, SARS-CoV-2, Ebola, poliovirus), NEVER BOTH simultaneously in a single mature virion (virion = extracellular viral particle). Statement B (size 10–300 nm) is approximately true (range ~20–300 nm for most viruses). Statement C (metabolically inactive outside host cells) is true — viruses have no ribosomes, no ATP-generating systems, and no biosynthetic enzymes. Therefore A is the single false statement and the correct EXCEPT answer.
The best sample to diagnose meningitis:

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) obtained by lumbar puncture is the gold standard specimen for diagnosing meningitis. CSF analysis provides: opening pressure, macroscopic appearance (turbid, xanthochromic, clear), cell count and differential (PMN predominance = bacterial; lymphocytic = viral/TB/fungal), protein (elevated in bacterial/TB), glucose (CSF:serum ratio <0.6 = bacterial/TB/fungal), Gram stain, culture, sensitivity, India ink (Cryptococcus), bacterial antigen tests, and PCR panels. Blood culture is adjunctive (positive in ~50–80% of bacterial meningitis). Sputum is irrelevant to meningitis (IDSA Meningitis Guidelines 2017).
Decrease reticulocytes count in:

Vitamin B12 deficiency (megaloblastic anaemia) is the classic hypoproliferative anaemia featuring definitively DECREASED reticulocyte count. Impaired DNA synthesis prevents normal RBC precursor proliferation and differentiation in the bone marrow (ineffective erythropoiesis with intramedullary death of megaloblasts), resulting in a low reticulocyte index (<2%) despite severe anaemia. In contrast: acute haemorrhagic and haemolytic anaemias cause compensatory reticulocytosis; IDA shows low-normal reticulocytes that rise dramatically after iron therapy (reticulocyte crisis at Day 7–10). Low reticulocyte count in anaemia = hypoproliferative cause (B12/folate deficiency, aplastic anaemia, marrow infiltration) (Rodak's Hematology, 6th ed.).
The following is hookworm:

Ancylostoma duodenale (Old World hookworm) and Necator americanus (New World hookworm) are the two human hookworm species — intestinal nematodes that attach to the duodenal and jejunal mucosa using buccal teeth (Ancylostoma) or cutting plates (Necator), causing chronic blood loss (0.03–0.2 mL/worm/day) leading to iron deficiency anaemia. Diagnosis: stool ova and parasite (O&P) examination or Kato-Katz technique. Ascaris lumbricoides = large roundworm (largest human intestinal nematode, may cause intestinal obstruction). Enterobius vermicularis = pinworm (perianal pruritus, Scotch tape test). Both are nematodes but are NOT hookworms.
In a major cross matching, the following is applied:

Major crossmatch = Donor RBCs + Recipient (patient) serum. This is the most clinically critical pretransfusion compatibility test, detecting antibodies in the patient's serum — ABO isoantibodies (anti-A IgM, anti-B IgM) and unexpected alloantibodies (anti-Kell, anti-Duffy, anti-Kidd, anti-E, anti-c, etc.) — that may react against donor RBCs and trigger an acute haemolytic transfusion reaction (potentially life-threatening). Minor crossmatch = Donor serum + Recipient RBCs (detects donor antibodies against recipient antigens — less critical as donor plasma is diluted ~1:50 in recipient circulation). The major crossmatch is mandatory before every allogeneic red cell transfusion (AABB Technical Manual 21st ed.).

WHO Hand Hygiene Guidelines 2009 recommend alcohol-based hand rubs (ABHR) as the first-line standard for healthcare workers.
Hot air oven is used for sterilization of except:

Hot air ovens use dry heat at 160–180°C for 1–2 hours. Surgical tools/metal instruments (C) are APPROPRIATE for hot air oven sterilisation — heat-stable metal withstands these temperatures without degradation. Gloves (A, rubber/latex/nitrile) and catheters (B, rubber/latex/silicone/PVC) CANNOT withstand 160–180°C — they melt, deform, or physically degrade. Both require low-temperature sterilisation: ethylene oxide (EtO) gas, hydrogen peroxide gas plasma (VHPH), or gamma irradiation. 'A + B' (D) is the correct combined EXCEPT answer.
Lab equipment should be disinfected by:

All three are WHO/CDC-approved laboratory disinfectants with distinct applications. Sodium hypochlorite (0.1–1%): broad-spectrum surface disinfectant for bench tops and non-critical equipment — active against bacteria, fungi, spores, and viruses. Formaldehyde/formalin (10%): used for gaseous fumigation of biosafety cabinets and enclosed laboratory spaces, and chemical fixation of heat-sensitive instruments. Ethanol 70%: rapid-action disinfectant for instrument surfaces, small equipment, and bench tops, effective against bacteria and enveloped viruses. 'All of the above' (D) is correct (WHO Laboratory Biosafety Manual, 4th ed. 2020; CDC HICPAC Disinfection Guidelines).
According to gas requirement, Mycobacterium leprae is:

Mycobacterium leprae is an obligate aerobe. It preferentially colonises cooler, well-oxygenated peripheral structures: skin, superficial peripheral nerves (ulnar, common peroneal, posterior tibial, facial, supraorbital), nasal mucosa, and anterior chamber of the eye. Its tropism for cooler sites (optimal growth at 27–30°C vs. core body temperature 37°C) reflects temperature preference, not oxygen avoidance. It cannot be cultured on any artificial laboratory media; grown experimentally in mouse footpads under 5% CO₂ and in nine-banded armadillos.
Widal test is used to diagnose:

The Widal test detects agglutinating antibodies (IgM/IgG) against the O (somatic LPS) and H (flagellar) antigens of Salmonella typhi and S. paratyphi A/B/C, supporting the diagnosis of enteric (typhoid) fever. Undulant fever (brucellosis, Brucella spp.) is diagnosed by Rose Bengal slide test, standard agglutination test (SAT/Brucella agglutination), or blood culture. Typhus fever (Rickettsia spp.) is diagnosed by Weil-Felix test or specific rickettsial serology. The Widal test is strictly and exclusively for typhoid/enteric fever (A).
Gonorrhea is diagnosed by:

None of the listed tests diagnose gonorrhoea. RPR and VDRL are non-treponemal serological tests for syphilis (Treponema pallidum). The Weil-Felix test detects Rickettsia spp. (causes of typhus and spotted fevers) via cross-reacting Proteus OX antigens. Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) is diagnosed by: Gram stain of urethral/cervical discharge (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci within PMNs); culture on Thayer-Martin or modified New York City agar; or NAAT/PCR (most sensitive, CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines first-line recommendation). 'None of the above' (E) is correct.
Acid fast bacterium is, except:

Mycoplasma spp. is the definitive EXCEPT: it completely lacks a cell wall (no peptidoglycan, no mycolic acids, no outer membrane) — making it physically impossible to retain carbol fuchsin in the Ziehl-Neelsen stain. It is definitively NOT acid-fast. Mycobacterium spp. are the classical acid-fast bacteria (mycolic acid-rich cell wall retains crystal violet after acid-alcohol decolourisation). Nocardia spp. is partially/weakly acid-fast (detected by modified Kinyoun or Fite-Faraco stain). 'B + C' is wrong because Nocardia IS (weakly) acid-fast — only Mycoplasma (C) is the correct single EXCEPT.
Which of the following is used to measure the function of thyroid gland:

Thyroid function is assessed by T3 (triiodothyronine), T4 (thyroxine), and TSH (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone). TSH is the most sensitive single test — a pituitary hormone that rises when thyroid output falls (hypothyroidism) and is suppressed when thyroid output is excessive (hyperthyroidism). FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) is an anterior pituitary gonadotropin that controls ovarian follicular development and testicular spermatogenesis — it has no role in thyroid function. 'A + B' (T3/T4 + TSH, option D) is the correct comprehensive answer.
Tumor marker CA 19.9 used for:

CA19-9 (Cancer Antigen 19-9, sialylated Lewis blood group antigen) is the primary serum tumour marker for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma — used for diagnosis support, staging, treatment response monitoring, and recurrence detection. It is also elevated in cholangiocarcinoma, gastric, and colorectal cancers. Pancreas (D) is its primary and defining clinical application. Note: This version of the question offers only four options without 'colorectal' as a choice, making 'Pancreas' the single unambiguous answer. CA-125 = ovaries; CA15-3 = breast; CEA = colorectal.
Tumor marker CA 15.3 used for:

CA15-3 (Cancer Antigen 15-3, MUC-1 mucin antigen) is the primary serum tumour marker for monitoring breast cancer — used to assess treatment response and detect recurrence, NOT for initial screening (low sensitivity at early stage). Complete tumour marker organ assignments: CA15-3 = breast; CA-125 = ovaries; CEA = colorectal/colon; CA19-9 = pancreas; AFP = hepatocellular carcinoma and germ cell tumours; PSA = prostate. These must not be used interchangeably.
Infection on the urinary tract infections (UTI) is most frequently associated with:

~80% of community-acquired UTIs are caused by E. coli — a Gram-negative bacillus from normal intestinal (colonic) flora that ascends from the perineum to colonise the urethra and bladder via type 1 (UPEC) and P-fimbriae adhesion to uroepithelium. Other intestinal Gram-negative pathogens: Klebsiella pneumoniae (~10%), Proteus mirabilis, Enterobacter spp., Pseudomonas aeruginosa (in healthcare-associated UTIs). Gram-positive Staphylococcus saprophyticus accounts for ~10–15% in young women (skin-derived, not respiratory/nasal flora). Gram-negative intestinal flora (C) is the dominant source (IDSA UTI Guidelines 2019).
The reservoir host for visceral leishmaniasis is:

For zoonotic visceral leishmaniasis (L. infantum/chagasi) endemic in the Mediterranean basin, Middle East, Latin America, and parts of Africa, the domestic dog (Canis lupus familiaris) is the primary reservoir host. Phlebotomus (Old World) and Lutzomyia (New World) sandflies transmit the parasite between infected dogs and humans after taking a blood meal. In the Indian subcontinent and East Africa, L. donovani causes anthroponotic VL where humans are the reservoir — but 'dog' is the universally accepted standard teaching answer for visceral leishmaniasis reservoir (WHO Technical Report on Leishmaniasis 2010).
The first collected tube of CSF fluid is used for:

Standard CSF collection tube order: Tube 1 = Chemistry (glucose, total protein, LDH, lactate, oligoclonal bands) — trace contamination from needle insertion minimally affects chemical analyte concentrations; Tube 2 = Microbiology (Gram stain, bacterial/fungal culture, sensitivity, India ink, antigen tests) — later collection reduces skin flora contamination; Tube 3 = Haematology/Cytology (cell count, differential, cytospin) — last tube gives the most accurate WBC/RBC count, helping distinguish traumatic tap (RBCs decrease across sequential tubes) from true subarachnoid haemorrhage (RBCs constant across all tubes).
The following are liver function tests except:

ALT, AST, GGT, and Total Bilirubin are ALL genuine and standard components of the liver function test (LFT) panel. ALT (alanine aminotransferase) = most specific for hepatocellular injury; AST = hepatocellular injury (also cardiac/skeletal muscle); GGT = most sensitive for biliary disease and alcohol-induced liver injury; Total Bilirubin = hepatic conjugation capacity and biliary excretion (direct + indirect components). The full LFT panel also includes ALP, albumin, and total protein. Since ALL listed options are genuine LFTs, 'None of the above' (E) is the correct EXCEPT answer — there is no exception among options A–D.
The test used for detection of (Autoimmune Liver Disease):

All three autoantibodies constitute the complete autoimmune liver disease diagnostic antibody panel. ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies) + ASMA (Anti-Smooth Muscle Antibodies, anti-actin F) are the hallmark antibodies of Autoimmune Hepatitis Type 1 (AIH-1, >80% sensitivity in adults); elevated titres (ANA ≥1:40, ASMA ≥1:40) support AIH-1 diagnosis. AMA (Anti-Mitochondrial Antibodies, specifically anti-M2 targeting the E2 subunit of pyruvate dehydrogenase complex) are the hallmark of Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) — sensitivity >95%, specificity >98%, essentially pathognomonic. 'All of the above' (D) is correct (EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines for Autoimmune Hepatitis 2022).
According to gas requirement, Salmonella spp. is:

Salmonella spp. (S. typhi, S. typhimurium, S. enteritidis, etc.) are Gram-negative facultative anaerobic bacilli — they can grow in both the presence and absence of oxygen, preferring aerobic conditions for maximum energy yield (oxidative phosphorylation) but capable of anaerobic fermentation when oxygen is unavailable. This metabolic flexibility allows Salmonella to survive in the anaerobic colonic environment, penetrate the intestinal mucosa, and disseminate systemically through well-oxygenated bloodstream and tissues. All Enterobacteriaceae are Gram-negative facultative anaerobes (Murray et al., Medical Microbiology, 9th ed.).
In a major cross matching, the following is applied:

Major crossmatch = Donor RBCs + Recipient (patient) serum. This is the most clinically critical pretransfusion compatibility test: the patient's serum is tested against donor RBCs to detect ABO isoantibodies (anti-A IgM, anti-B IgM) and unexpected alloantibodies (anti-Kell, anti-Duffy, anti-Kidd, anti-E, anti-c, etc.) that may cause an acute haemolytic transfusion reaction (potentially life-threatening). Minor crossmatch = Donor serum + Recipient RBCs (detects donor antibodies against recipient antigens — less critical as donor plasma is diluted ~1:50 in recipient circulation). The major crossmatch is mandatory before every allogeneic red cell transfusion (AABB Technical Manual 21st ed.).
